question_text: Review: curves and tangents in R³: Consider the following parametric equation in Cartesian coordinates: v(λ) = (sin(λ²), ln(λ+1), 2λ²-1). Compute the tangent vector to this curve at any value of λ.
solution_text: dv/dλ = (2λcos(λ²), 1/(λ+1), 4λ)

question_text: Review: curves and tangents in R³: Consider the following parametric equation in Cartesian coordinates: v(λ) = (sin(λ²), ln(λ+1), 2λ²-1). Transform v(λ) to spherical coordinates in the range 0<λ<1.
solution_text: r = (vₓ² + vᵧ² + vᵤ²)^(1/2) = [sin²(λ²)+ln²(λ+1)+(2λ²-1)²]^(1/2), θ = cos⁻¹(vᵤ/r) = cos⁻¹((2λ²-1)/[sin²(λ²)+ln²(λ+1)+(2λ²-1)²]^(1/2)), φ = tan⁻¹(vᵧ/vₓ) = tan⁻¹(ln(λ+1)/sin(λ²))

question_text: Review: curves and tangents in R³: Consider the following parametric equation in Cartesian coordinates: v(λ) = (sin(λ²), ln(λ+1), 2λ²-1). Compute v(λ)·v(λ).
solution_text: v·v = |v|² = sin²(λ²)+ln²(λ+1)+(2λ²-1)²

question_text: Review: curves and tangents in R³: Consider the following parametric equation in Cartesian coordinates: v(λ) = (sin(λ²), ln(λ+1), 2λ²-1). Compute v(λ)×v'(λ) in your choice of coordinates.
solution_text: v×v' = (4λln(λ+1)-(2λ²-1)/(λ+1), (2λ²-1)cos(λ²)2λ-4λsin(λ²), sin(λ²)/(λ+1)-ln(λ+1)cos(λ²)2λ)

question_text: Review: Short Problems: As always, make sure you show your work (and do not use software) to get credit. Chain rule: let f(x(λ),y(λ),λ) = λ² + sin(x(λ)) + y(λ)/x(λ). Compute ∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y and df/dλ.
solution_text: ∂f/∂x = cos(x(λ)) - y(λ)/x²(λ), ∂f/∂y = 1/x(λ), ∂f/∂λ = cos(x(λ))∂x/∂λ - y(λ)/x²(λ)∂x/∂λ + 1/x(λ)∂y/∂λ + 2λ

question_text: Review: Short Problems: As always, make sure you show your work (and do not use software) to get credit. Taylor expansion: Taylor expand the following expression around x=0 up to and including O(x²): 1/(1-x²)^(1/3).

Review: Short Problems: As always, make sure you show your work (and do not use software) to get credit. Differential equation: Solve the following differential equation: f''(x) - 2f(x) = 0 and write your solution in terms of the hyperbolic trig functions sinh and cosh.
solution_text: f(x) = Asinh(√2x) + Bcosh(√2x)

question_text: Review: Rotation Matrix: Given a point (x₀,y₀,z₀) in Cartesian coordinates in R³, we would like to rotate that point an angle θ about the origin in the x-y plane. Write down the new coordinates of the point after this rotation.
solution_text: x' = x₀cosθ - y₀sinθ, y' = x₀sinθ + y₀cosθ, z' = z₀

question_text: Review: Rotation Matrix: Given a point (x₀,y₀,z₀) in Cartesian coordinates in R³, we would like to rotate that point an angle θ about the origin in the x-y plane. Show that the relation can be written as a linear algebra equation X' = R[θ] · X where X = (x₀,y₀,z₀), X' is the vector you obtained in part (a), and R[θ] is a matrix that you will need to write down.
solution_text: Let R[θ] = [[cosθ, -sinθ, 0], [sinθ, cosθ, 0], [0, 0, 1]], then it is easy to check that X' = R[θ]·X, where X' = [x₀', y₀', z₀']ᵀ and X = [x₀, y₀, z₀]ᵀ

question_text: Review: Matrix Multiplication: Given two square matrices [M] and [N] with entries M₁₁, M₁₂, M₁₃, M₂₁, M₂₂, M₂₃, M₃₁, M₃₂, M₃₃ and N₁₁, N₁₂, N₁₃, N₂₁, N₂₂, N₂₃, N₃₁, N₃₂, N₃₃, a row vector v = (v₁ v₂ v₃) and a column vector u = [u₁, u₂, u₃]ᵀ, write down [M]·[N] using the Σ sum notation.
solution_text: Let [A] = [M]·[N]. Then Aᵢⱼ = Σₖ MᵢₖNₖⱼ

question_text: Review: Matrix Multiplication: Given two square matrices [M] and [N] with entries M₁₁, M₁₂, M₁₃, M₂₁, M₂₂, M₂₃, M₃₁, M₃₂, M₃₃ and N₁₁, N₁₂, N₁₃, N₂₁, N₂₂, N₂₃, N₃₁, N₃₂, N₃₃, a row vector v = (v₁ v₂ v₃) and a column vector u = [u₁, u₂, u₃]ᵀ, write down [N]·[M] using the Σ sum notation.
solution_text: Let [B] = [N]·[M]. Then Bᵢⱼ = Σₖ NᵢₖMₖⱼ

question_text: Review: Matrix Multiplication: Given two square matrices [M] and [N] with entries M₁₁, M₁₂, M₁₃, M₂₁, M₂₂, M₂₃, M₃₁, M₃₂, M₃₃ and N₁₁, N₁₂, N₁₃, N₂₁, N₂₂, N₂₃, N₃₁, N₃₂, N₃₃, a row vector v = (v₁ v₂ v₃) and a column vector u = [u₁, u₂, u₃]ᵀ, write down v·[M]·u using the Σ sum notation.
solution_text: v·[M]·u = Σⱼ Σᵢ vⱼMⱼᵢuᵢ

question_text: Review: Matrix Multiplication: Given two square matrices [M] and [N] with entries M₁₁, M₁₂, M₁₃, M₂₁, M₂₂, M₂₃, M₃₁, M₃₂, M₃₃ and N₁₁, N₁₂, N₁₃, N₂₁, N₂₂, N₂₃, N₃₁, N₃₂, N₃₃, a row vector v = (v₁ v₂ v₃) and a column vector u = [u₁, u₂, u₃]ᵀ, write down v·u using the Σ sum notation.
solution_text: v·u = Σᵢ vᵢuᵢ

question_text: (20 points) Using Galilean relativity, show using equations that both Newton's second law F = ma and Newton's law of gravitation F = -Gm₁m₂r̂/r² are consistent with the Principle of Relativity (that is, that reference frames with constant relative velocity between them agree on both laws). For this you need only transform ma and -Gm₁m₂r̂/r².
solution_text: Suppose an observer A has a device that can apply a constant force F_A on a test mass m. Since Newton's second law holds in A's frame, we have F_A = ma_A, where a_A is the acceleration of the test mass observed by A. Now to check that Newton's second law is consistent with the Principle of Relativity, let's consider an observer B who is moving along the x-axis (choosing the x-axis such that x̂ = â_A) with a velocity +v relative to A. Since the force exerted by the device on the test mass is independent of the reference frame, we just need to check that F_A is still equal to ma_B, where a_B is the acceleration of the test mass observed by B. Using Galilean relativity that relates x_B = x_A - vt_A and t_B = t_A, we find that a_B = d²x_B/dt_B² = d²(x_A-vt_A)/dt_A² = d/dt_A(dx_A/dt_A - v) = d²x_A/dt_A² = a_A. Since a_B = a_A, we find F_A = ma_B. In order words, Newton's second law still holds in B's frame. To check Newton's law of gravitation, suppose we have two test masses m₁ and m₂ located at x₁,A and x₂,A, respectively, in A's frame. Align the x-axis along the direction of r_A ≡ x₁,A - x₂,A. The gravitational force between the two masses observed in A's frame is F_A = -Gm₁m₂r̂_A/r_A² = -Gm₁m₂(x₁,A - x₂,A)/(x₁,A - x₂,A)³. Now observer B observes in their frame F_B = -Gm₁m₂(x₁,B - x₂,B)/(x₁,B - x₂,B)³. Using Galilean Relativity, we find that x₁,B - x₂,B = (x₁,A - vt_A) - (x₂,A - vt_A) = x₁,A - x₂,A. Plugging back to Eq. (5.4), we find that F_B = -Gm₁m₂(x₁,A - x₂,A)/(x₁,A - x₂,A)³ = F_A, so Newton's law of gravitation is consistent with the Principle of Relativity as well under Galilean Relativity.

question_text: (15 points) Explain why it makes sense to talk about observer 1 being "at rest relative to" observer 2 or "moving at some velocity" relative to observer 2, but it does not make sense to talk about observer 1 being "inertial relative to" observer 2.
solution_text: It makes sense to talk about observer 1 being "at rest relative to" observer 2 or "moving at some velocity" relative to observer 2 because there is no absolute way of determining if an observer is at rest or not. Therefore, it only makes sense to say if one observer is moving relative to another. On the other hand, an observer can tell if her experience is consistent with Newton's law of inertia. This is an absolute definition that makes no reference to any other frame, so it does not make sense to say observer 1 being "inertial relative to" observer 2.

question_text: (25 points) Consider an upwards-pointing two-dimensional cone with radius r and height h: (5 points) Write down the parametric equations for the cone in R³ using h and r and Cartesian coordinates. That is, write down the equations of the form: x = y = z = In terms of some parameters (I recommend θ and z). Be sure to specify the range of those parameters.
solution_text: The parametric equations for the two-dimensional cone in R³ is x = (r/h)zcosθ, y = (r/h)zsinθ, z = z, where we have 0 ≤ z ≤ h and 0 ≤ θ < 2π.

question_text: (25 points) Consider an upwards-pointing two-dimensional cone with radius r and height h: (5 points) Write down both the equations for the cone in terms of the opening angle, call it α, rather than h and r. Note that since these equations do not invoke h and r, you can use them to specify an infinitely-extended cone. What range of parameters does that correspond to?
solution_text: Using the opening angle of the cone, α, (and recall that the opening angle is defined as α = 2tan⁻¹(r/h)) we can rewrite Eq. (7.1) as x = ztan(α/2)cosθ, y = ztan(α/2)sinθ, z = z. To specify an infinitely-extended cone, we just need to extend the range of z from previously [0,h] to now [0,∞).

question_text: (25 points) Consider an upwards-pointing two-dimensional cone with radius r and height h: (15 points) Write down the equations for the solid (three-dimensional) cone (including its boundary) in terms of α and your parameters. Use this to prove (using equations) that any point p inside the solid cone lies on a line from the origin to p, where the angle that this line makes with the z-axis is smaller than α/2. Conversely, show (using either equations or no more than two sentences in words) that any point that lies on such a line must live inside of the solid cone.
solution_text: To write down the equations for the three-dimensional cone, we just need to allow the opening angle from Eq. (7.2) to vary between 0 and the cone's boundary, namely x = ztan(α'/2)cosθ, y = ztan(α'/2)sinθ, z = z, where α' ∈ [0,α]. It is straightforward to use Eq. (7.3) to prove the statement that we are asked to prove. We pick an arbitrary point inside the cone using Eq. (7.3), namely fixing a value for z, θ, and α', respectively, and call it p. Then if we allow z to vary while fixing θ and α', we get the equation for the line that connects the origin and point p. Since we picked p to be inside the cone, namely requiring α' < α, the angle that this line makes with the z-axis (namely α'/2) is smaller than α/2. Conversely, we can pick a line with α'/2 < α/2 by fixing θ while allowing z to vary. Then any point on this line has α' < α, and by Eq. (7.3) we know such a point is located inside the cone, so we are done with the proof.

question_text: (40 points) Recall that an event B lies in the future of an event A if and only if it lies in the solid cone bounded by light rays. Using the fact that light rays travel at 45 degree angles and the results of problem 7(c), prove that B lies in the future of A if and only if there there is a single (sublight) worldline that passes through A and then through B. Note that you are being asked to prove an if and only if statement: that means you must prove that if B lies in the future of A, then there is a sublight worldline that passes through A and then through B AND also prove that if there is a sublight worldline that passes through A and then through B then B lies in the future of A.
solution_text: Suppose B lies in the future of A, then by the statement given at the beginning of the problem, B lies in the solid cone bounded by light rays emitted from A. The conclusion of problem 7(c) states that any point p inside a solid cone lies on a line from the origin to p, where the angle that this line makes with the z-axis is smaller than half of the opening angle of the cone. This means B lies on a line from A to B, and the angle this line makes with the z-axis is smaller than 45 degrees. Namely, there is a single (sublight) worldline that passes through A and then through B. Now we prove the other direction. Suppose there is a single sublight (i.e. the angle with the z-axis is smaller than 45 degrees) worldline that passes through A and then through B, then the conclusion of problem 7(c) indicates that B, as a point that lies on such a line, must live inside of the solid cone. Then by the statement given at the beginning of this problem, B in the solid cone bounded by light rays from A indicates that B lies in the future of event A, so we are done.

question_text: Using natural units: Convert the following quantities to natural units: 5 Joules of energy, a density of 10kg/m³, an acceleration of 3m/s².
solution_text:
We can use the definition of natural units, given in your lecture
l_p = 1.6·10^{-35}m
m_p = 2.2·10^{-8}kg
t_p = 5.4·10^{-44}s

to convert SI units to natural units. Namely,
1m = 6.3·10^{34}l_p
1kg = 4.6·10^7m_p
1s = 1.9·10^{43}t_p

Then we get
5J = 5kg·m²·s^{-2} = 5×4.6·10^7m_p ×[6.3·10^{34}l_p/(1.9·10^{43}t_p)]² = 2.5·10^{-9}m_p·l_p²·t_p^{-2},

and similarly
10kg·m^{-3} = 1.8·10^{-96}m_p·l_p^{-3},

and
3m·s^{-2} = 5.2·10^{-52}l_p·t_p^{-2}.

question_text: Using natural units: Convert the following quantities from natural units to SI: a velocity of 5, and a time of 3.
Flesh out the algebra with factors of c and show using a Taylor expansion for small velocities that the Lorentz transformations reduce to Galilean transformations.
solution_text:
The Lorentz transformations, with factors of c inserted, are
x' = γ(v)(x-vt) = [1-(v/c)²]^{-1/2}(x-vt)
t' = γ(v)(t-vx/c²) = [1-(v/c)²]^{-1/2}(t-vx/c²)

Using the formula (1+ε)^α = 1+αε+O(ε²) where ε ≪ 1, we can expand Eq. (7) and find
x' = [1 + (1/2)(v/c)² + O((v/c)⁴)](x-vt)

Dropping all the terms that involves (v/c) since we are considering the case where v ≪ c, we get
x' ≈ x-vt

Similarly, we expand Eq. (8) and find
t' = [1 + (1/2)(v/c)² + O((v/c)⁴)](t-vx/c²)

Again, dropping terms that involves (v/c), we get
t' ≈ t

Equations (10) and (12) are indeed Galilean transformations.

question_text: A rod of length L is at rest in R, with one end at the origin and one end at x = +L. According to the frame R', R moves in the +x direction with speed v. R and R' coincide at t=0 (i.e. there is no rigid translation between them). At t = 0, which coincides with t' = 0, a flash of light is emitted. Compute the coordinates of the event at which this flash of light reaches the other end of the rod in both R and R'.
solution_text: First, let's consider the event at which the flash of light emitted from the origin reaches the end of the rod in frame R. We know that the rod is at rest in R, so the end of the rod is still located at x = L when light reaches it, and it takes light a time of L to reach the end of the rod (recall that the speed of light is c in any inertial reference frame and we are setting c = 1, so L/c = L). The coordinates of the event in frame R is: (t,x) = (L,L). To find the coordinates of the same event (recall that an event itself is invariant of the reference frame but its coordinates are not) in frame R', we just need to perform the Lorentz transform on x and t. We find x' = γ(v)(x+vt) = L√((1+v)/(1-v)) and t' = γ(v)(t+vx) = L√((1+v)/(1-v)), which are the coordinates of the event in frame R'.

question_text: The two submarines USS Barracuda and USS Argonaut are traveling toward one another. The relative velocity between them is v. For purposes of this problem you may ignore water friction. The proper length of each submarine is L₀. The submarines are slightly offset so one is deeper than the other. As part of a military exercise, USS Argonaut fires a torpedo vertically towards the USS Barracuda as soon as the tail of the Barracuda passes the head of the Argonaut in the Argonaut rest frame. (Assume that the torpedo is very fast and the offset is very small so we may approximate the torpedo as traveling through the offset instantaneously.) Determine the coordinates when the head of the Argonaut and tail of the Barracuda are aligned in both the Argonaut rest frame (frame A) and Barracuda rest frame (frame B).
solution_text: The problem did not specify the origin of frame A (the Argonaut's frame) or frame B (the Barracuda's frame), so we can make the choice that is most convenient for us. The easiest choice for part a is to place frame A's origin at the head of the Argonaut and place frame B's origin at the tail of the Barracuda. The origins of the two frames coincide when the head of the Argonaut and tail of the Barracuda are aligned (call it event 1). In addition, we can set the clocks of the Argonaut and the Barracuda to both read time 0 at that instance. Then the coordinates of event 1 in the Argonaut's frame and in the Barracuda's frame are (t_A^(1), x_A^(1)) = (0,0), and (t_B^(1), x_B^(1)) = (0,0), respectively.

question_text: The two submarines USS Barracuda and USS Argonaut are traveling toward one another. The relative velocity between them is v. The proper length of each submarine is L₀. As part of a military exercise, USS Argonaut fires a torpedo vertically towards the USS Barracuda as soon as the tail of the Barracuda passes the head of the Argonaut in the Argonaut rest frame. Determine the coordinates when the torpedo is fired in both frames A and B.
solution_text: Let's call the Argonaut firing the torpedo event 2. In frame A, the torpedo is fired at the same time as event 1, so t_A^(2) = t_A^(1) = 0. We also know that the torpedo is fired from the Argonaut's tail, so x_A^(2) = -L₀. To find out the coordinates of event 2 in frame B, we just need to perform the Lorentz transform: x_B^(2) = γ(v)(x_A^(2) + vt_A^(2)) = -L₀/√(1-v²) and t_B^(2) = γ(v)(t_A^(2) + vx_A^(2)) = -vL₀/√(1-v²). So, the coordinates of the torpedo being fired are (t_A^(2), x_A^(2)) = (0, -L₀) in frame A and (t_B^(2), x_B^(2)) = (-vL₀/√(1-v²), -L₀/√(1-v²)) in frame B.

question_text: The two submarines USS Barracuda and USS Argonaut are traveling toward one another. The relative velocity between them is v. The proper length of each submarine is L₀. Determine the coordinates when the tail of the Argonaut and the head of the Barracuda are aligned in both frames A and B. Does the Barracuda get hit or not? Do the sailors on the Argonaut agree with the sailors on the Barracuda?
solution_text: Let's call the event at which the tail of the Argonaut and the head of the Barracuda are aligned event 3. To figure out the coordinates of event 3 in frame A, notice that event 3 happens at the tail of the Argonaut which is always at rest at x_A = -L₀ in frame A, so x_A^(3) = -L₀. To figure out the time of event 3 in frame A, we notice that the length of the Barracuda is contracted to be L₀/γ(v) in frame A, so the Barracuda needs to travel a distance of L₀ - L₀/γ(v) in order for its head to reach the tail of the Argonaut. That implies t_A^(3) = (L₀ - L₀/γ(v))/v = [1 - 1/γ(v)]L₀/v = (1-√(1-v²))L₀/v. Now we just need to perform the Lorentz transform to get the coordinates of event 3 in frame B: x_B^(3) = γ(v)(x_A^(3) + vt_A^(3)) = -L₀ and t_B^(3) = γ(v)(t_A^(3) + vx_A^(3)) = -(1-√(1-v²))L₀/v. To determine if the Barracuda gets hit or not, we just need to compare t^(2) and t^(3) in a particular frame. First, it is clear that t_A^(3) > t_A^(2) since t_A^(2) = 0 and 1 - 1/γ(v) > 0. So, according to the Argonaut, the Barracuda does not get hit. Checking in frame B: t_B^(3) - t_B^(2) = L₀/(γ(v)v)[1/√(1-v²) - √(1-v²)] > 0, so t_B^(3) > t_B^(2). This means the Barracuda does not get hit in frame B either, just as expected.

question_text: Time dilation, another way. Do not use the Lorentz transformation formulas on this problem, the point is to derive time dilation in a different way. Alice is holding a rod of proper length L vertically with two mirrors on its ends. A light beam starts out at one mirror, travels to the second, bounces off of it, and returns to the first mirror. What is the elapsed time ∆t_A for the light beam to reach the second mirror in Alice's frame?
solution_text: In Alice's frame, light travels vertically up for a distance L to reach the second mirror, so the time that it takes is simply ∆t_A = L/c.

question_text: Muons are unstable particles that decay after a short time into other particles (and radiation). Consider a muon that lives for 10⁻⁶s (this is a bit shorter than the average lifetime of 2·10⁻⁶s). Muons are frequently created in the upper atmosphere, at a height of about 30,000m, when ambient radiation (cosmic rays) from space collides with the atoms of the atmosphere. Consider our muon of 10⁻⁶s lifetime falling directly towards the earth's surface at speed v in Ls/s. In a naive, Galilean calculation, what would v need to be for the muon to reach earth?
solution_text: Since a muon decays after 10⁻⁶s, it must take the muon less than 10⁻⁶s to reach the surface of earth in order to be directly detected. In a naive Galilean calculation, this tells us that the muon must travel through the atmosphere at a speed of v = h/t = 30,000m/10⁻⁶s = 3·10¹⁰m/s = 100c, which is just 100 in natural units. This is clearly wrong in relativity since it would indicate that muons travel at least 100 times faster than light!

question_text: Muons are unstable particles that decay after a short time into other particles (and radiation). Consider a muon that lives for 10⁻⁶s (this is a bit shorter than the average lifetime of 2·10⁻⁶s). Muons are frequently created in the upper atmosphere, at a height of about 30,000m, when ambient radiation (cosmic rays) from space collides with the atoms of the atmosphere. Consider our muon of 10⁻⁶s lifetime falling directly towards the earth's surface at speed v in Ls/s. Taking into account relativity, what does v need to be for the muon to reach earth?
solution_text: It takes the muon t_decay = 10⁻⁶s to decay in its rest frame. When the muon is traveling at a relativistic speed, its lifetime measured on the surface of earth, t'_decay, is much longer due to time dilation: t'_decay = γ(v_muon)t_decay. Then if a muon reaches the surface of earth before it decays, it needs to travel at least with a speed of v_muon = h/t'_decay. Plug in the time dilation equation and solve for v_muon, we get v_muon = (h/t_decay)/√(1+h²/t²_decay) = 0.99995.

question_text: Muons are unstable particles that decay after a short time into other particles (and radiation). Consider a muon that lives for 10⁻⁶s (this is a bit shorter than the average lifetime of 2·10⁻⁶s). Muons are frequently created in the upper atmosphere, at a height of about 30,000m, when ambient radiation (cosmic rays) from space collides with the atoms of the atmosphere. If the muon were falling towards earth at v = 0.99999, and once you catch it on earth you wanted to shoot back up towards the atmosphere at v = 0.99999 so it decays just as it reaches the upper atmosphere at 30km, how long should you keep the muon at the surface of the earth before launching it back up?
solution_text: We know a muon has a lifetime of t_decay = 10⁻⁶s. Then the time for the muon to travel down to the surface of earth, being kept at the surface, and traveling back to the atmosphere must add up to t_decay in the muon's rest frame. The time for the muon to travel down to earth, measured by an observer at the surface of earth, is t' = h/v. It is related to the time measured in the muon's rest frame (call it Δτ) by t' = γ(v)Δτ. Using these equations, we can solve for Δτ and get Δτ = γ⁻¹(v) × (h/v) = (h/v)√(1-v²) = 4.47·10⁻⁷s. When we keep the muon at the surface of earth, there is no time dilation effect since the muon is at rest, and the time for the muon to travel back to the atmosphere is again Δτ. We then find the time to keep the muon at the surface of earth to be Δt = t_decay - 2Δτ = 1.1·10⁻⁷s.

question_text: Let L₀ be the length of a rod in its rest frame R. In R', the rod is moving at speed v in the +x direction. In R, the rod is sitting at an angle θ to the x axis. What is the length of the rod in R'? What angle does the rod make with the x'-axis?
solution_text: Recall from the lecture that for a moving object, only the length parallel to the direction of motion is Lorentz contracted. In frame R, the length of the rod projected on the x axis and y axis are L_x = L₀ cosθ and L_y = L₀ sinθ, respectively. In frame R', since the rod is moving along the x direction, only its length along the x direction is contracted, but not the length along the y direction. We thus measure the length of the rod along the x' and y' axis in frame R' to be L'_x = L_x/γ(v) = √(1-v²)L₀ cosθ and L'_y = L_y = L₀ sinθ. Then the length of the rod in frame R' is L' = √((L'_x)² + (L'_y)²) = L₀√(1-v²cos²θ), and the angle it makes with the x'-axis is θ' = tan⁻¹(L'_y/L'_x) = tan⁻¹(sinθ/√(1-v²)cosθ).

question_text: A spaceship travels to a far away planet at speed v = 0.9c. It spends 1 year on that planet. It then returns to earth at speed v = 0.99c. The spaceship clocks measure 5 years upon return. Ignoring effects of deceleration and acceleration: If you're an observer sitting on earth, how far away do you conclude the planet is in Ls?
solution_text: Let's call the outbound journey trip 1 and the inbound journey trip 2, then v₁ = 0.9c and v₂ = 0.99c. We know that the time passed on earth during trip 1 and 2 are just t'₁ = d/v₁ and t'₂ = d/v₂, where d is the distance between earth and the planet. Due to time dilation, the time passed on the spaceship is different and is given by t₁ = t'₁/γ(v₁) = √(1-(v₁/c)²) × d/v₁ and t₂ = t'₂/γ(v₂) = √(1-(v₂/c)²) × d/v₂. We know that t₁ and t₂ should sum up to 4 years (5 years minus 1 year being on the planet), so we can solve for the distance by setting c = 1Ls/s and converting years to seconds: t₁ + t₂ = √(1-(v₁/c)²) × d/v₁ + √(1-(v₂/c)²) × d/v₂ = 4×3.15·10⁷s, from which we solve for d and find that d = 2·10⁸s.

question_text: A spaceship travels to a far away planet at speed v = 0.9c. It spends 1 year on that planet. It then returns to earth at speed v = 0.99c. The spaceship clocks measure 5 years upon return. Ignoring effects of deceleration and acceleration: How much time elapses in the outbound trip for the spaceship?
solution_text: Using the equation from part (a) and the distance we found (d = 2·10⁸s), we have t₁ = √(1-(v₁/c)²) × d/v₁ = 9.7·10⁷s = 3.1yr.

question_text: A spaceship travels to a far away planet at speed v = 0.9c. It spends 1 year on that planet. It then returns to earth at speed v = 0.99c. The spaceship clocks measure 5 years upon return. Ignoring effects of deceleration and acceleration: How much time elapses in the entire trip in your reference frame?
solution_text: In the reference frame on earth, the total time passed during the entire trip is t'₁ + 1yr + t'₂ = d/v₁ + 3.15·10⁷s + d/v₂ = 4.6·10⁸s = 14.5yr.

question_text: A spaceship travels to a far away planet at speed v = 0.9c. It spends 1 year on that planet. It then returns to earth at speed v = 0.99c. The spaceship clocks measure 5 years upon return. Ignoring effects of deceleration and acceleration: Thirty minutes into the trip in the spaceship frame, the spaceship sent out a light beam back towards earth. How much time do you find has elapsed between the moment that the rocket left and the moment that you received the signal?
solution_text: First, we need to find out how much time has passed on earth when the spaceship measures 30 minutes: t_earth = t_ship × γ(v₁) = 1800s × 1/√(1-0.9²) = 4130s. So we know that when the earth clock reads 4130 seconds, light was emitted from the spaceship. At that moment, the spaceship was d_ship = v₁ × t_earth = 0.9Ls/s × 4130s = 3717Ls away from the earth, so people on earth had to wait another 3717 seconds for light to reach them. Then the time elapsed between the moment that the rocket left and the moment that people on earth received the signal is t_total = t_earth + d_ship/c = 7847s = 131min.

question_text: Alice is holding a device that blinks every second in her rest frame. Bob perceives Alice to be moving away from him at v = 0.8c. How frequently are light rays being emitted by the blinks in Bob's frame?
solution_text: In Alice's frame, the light signals are sent at an interval of Δt_A = 1s. Due to time dilation, this interval measured by Bob is then Δt_B = γ(v)Δt_A = 1.67s.

question_text: Alice is holding a device that blinks every second in her rest frame. Bob perceives Alice to be moving away from him at v = 0.8c. What is the distance covered by Alice between blinks in Bob's frame?
solution_text: The distance covered by Alice between blinks in Bob's frame is Δx_B = v × Δt_B = 0.8 × 3·10⁸m/s × 1.67s = 4·10⁸m = 1.33Ls.

question_text: Alice is holding a device that blinks every second in her rest frame. Bob perceives Alice to be moving away from him at v = 0.8c. What is the time interval between the blinks received by Bob in Bob's frame?
solution_text: From part a, we know that in Bob's frame, the light signals are emitted every 1.67s. However, each light signal has to travel a distance of Δx_B more than the previous one since Alice has moved away by the amount of Δx_B, as calculated in part b. So, the time interval between the blinks received by Bob in his frame is Δτ = Δt_B + Δx_B/c = 1.67s + 1.33s = 3s. This can also be written as Δτ = Δt_A × √((1+v/c)/(1-v/c)), which is the relativistic Doppler effect.

question_text: As an expert multitasker, you decide to hone both your tennis skills and your 8.033 knowledge at the same time. You are practicing volleying a ball against a moving wall. The ball has the very special (and unrealistic) property that it bounces back from the wall at the exact speed with which it hit the wall (in the wall rest frame). The wall is moving towards you at v_WY. If you perceive your throw of the ball to be at speed v_BY, at what speed do you perceive the ball bounce back at you? What happens when v_BY approaches 1Ls/s?
solution_text: Suppose that you throw the ball along the +x direction and that the wall moves toward you in the -x direction, then the ball has a velocity of +v_BY and the wall has a velocity of -v_WY in your reference frame (equivalently, your velocity relative to the wall is v_YW = +v_WY). The ball bounces back from the wall at the same speed it hits the wall, so we need to find the ball's velocity relative to the wall before the impact. Using the velocity addition formula, we get v_BW = (v_BY + v_WY)/(1 + v_BY × v_WY). Then, the velocity of the ball relative to the wall after the ball bounces back is v'_BW = -v_BW. Then, we can use the velocity addition formula again to find out the ball's velocity relative to you after it bounces back: v'_BY = -(v_BY + 2v_WY + v_BY × v²_WY)/(1 + 2v_BY × v_WY + v²_WY). Now suppose v_BY → 1, the equation becomes v'_BY → -1. This is expected, since if you send a light pulse toward the wall and measure the speed of light that is reflected back at you, you will certainly get the velocity to be -1 as the principle of relativity tells us that must be the case.

question_text:
Let {xμ}³μ=0 be the coordinates in some coordinate system, and let {xμ'}³μ'=0 be another set of coordinates in a different coordinate system. We write xμ'(xμ) to denote the primed coordinates as functions of the unprimed coordinates. For example, if xμ were spherical coordinates in some inertial reference frame and xμ' were Cartesian coordinates in the same inertial reference frame, then x1'(x⁰,x¹,x²,x³) = x(t,r,θ,φ) = rsinθcosφ. For now, take the primed and unprimed systems to be two general coordinate systems (i.e. don't assume that one is Cartesian or spherical).

Let Γ(λ) be a curve. The components of its tangent vector vᵃ at some λ=λ₀ are given in the unprimed coordinate system by:
vμ = dxμ(λ)/dλ

Write the components of vμ' using the chain rule; write all terms that you can under a summation sign Σ. Note that vectors are defined so as to be invariant under boosts, translations, rotations, or more generally any coordinate transformation. The formula you derive here tells you how the components vμ must change in the primed coordinate system to vμ' in a way which guarantees that the vector itself doesn't change: i.e. that vμ' = dxμ'(λ)/dλ. So that vμ describes the components of the vector tangent to Γ in one coordinate system, and vμ' describes the components of the vector tangent to Γ in another coordinate system, but they both describe the same vector tangent to Γ: vᵃ.
solution_text:
By definition, we know the tangent vector in the primed coordinate system is given by
vμ' = dxμ'(λ)/dλ. (1)

We can regard the primed coordinates as functions of unprimed coordinates. Namely,
xμ'(λ) = xμ'(x⁰(λ),x¹(λ),x²(λ),x³(λ)) ≡ xμ'(xμ(λ)). (2)

Then we can apply the chain rule to Eq. (1) and express vμ' in terms of vμ
vμ' = dxμ'(λ)/dλ = dxμ'(xμ(λ))/dλ
= ∂xμ'/∂x⁰ · dx⁰/dλ + ∂xμ'/∂x¹ · dx¹/dλ + ∂xμ'/∂x² · dx²/dλ + ∂xμ'/∂x³ · dx³/dλ (3)
= Σ³μ=0 (∂xμ'/∂xμ) · (dxμ/dλ).

Realizing that vμ = dxμ/dλ, we get
vμ' = Σ³μ=0 (∂xμ'/∂xμ)vμ. (4)

Note that Eq (4) not just gives us the rule governing how the components of the tangent vector vᵃ transform between the primed and unprimed coordinates. In fact, the components of an arbitrary vector, say wᵃ, will follow the same transformation rule
wμ' = Σ³μ=0 (∂xμ'/∂xμ)wμ. (5)

question_text: Let {xμ}³μ=0 be the coordinates in some coordinate system, and let {xμ'}³μ'=0 be another set of coordinates in a different coordinate system. We write xμ'(xμ) to denote the primed coordinates as functions of the unprimed coordinates. Using the transformation rule for vectors derived in part (a): vμ' = Σ³μ=0 (∂xμ'/∂xμ)vμ, find a simple coordinate transformation that shows that xμ, the coordinates in a coordinate system, do not transform like a vector (i.e. that they do not transform via the transformation rule you derived in (a)). Conclude that there is no vector xᵃ.
solution_text:
For simplicity, let's consider a rigid translation of coordinates by defining
xμ' = xμ + aμ. (6)

If the coordinates xμ were components of a vector, we would be able to transform between the primed and unprimed coordinates using Eq. (5), namely
xμ' = Σ³μ=0 (∂xμ'/∂xμ)xμ. (7)

However, xμ + aμ cannot be written only as a multiplicative action on xμ, so Eq. (7) does not hold. Therefore we conclude that the coordinates xμ are not components of a vector, so there is no vector xᵃ.

question_text:
Let {xμ}³μ=0 be the coordinates in some coordinate system, and let {xμ'}³μ'=0 be another set of coordinates in a different coordinate system. Recall that the infinitesimal line element can be written in Cartesian coordinates:
ds² = -(dx⁰')² + (dx¹')² + (dx²')² + (dx³')².

Using the chain rule, write a general formula for the transformation of ds² from a cartesian coordinate system {xμ'} to some arbitrary (not necessarily Cartesian) coordinate system {xμ}. Use Σ sum notation whenever possible.
solution_text:
The chain rule tells us that the differentials of the primed coordinates and those of the unprimed coordinates are related by
dxμ' = Σ³μ=0 (∂xμ'/∂xμ)dxμ, (8)

so we get
ds² = -(dx⁰')² + (dx¹')² + (dx²')² + (dx³')²
= -(Σ³μ=0 (∂x⁰'/∂xμ)dxμ)² + (Σ³μ=0 (∂x¹'/∂xμ)dxμ)² + (Σ³μ=0 (∂x²'/∂xμ)dxμ)² + (Σ³μ=0 (∂x³'/∂xμ)dxμ)² (9)
= -(Σ³μ=0 (∂x⁰'/∂xμ)dxμ)² + Σ³μ'=1 (Σ³μ=0 (∂xμ'/∂xμ)dxμ)².

question_text: Using your formula from 1(c), write down ds² in spherical coordinates.
solution_text:
Let's take the primed coordinates to be Cartesian {xμ'} = {t,x,y,z} and the unprimed coordinates to be spherical coordinates {xμ} = {t,r,θ,φ}. We know that they are related by
t' = t,
x' = rsinθcosφ,
y' = rsinθsinφ,
z' = rcosθ,

or equivalently,
x⁰' = x⁰,
x¹' = x¹sinx²cosx³,
x²' = x¹sinx²sinx³,
x³' = x¹cosx².

Now applying Eq. (9), we get
ds² = -(Σ³μ=0 (∂x⁰'/∂xμ)dxμ)² + (Σ³μ=0 (∂x¹'/∂xμ)dxμ)² + (Σ³μ=0 (∂x²'/∂xμ)dxμ)² + (Σ³μ=0 (∂x³'/∂xμ)dxμ)²
= -(dt)² + (sinθcosφdr + rcosθcosφdθ - rsinθsinφdφ)²
+ (sinθsinφdr + rcosθsinφdθ + rsinθcosφdφ)² + (cosθdr - rsinθdθ)².

Now expand the quadratics, and we see that the cross terms (the ones that involve drdθ, drdφ, and dθdφ) all cancel out. Collect terms that involve dt², dr², dθ², and dφ², and use the trig identity sin²φ + cos²φ = 1, we finally arrive at
ds² = -dt² + dr² + r²dθ² + r²sin²θdφ². (13)

question_text:
For fun, let's suppose that the correct invariant distance measure in relativity were not given by
Δs² = -Δt² + Δx² + Δy² + Δz²
But instead by
Δs² = -Δt² + α²t²Δx² + Δy² + Δz².
Here α has units of length⁻² for dimensional consistency.

Consider a rod extended in the x direction at t = -1s with endpoints at x = ±x₀. What is the invariant length of this rod at this time? If the rod's endpoints remain at x = ±x₀, what is happening to its invariant length as it moves forwards in time? How is this consistent with the fact that this is the invariant length?
solution_text:
The invariant length of the rod is given by
Δs² = -Δt² + α²t²Δx² + Δy² + Δz². (14)

At t = -1s, the rod has Δt = 0 (since we are measuring the rod's length at one particular instance of time) and Δy = Δz = 0 (assuming the rod has no extent in the y and z direction), so the only non-zero interval is Δx = +x₀ - (-x₀) = 2x₀. Plugging into Eq. (14), we get
Δs² = α²(-1s)²(2x₀)² = 4α²x₀²s². (15)

We see that the invariant length of the rod is time dependent:
Δs² = 4α²x₀²t², (16)

so as time moves forward, say from t = -1s to t = 0s, the invariant length of the rod will shrink to zero (and then increases again when t > 0). This is no means contradictory with the definition of the invariant length. The invariant length is invariant under coordinate transformation, meaning that if we measure the length of the rod at a particular instance of time, then Δs² we get won't depend on what coordinate systems that we use. However, the invariant length can certainly change for different events. For example, here measuring the rod's length at t = -1s and t = 0s are two different pairs of events (since they are two sets of measurements at two different times), and each pair has its own invariant length which does not need to agree with the invariant length of the other one.

question_text:
For the hypothetical universe where the invariant distance measure is
Δs² = -Δt² + α²t²Δx² + Δy² + Δz²,
we'll now work with the line element – the infinitesimal version of the invariant interval:
ds² = -dt² + α²t²dx² + dy² + dz².

Using your formula from 1(c), show by construction that this is just a coordinate transformation of standard Cartesian special relativity coordinates. You may ignore the y and z directions. Hint: a² - b² = 1 is well-known to be satisfied by a = cosh(β) and b = sinh(β).
solution_text:
Ignoring the y and z directions, the invariant length we have is
ds² = -dt² + α²t²dx². (17)

Could we get this result by a coordinate transformation from the Cartesian coordinates? Suppose the primed coordinates are Cartesian, and the unprimed coordinates are the new coordinates which have the invariant interval as Eq. (17). At the same time, Eq. (9) also tells us what the invariant interval should look like in the new coordinates. In this case, Eq. (9) becomes
ds² = -(∂t'/∂t dt + ∂t'/∂x dx)² + (∂x'/∂t dt + ∂x'/∂x dx)². (18)

Expand and regroup the terms in Eq. (18), we have
ds² = [-(∂t'/∂t)² + (∂x'/∂t)²]dt² + [(∂x'/∂x)² - (∂t'/∂x)²]dx² + 2[∂x'/∂t ∂x'/∂x - ∂t'/∂t ∂t'/∂x]dxdt. (19)

We need to find coordinate transformations (i.e. writing the primed coordinates as functions of the unprimed coordinates) such that Eq. (19) would agree with Eq. (17). In other words, we need to find t'(t,x) and x'(t,x) that satisfy
[-(∂t'/∂t)² + (∂x'/∂t)²] = -1, (20)
[(∂x'/∂x)² - (∂t'/∂x)²] = α²t², (21)
∂x'/∂t ∂x'/∂x - ∂t'/∂t ∂t'/∂x = 0. (22)

Using the hint given by the problem, we can take (∂t'/∂t) = cosh β and (∂x'/∂t) = sinh β, where β is a function of x. After some trials and errors, it is not too difficult to find that
t' = tcosh(αx), (23)
x' = tsinh(αx) (24)
satisfy Eqs. (20, 21, and 22). In fact, Eqs. (23) and (24) are called Milne coordinates for Minkowski space.

question_text:
For the hypothetical universe where the invariant distance measure is
Δs² = -Δt² + α²t²Δx² + Δy² + Δz²,
and given the results from parts (a) and (b), explain how your results in parts (a) and (b) of this problem are consistent with the standard invariant distance measure.
solution_text: Part (b) tells us that the invariant length given in part (a) is just the usual invariant length in Minkowski space but expressed in the Milne coordinates instead of Cartesian coordinates.

question_text:
Consider the following primed coordinate system transformation (t,x,y,z):
t' = xsinh(at)
x' = xcosh(at)
y' = y
z' = z
where a is a constant, and sinh and cosh are hyperbolic trig functions, and the coordinates t', x', y', and z' are Cartesian coordinates.

Let Γ be the curve given by x = x₀, y = y₀, z = z₀, where x₀, y₀, z₀ are all constants. Pick a parameter λ with which to parametrize this curve.
solution_text:
The curve Γ has its x, y, and z components fixed at constants x₀, y₀, and z₀, so the only degree of freedom comes from the time coordinate t. In other words, Γ in the unprimed coordinates can be regarded as the trajectory of an observer in their own "rest frame", in which the observer is stationary in space and only moves forward in time. It is natural to parametrize this with the time coordinate t, so
Γ(t) = (t,x₀,y₀,z₀) (25)
in the unprimed coordinates.

question_text:
Consider the following primed coordinate system transformation (t,x,y,z):
t' = xsinh(at)
x' = xcosh(at)
y' = y
z' = z
where a is a constant, and sinh and cosh are hyperbolic trig functions, and the coordinates t', x', y', and z' are Cartesian coordinates.

Let Γ be the curve given by x = x₀, y = y₀, z = z₀, where x₀, y₀, z₀ are all constants. Write the coordinates of Γ in both the primed and unprimed coordinate systems.
solution_text:
We have already written down the coordinates of Γ in the unprimed coordinates in part (a). To write down the coordinates of Γ in the primed coordinates, we can simply use the coordinate system transformation given by the problem, so
Γ(t) = (x₀sinh(at), x₀cosh(at), y₀, z₀). (26)

question_text:
Consider the following primed coordinate system transformation (t,x,y,z):
t' = xsinh(at)
x' = xcosh(at)
y' = y
z' = z
where a is a constant, and sinh and cosh are hyperbolic trig functions, and the coordinates t', x', y', and z' are Cartesian coordinates.

Let Γ be the curve given by x = x₀, y = y₀, z = z₀, where x₀, y₀, z₀ are all constants, parametrized by t so that Γ(t) = (t,x₀,y₀,z₀). Write the tangent vector vᵃ to this curve in both primed and unprimed coordinate systems.
solution_text:
We can use the definition of the tangent vector that we already used in problem 1. In the unprimed coordinates, the components of the tangent vector vᵃ are given by
vμ = dxμ/dt, (27)
where xμ (μ = 0,1,2,3) denotes the μth component of the curve Γ in the unprimed coordinates. We get
vᵃ-unprimed → [1, 0, 0, 0]. (28)

The components of the tangent vector in the primed coordinates are given by
vμ' = dxμ'/dt, (29)
where xμ' (μ = 0,1,2,3) denotes the μ'th component of the curve Γ in the primed coordinates. We get
vᵃ-primed → [ax₀cosh(at), ax₀sinh(at), 0, 0]. (30)

question_text:
Consider the following primed coordinate system transformation (t,x,y,z):
t' = xsinh(at)
x' = xcosh(at)
y' = y
z' = z
where a is a constant, and sinh and cosh are hyperbolic trig functions, and the coordinates t', x', y', and z' are Cartesian coordinates.

Let Γ be the curve given by x = x₀, y = y₀, z = z₀, where x₀, y₀, z₀ are all constants. Use the invariant interval in finite Cartesian form
Δs² = -(Δt')² + (Δx')² + (Δy')² + (Δz')²
to compute the proper distance between any point on Γ and the origin at t' = x' = y' = z' = 0.
solution_text:
We can compute the invariant interval between the points on Γ and the origin in Cartesian (primed) coordinates as
Δs² = -(Δt')² + (Δx')² + (Δy')² + (Δz')²
= -(x₀sinh(at))² + (x₀cosh(at))² + (y₀)² + (z₀)² (31)
= (x₀)² + (y₀)² + (z₀)²,
where we have used the identity cosh²(at) - sinh²(at) = 1 in the last line.

question_text: Consider three tensors A_a, B^a, C_{ab}. What are the ranks of these tensors? Are any of them vectors? 1-forms?
solution_text: A_a has rank (1,0). It is a vector. B^a has rank (0,1). It is a 1-form. The tensor C_{ab} has rank (0,2).

question_text: Consider three tensors A_a, B^a, C_{ab}. In a choice of reference frame R, the components of these tensors are: {A_0 = 5, A_1 = 0, A_2 = -1, A_3 = -6}, {B^0 = 0, B^1 = -2, B^2 = 4, B^3 = 0}, {C_{00} = 0, C_{01} = 0, C_{02} = 3, C_{03} = 3, C_{30} = -1, C_{10} = 5, C_{11} = -2, C_{12} = -2, C_{13} = 0, C_{21} = 5, C_{22} = 2, C_{23} = -2, C_{20} = 4, C_{31} = -1, C_{32} = -3, C_{33} = 0}. In this reference frame, compute: i. A^α B_α ii. A^α C_{αβ} for all β iii. A^γ C_{γα} for all α iv. A^ρ C_{αρ} for all α v. A^α B_β for all α and β vi. A^i B_i, keeping in mind that later latin letters like i,j,k are all labels that range over the spatial components only, i.e. from 1 to 3 vii. A^j B_k
solution_text:
i. A^α B_α = Σ(μ=0 to 3) A^μ B_μ = -4

ii. A^α C_{α0} = 2, A^α C_{α1} = 1, A^α C_{α2} = 31, A^α C_{α3} = 17

iii. Compared with part ii, the differences here are that the index α is switched to γ and β is switched to α. Recall that we can relabel a dummy index which will not change the summation. Also, the free index here is simply a label for values ranging from 0 to 3, so the expression in part iii is identical to the one in the previous part. The answers are thus identical to those in the previous part.

iv. A^ρ C_{0ρ} = -21, A^ρ C_{1ρ} = 27, A^ρ C_{2ρ} = 30, A^ρ C_{3ρ} = -2

v. A^0 B_0 = 0, A^0 B_1 = -10, A^0 B_2 = 20, A^0 B_3 = 0, A^1 B_0 = 0, A^1 B_1 = 0, A^1 B_2 = 0, A^1 B_3 = 0, A^2 B_0 = 0, A^2 B_1 = 2, A^2 B_2 = -4, A^2 B_3 = 0, A^3 B_0 = 0, A^3 B_1 = 12, A^3 B_2 = -24, A^3 B_3 = 0

vi. A^i B_i = A^1 B_1 + A^2 B_2 + A^3 B_3 = -4

vii. We have already computed A^α B_β in part v. A^j B_k are just the spatial components of A^α B_β, taking j and k to be from 1 to 3.

question_text: Identify the hungry ("free") and fed ("dummy") indices in the following equation and change it into an equivalent expression with different indices. How many different component equations does this tensor equation represent? A^a B_a = 5
solution_text: The only index a is a dummy index. The expression represents one equation. We can rewrite the equation as A^b B_b = 5.

question_text: Identify the hungry ("free") and fed ("dummy") indices in the following equation and change it into an equivalent expression with different indices. How many different component equations does this tensor equation represent? T_{abc} A^a C^{bd} = D_{cd}
solution_text: The dummy indices are a and b. The free indices are c and d. Since each free index can represent any number from 0 to 3, the expression represents a total of 16 equations. We can rewrite the expression as T_{efg} A^e C^{fh} = D_{gh}.

question_text: Identify the hungry ("free") and fed ("dummy") indices in the following equation and change it into an equivalent expression with different indices. How many different component equations does this tensor equation represent? R_{ab} - (1/2)g_{ab} = G_{ab}
solution_text: Both a and b are free indices. The expression represents a total of 16 equations. We can rewrite the expression as R_{cd} - (1/2)g_{cd} = G_{cd}.

question_text: If in the frame R the vectors v^a and u^a have the components v^0 = 5, v^1 = -1, v^2 = 0, v^3 = 1 and u^0 = -2, u^1 = 1, u^2 = 1, u^3 = -6, find (in R) the components of -6v^a.
If in the frame R the vectors v^a and u^a have the components v^0 = 5, v^1 = -1, v^2 = 0, v^3 = 1 and u^0 = -2, u^1 = 1, u^2 = 1, u^3 = -6, find (in R) the components of 3v^a + u^a.
If in the frame R the vectors v^a and u^a have the components v^0 = 5, v^1 = -1, v^2 = 0, v^3 = 1 and u^0 = -2, u^1 = 1, u^2 = 1, u^3 = -6, find (in R) the components of -6v^a u^b.
solution_text: Let's call the product -6v^a u^b tensor T^{ab}. Namely, we define T^{ab} = -6v^a u^b. Then the components of the tensor T^{ab} in the R frame are T^{00} = 60, T^{01} = -30, T^{02} = -30, T^{03} = 180, T^{10} = -12, T^{11} = 6, T^{12} = 6, T^{13} = -36, T^{20} = 0, T^{21} = 0, T^{22} = 0, T^{23} = 0, T^{30} = 12, T^{31} = -6, T^{32} = -6, T^{33} = 36.

question_text: If the components of v^a in R are v^0 = 0, v^1 = -2, v^2 = 3, v^3 = 5, find the components of v^a in a reference frame R' which moves at speed 0.8 relative to R in the +x direction.
solution_text: The reference frame R' moves with a speed of V_1 = 0.8 relative to R in the +x direction. We can get the components of the vector v^a in the R' frame through the Lorentz transform: v^{0'} = γ(V_1)(v^0 - V_1 v^1) = 8/3, v^{1'} = γ(V_1)(v^1 - V_1 v^0) = -10/3, v^{2'} = v^2 = 3, v^{3'} = v^3 = 5.

question_text: If the components of v^a in R are v^0 = 0, v^1 = -2, v^2 = 3, v^3 = 5, find the components of v^a in a reference frame R'' which moves at speed 0.6 relative to R' in the positive x direction, where R' moves at speed 0.8 relative to R in the +x direction.
solution_text: The reference frame R'' moves with a speed of V_2 = 0.6 relative to R' in the +x direction. We can get the components of the vector v^a in the R'' frame by performing another Lorentz transform on the components of v^a in the R' frame: v^{0''} = γ(V_2)(v^{0'} - V_2 v^{1'}) = 35/6, v^{1''} = γ(V_2)(v^{1'} - V_2 v^{0'}) = -37/6, v^{2''} = v^{2'} = 3, v^{3''} = v^{3'} = 5.

question_text: Consider a particle with velocity U^a. Given the components U^μ in some reference frame where the particle is not at rest, express the particle's 3-velocity in this frame for general U^μ. Express this in index form, i.e. using the index label i.
solution_text: By the definition of 4-velocity, [U^0, U^1, U^2, U^3]^T = γ[1, V_1, V_2, V_3]^T, where V = √((V_1)^2 + (V_2)^2 + (V_3)^2). Therefore, we see that V_i = U^i/γ, where i can take the value of 1, 2, and 3. However, we want to express the components of the 3-velocity using U^μ only. Note that U^0 = γ from the equation above, so we get V_i = U^i/U^0.

question_text: Consider a particle with velocity U^a. If the components of U^a in this frame are U^0 = 2, U^1 = 1, U^2 = 1, U^3 = 1, write down the particle's 3-velocity in this frame.
solution_text: Using the result from part (a), V_i = U^i/U^0, we get V_1 = 1/2, V_2 = 1/2, V_3 = 1/2.

question_text: Given a tensor T_{ab}, the tensor T_{(ab)} is the symmetrization of T_{ab}: a symmetric tensor constructed just from T_{ab} and satisfying the requirement that if T_{ab} is already symmetric, then T_{(ab)} = T_{ab}. Write down T_{(ab)} in terms of T_{ab}.
solution_text:
Since T_{(ab)} is constructed from elements of T_{ab} only, the options are limited. After some trial and errors, we come up with

T_{(ab)} = (1/2)(T_{ab} + T_{ba}). (1)

It is easy to check that Eq. (1) indeed satisfies the definition of tensor symmetrization. First, T_{(ab)} needs to be symmetric

T_{(ab)} = (1/2)(T_{ab} + T_{ba}) = (1/2)(T_{ba} + T_{ab}) = T_{(ba)}. (2)

In addition, if T_{ab} is already symmetric (T_{ab} = T_{ba}), then

T_{(ab)} = (1/2)(T_{ab} + T_{ba}) = (1/2)(T_{ab} + T_{ab}) = T_{ab}. (3)

question_text: Given a tensor T_{ab}, the antisymmetrization of T_{ab} is denoted T_{[ab]} and is defined as the antisymmetric tensor (an antisymmetric tensor Q_{ab} satisfies Q_{ab} = -Q_{ba}) constructed just from T_{ab} and satisfying the requirement that if T_{ab} is already antisymmetric, then T_{[ab]} = T_{ab}. Write down T_{[ab]} in terms of T_{ab}.
solution_text:
We can check that

T_{[ab]} = (1/2)(T_{ab} - T_{ba}) (4)

is the antisymmetrization of T_{ab}. First, check that T_{[ab]} is antisymmetric

T_{[ba]} = (1/2)(T_{ba} - T_{ab}) = -T_{[ab]}. (5)

In addition, if T_{ab} = -T_{ba}, then

T_{[ab]} = (1/2)(T_{ab} - T_{ba}) = (1/2)(T_{ab} + T_{ab}) = T_{ab}. (6)

question_text: Given a tensor T_{ab}, using the results from parts (a) and (b) where T_{(ab)} = (1/2)(T_{ab} + T_{ba}) is the symmetrization and T_{[ab]} = (1/2)(T_{ab} - T_{ba}) is the antisymmetrization, show that any tensor can be written as a sum of symmetric and antisymmetric parts.
solution_text:
We can simply add Eq. (1) and Eq. (4) together and get

T_{(ab)} + T_{[ab]} = T_{ab}, (7)

which is to say that an arbitrary tensor T_{ab} can be written as the sum of a symmetric tensor T_{(ab)} and an antisymmetric tensor T_{[ab]}.

question_text: Show that if and only if T_{[ab]} = T_{ab}, then T^{ab} = T^{[ab]}. That is, raising and lowering indices preserves antisymmetry of the index. Is this also true of T_{(ab)}?
solution_text:
We first assume that T_{ab} = -T_{ba} (which is equivalent to T_{[ab]} = T_{ab}), then we raise its indices using the metric

T^{ab} = g^{aa′}g^{bb′}T_{a′b′} = -g^{aa′}g^{bb′}T_{b′a′} = -T^{ba}. (8)

Now we check the other way around. Assuming T^{ab} = -T^{ba}, we lower the indices of T^{ab}, we get

T_{ab} = g_{aa′}g_{bb′}T^{a′b′} = -g_{aa′}g_{bb′}T^{b′a′} = -T_{ba}. (9)

So we have shown that raising and lowering indices preserves antisymmetry of the index.

The same argument applies to the symmetry of the index. We assume that T_{ab} = T_{ba}, then

T^{ab} = g^{aa′}g^{bb′}T_{a′b′} = g^{aa′}g^{bb′}T_{b′a′} = T^{ba}. (10)

Assuming T^{ab} = T^{ba}, then

T_{ab} = g_{aa′}g_{bb′}T^{a′b′} = g_{aa′}g_{bb′}T^{b′a′} = T_{ba}. (11)

So we have shown that raising and lowering indices preserves symmetry of the index.

question_text: Derive the law of transverse velocity addition using the technology of chapter 4: that is, without using the matrix form of the Lorentz transformations or the 4-velocity vector.
solution_text:
Suppose Alice is boosted with 3-velocity v⃗_{AB} in Bob's frame, which we assume not just along the +x direction, and Charlie is boosted with speed v_{CB} along the +x direction in Bob's frame. We are interested in knowing Alice's 3-velocity measured by Charlie in the +y and +z direction, namely v^y_{AC} and v^z_{AC}.

By definition, we have

v^y_{AC} = dy_{AC}/dt_{AC}, (12)

where y_{AC} is Alice's y coordinate measured by Charlie and t_{AC} is Alice's time coordinate measured by Charlie. To find out how 3-velocities add, we need to express y_{AC} and t_{AC} in terms of quantities measured in Bob's frame. By Lorentz transform, we have

y_{AC} = y_{AB}, (13)
t_{AC} = γ_{BC}(t_{AB} + v_{BC}x_{AB}). (14)

Plugging into Eq. (12), we get

v^y_{AC} = dy_{AC}/dt_{AC} = dy_{AB}/d[γ_{BC}(t_{AB} + v_{BC}x_{AB})] = dy_{AB}/(γ_{BC}(dt_{AB} + v_{BC}dx_{AB})) = v^y_{AB}/(γ_{BC}(1 + v_{BC}v^x_{AB})), (15)

where to get the last equality, we have divided the numerator and denominator by dt_{AB} and used the definition

v^y_{AB} = dy_{AB}/dt_{AB}, v^x_{AB} = dx_{AB}/dt_{AB}. (16)

The same logic applies to Alice's 3-velocity measured by Charlie in the z direction

v^z_{AC} = dz_{AC}/dt_{AC} = dz_{AB}/d[γ_{BC}(t_{AB} + v_{BC}x_{AB})] = dz_{AB}/(γ_{BC}(dt_{AB} + v_{BC}dx_{AB})) = v^z_{AB}/(γ_{BC}(1 + v_{BC}v^x_{AB})). (17)

question_text: In a reference frame R, two particles move with 3-velocities u⃗ and v⃗ in the x-y plane. The angle between the two trajectories is θ. What is the 3-velocity of one particle in the rest frame of the other?
solution_text:
We can choose the axes of the coordinate system such that u⃗ is aligned with the x axis in frame R. In this reference frame, the particle U has 3-velocity

V^x_{UR} = u^x, V^y_{UR} = 0, (18)

and particle V has 3-velocity

V^x_{VR} = v^x, V^y_{VR} = v^y, (19)

To find out particle V's 3-velocity in U's frame, we just need to apply the 3-velocity addition formula

V^x_{VU} = (V^x_{VR} + V^x_{RU})/(1 + V^x_{VR}V^x_{RU}) = (v^x - u^x)/(1 - v^xu^x), (20)

and

V^y_{VU} = V^y_{VR}/(γ_{RU}(1 + V^x_{RU}V^x_{VR})) = v^y/(γ(|u⃗|)(1 - u^xv^x)). (21)

question_text:
Consider the Euclidean, purely spatial version of the Levi Civita symbol:

ε_{ijk} = +1, if ijk is an even permutation of (123); -1, if ijk is an odd permutation of (123); 0, otherwise.

and similarly

ε^{ijk} = +1, if ijk is an even permutation of (123); -1, if ijk is an odd permutation of (123); 0, otherwise.

Show that ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} = δ^m_jδ^n_k - δ^n_jδ^m_k where δ^i_j is the usual Kronecker delta symbol: vanishing if i ≠ j and taking the value 1 if i = j.
solution_text:
We first write ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} as the sum of three terms

ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} = ε_{1jk}ε^{1mn} + ε_{2jk}ε^{2mn} + ε_{3jk}ε^{3mn}. (30)

Notice that out of the three terms on the right-hand side, at most one of them is non-zero. For example, suppose the first term ε_{1jk}ε^{1mn} is none-zero, which can only be true if one of j, k is 2 and the other is 3 while, at the same time, one of m, n is 2 and the other is 3. So, there are 4 possible combinations of values for j, k, m, and n that would allow ε_{1jk}ε^{1mn} to be none-zero:

j = 2, k = 3, m = 2, n = 3, so ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} = ε_{123}ε^{123} = 1,
j = 2, k = 3, m = 3, n = 2, so ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} = ε_{123}ε^{132} = -1,
j = 3, k = 2, m = 2, n = 3, so ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} = ε_{132}ε^{123} = -1,
j = 3, k = 2, m = 3, n = 2, so ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} = ε_{132}ε^{132} = 1. (31)

Both ε_{2jk}ε^{2mn} and ε_{3jk}ε^{3mn} will be zero in all these four cases due to repeated indices. We can draw essentially the same conclusions if ε_{2jk}ε^{2mn} is the none-zero term or if ε_{3jk}ε^{3mn} is the none-zero term.

From Eq. (31), we see that ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} is 1 only if j = m and k = n while j ≠ k, and ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} is -1 only if j = n and k = m while j ≠ k. We can summarize this conclusion with

ε_{ijk}ε^{imn} = δ^m_jδ^n_k - δ^n_jδ^m_k. (32)

question_text: Consider the Euclidean, purely spatial version of the Levi Civita symbol ε_{ijk}. Show that the cross product in three dimensions can be written in terms of ε_{ijk}. Write the curl of a vector function f⃗ in terms of ε_{ijk}.
solution_text:
By the definition of the cross product, we have

(A⃗ × B⃗)₁ = A₂B₃ - A₃B₂
(A⃗ × B⃗)₂ = A₃B₁ - A₁B₃ (33)
(A⃗ × B⃗)₃ = A₁B₂ - A₂B₁

We see that this is the same as

(A⃗ × B⃗)ᵢ = ε_{ijk}AʲBᵏ, (34)

or equivalently,

A⃗ × B⃗ = ε^{i}_{jk}AʲBᵏêᵢ. (35)

Then, we can write

∇⃗ × f⃗ = ε^{ij}_k ∂_j fᵏêᵢ. (36)

An additional note: in this problem I am writing some indices in the upper position and some in the lower position just to keep the convention that repeated indices, with one at the upper position and one at the lower position, are summed over. Since we are working in 3-D Euclidean space, an index appearing in the upper position or lower position does not make a physical difference.

question_text:
Show that the Lorentzian Levi Civita symbol ε_{μνρσ} defined:

ε_{μνρσ} = +1, if μνρσ is an even permutation of (0123); -1, if μνρσ is an odd permutation of (0123); 0, otherwise.

does not give the components of a tensor. Find a multiplicative factor that will turn it into a tensor.
solution_text:
For any 4 by 4 matrix L, a theorem from linear algebra gives

ε_{αβγδ}L^α_μL^β_νL^γ_ρL^δ_σ = ε_{μνρσ}|L|, (37)

where |L| is the determinant of the matrix L.

Now let L^μ_{μ′} = ∂x^μ/∂x^{μ′}, the coordinate transformation matrix, then we get

ε_{αβγδ}L^α_{α′}L^β_{β′}L^γ_{γ′}L^δ_{δ′} = ε_{α′β′γ′δ′}|L|. (38)

The extra factor |L| = |∂x^μ/∂x^{μ′}| shows that the Levi Civita symbol does not transform like a tensor, so it is not a tensor.

Equivalently, we can write Eq. (38) as

ε_{α′β′γ′δ′} = |L|^{-1}ε_{αβγδ}L^α_{α′}L^β_{β′}L^γ_{γ′}L^δ_{δ′}
= |L^{-1}|ε_{αβγδ}L^α_{α′}L^β_{β′}L^γ_{γ′}L^δ_{δ′} (39)
= |∂x^{μ′}/∂x^μ|ε_{αβγδ}L^α_{α′}L^β_{β′}L^γ_{γ′}L^δ_{δ′}.

We call a quantity that transforms like a tensor but with an extra factor of |∂x^{μ′}/∂x^μ|^n a tensor density of weight n. The Levi Civita symbol ε_{αβγδ} is therefore a tensor density of weight 1.

To see how to convert it to a tensor, consider how the metric transforms between coordinates

g_{α′β′} = (∂x^α/∂x^{α′})(∂x^β/∂x^{β′})g_{αβ}. (40)

Taking the determinant of Eq. (40), we get

det(g_{α′β′}) = |∂x^{μ′}/∂x^μ|^{-2}det(g_{αβ}), (41)

where det(g_{αβ}) is computed if we were to place elements of g_{ab} into a matrix and compute the matrix's determinant. We conclude that the determinant of the metric det(g_{αβ}) is a scalar tensor density of weight -2. Define g ≡ det(g_{αβ}) and g′ ≡ det(g_{α′β′}), and take the absolute value of Eq. (41) and take the square root, we get

√|g′| = |∂x^{μ′}/∂x^μ|^{-1}√|g|. (42)

Therefore, we conclude that √|g| is a scalar tensor density of weight -1. In other words, it transforms with an extra factor of |∂x^{μ′}/∂x^μ|^{-1}, which would exactly cancel out the extra factor when ε_{αβγδ} transforms. We thus conclude that

ε̃_{αβγδ} = √|g|ε_{αβγδ} (43)

is a tensor. More generally, we can always convert a tensor density of weight w into a tensor by multiplying |g|^{w/2}.

question_text:
Let U^a be a velocity vector. Define the projection

P_{ab} = g_{ab} + U_aU_b.

The aim of this operator is to project any vector V^a onto the orthogonal complement of U^a. Prove that V^a_⊥ = P^a_bV^b is orthogonal to U^a, i.e. V^a_⊥U_a = 0, and that P^a_bV^b_⊥ = V^a_⊥ (i.e. P_{ab} does not modify the vector further once it has projected it).
solution_text:
First, we want to check that V^a_⊥ is orthogonal to U^a:

V^a_⊥U_a = P^a_bV^bU_a = (g^a_b + U^aU_b)V^bU_a
= (g^a_bU_a + U^aU_bU_a)V^b
= (g_{ab}U^a - U_b)V^b (44)
= (U_b - U_b)V^b = 0,

where in the second last line we have used U^aU_a = -1 for the 4-velocity vector U^a.

Now we want to check that V^a_⊥ is unaffected by further projections.

P^a_bV^b_⊥ = (g^a_b + U^aU_b)(g^b_c + U^bU_c)V^c
= (g^a_bg^b_c + U^aU_bg^b_c + g^a_bU^bU_c + U^aU_bU^bU_c)V^c
= (g^a_c + U^aU_bg_{bc} + g^a_{b}U^bU_c - U^aU_c)V^c (45)
= (g^a_c + U^aU_c + U^aU_c - U^aU_c)V^c
= (g^a_c + U^aU_c)V^c = P^a_cV^c = V^a_⊥.

question_text: Let U^a be a velocity vector. For the projection operator P_{ab} = g_{ab} + U_aU_b, to get the properties from part (a) for an arbitrary timelike vector which is not a 4-velocity, how would you have to modify P^a_b?
solution_text:
From (a), we saw that the definition P^a_b = g^a_b + U^aU_b worked because the property of 4-velocity that U^aU_a = -1. Now suppose we have an arbitrary timelike 4-vector W^a, in analogy, we can define the projection operator as P^a_b = g^a_b + nW^aW_b, where n is a scalar taken into account that W^aW_a is no longer -1. Similarly to how the term U^aU_b works for a 4-velocity, we want nW^aW_bW_a = -W_b, so we just need to set n = -1/(W^aW_a). Therefore, in this case the projection tensor is

P^a_b = g^a_b - (W^aW_b)/(W^cW_c). (46)

Comment: Note that I did not write

P^a_b = g^a_b - (W^aW_b)/(W^aW_a)

because the index a would appear three times with the one in the numerator being a free index and the two in the denominator being dummy indices, which is not good!

question_text: Let U^a be a velocity vector. For the projection operator P_{ab} = g_{ab} + U_aU_b, define a version of P_{ab} that projects onto the orthogonal complement of an arbitrary spacelike vector.
solution_text: Although we assumed W^a was an arbitrary timelike vector, our definition of the projection operator in Eq. (46) did not rely on whether W^aW_a < 0 or > 0. So the same definition in Eq. (46) can be applied to an arbitrary spacelike vector as well!

question_text: Prove or show that the statement is false: If V^a and W^a are spacelike, and V^aW_a = 0, then V^a + W^a is also spacelike.
solution_text:
To check if V^a + W^a is spacelike, we just need to check if (V^a + W^a)(V_a + W_a) > 0.

(V^a + W^a)(V_a + W_a) = V^aV_a + 2V^aW_a + W^aW_a. (47)

We are given that V^a and W^a are spacelike, so V^aV_a > 0 and W^aW_a > 0. In addition, it is given that V^aW_a = 0, so we conclude that

(V^a + W^a)(V_a + W_a) = V^aV_a + W^aW_a > 0, (48)

so we have shown that V^a + W^a is spacelike.

question_text: Prove or show that the statement is false: If U^a is any timelike vector and k^a is any null vector, then U^ak_a ≠ 0.
solution_text:
k^a is a null vector, so we can choose our coordinates such that k^1 = k^0, k^1 = k^2 = 0, where k^0 ≠ 0. In this frame,

U^ak_a = -U^0k^0 + U^1k^1 = -U^0k^0 + U^1k^0 = k^0(U^1 - U^0). (49)

Since k^a is a null vector, k^0 ≠ 0. We just need to show that U^1 - U^0 ≠ 0, which we will show by contradiction. Suppose U^1 - U^0 = 0 (or equivalently U^0 = U^1), then the norm of U^a is

U^aU_a = -(U^0)^2 + (U^1)^2 + (U^2)^2 + (U^3)^2 = (U^2)^2 + (U^3)^2. (50)

We are given that U^a is a timelike vector, so U^2 and U^3 cannot be both zero, which would otherwise indicate U^a is a null vector. Then U^aU_a = (U^2)^2 + (U^3)^2 > 0 which contradicts that U^a is a timelike vector. We thus conclude that U^0 ≠ U^1, so Eq. (49) cannot be zero.

Note that although we did this proof by choosing a specific frame such that k^1 = k^2 = 0, U^ak_a is a Lorentz scalar so it is zero in all frames. We are done with the proof.

question_text: Prove or show that the statement is false: If U^a and V^a are 4-velocities, then U^a - V^a is a 4-velocity.
solution_text: The statement is false. We can pick an arbitrary 4-velocity U^a and pick V^a = U^a. We see that U^a - V^a = 0, but 4-velocities must have their norms equal to -1, so we conclude that U^a - V^a is not a 4-velocity in general!

question_text: A timelike or null vector is said to be future-directed if it points into the future lightcone. Prove that the sum of a future directed timelike vector t^a and a future-directed null vector k^a is timelike and future directed.
solution_text:
t^a and k^a being future-directed means t^0 > 0 and k^0 > 0 in any reference frame (given that they are both timelike vectors). Then it is simple to verify that v^a = t^a + k^a is also future-directed. Since v^0 = t^0 + k^0, the sum of two positive numbers guarantees that v^0 > 0.

To show that v^a = t^a + k^a is timelike, we can choose an inertial reference frame where k^1 = k^0, k^2 = k^3 = 0. Then

v^av_a = (t^a + k^a)(t_a + k_a) = t^at_a + 2t^ak_a + k^ak_a = t^at_a + 2t^ak_a. (51)

Since t^a is timelike, t^at_a < 0. We just need to check if t^ak_a < 0. We write

t^ak_a = t^0k_0 + t^1k_1 = -t^0k^0 + t^1k^0 = k^0(t^1 - t^0). (52)

Since k^a is future directed, k^0 > 0. t^a being timelike implies that

(t^0)^2 > (t^1)^2 + (t^2)^2 + (t^3)^2 > (t^1)^2. (53)

Combining Eq. (53) with t^0 > 0, we conclude that t^0 > t^1, so t^1 - t^0 < 0. Then t^ak_a = k^0(t^1 - t^0) < 0. We have thus shown that v^a = t^a + k^a is timelike.

question_text: Derive equation 5.36 in the notes, showing that rapidities add.
solution_text:
Using the definition of the Λ matrix, we show
⎡cosh φ₁  -sinh φ₁⎤⎡cosh φ₂  -sinh φ₂⎤
Λ[φ₁]Λ[φ₂] = ⎢                ⎥⎢                ⎥
⎣-sinh φ₁  cosh φ₁⎦⎣-sinh φ₂  cosh φ₂⎦

⎡cosh φ₁ cosh φ₂ + sinh φ₁ sinh φ₂  -cosh φ₁ sinh φ₂ - sinh φ₁ cosh φ₂⎤
= ⎢                                                                    ⎥
⎣-sinh φ₁ cosh φ₂ - cosh φ₁ sinh φ₂   sinh φ₁ sinh φ₂ + cosh φ₁ cosh φ₂⎦

⎡cosh(φ₁ + φ₂)  -sinh(φ₁ + φ₂)⎤
= ⎢                              ⎥,
⎣-sinh(φ₁ + φ₂)   cosh(φ₁ + φ₂)⎦

where we have used the angle addition formula for hyperbolic functions to get the last line.
We recognize that the last line is just Λ[φ₁ + φ₂], so we have shown
Λ[φ₁]Λ[φ₂] = Λ[φ₁ + φ₂].

question_text: Practice with coordinate indices. Using Λᵘᵥ, show explicitly that the product of two vectors, UₐVᵇgₐᵦ is a Lorentz scalar.
solution_text:
UₐVᵇgₐᵦ is a Lorentz scalar, so it is invariant under coordinate transformations. In other words, UₐVᵇgₐᵦ computed using the components of Uₐ, Vᵇ, and gₐᵦ in a frame R should agree with Uₐ'Vᵇ'gₐ'ᵦ' computed in a different frame R'. We can check this explicitly by Lorentz transforming the components of Uₐ, Vᵇ, and gₐᵦ using the Λᵘᵥ:

Uᵘ'Vᵛ'gᵘ'ᵛ' = (Λᵘ'ᵘ Uᵘ)(Λᵛ'ᵛ Vᵛ)gᵘ'ᵛ' = UᵘVᵛ(gᵘ'ᵛ' Λᵘ'ᵘ Λᵛ'ᵛ) = UᵘVᵛgᵘᵛ.

question_text: Show that if {xᵘ} are coordinates in some frame, then ∂xᵘ/∂xᵛ = δᵘᵛ
solution_text:
Let {xᵘ} be an arbitrary set of coordinates. Now consider ∂xᵘ/∂xᵛ. If μ = ν, then xᵘ and xᵛ are the same variable, so we have
∂xᵘ/∂xᵛ = 1, if μ = ν.

If μ ≠ ν, then xᵘ and xᵛ are independent variables, so we have
∂xᵘ/∂xᵛ = 0, if μ ≠ ν.

Combining these equations, we get
∂xᵘ/∂xᵛ = δᵘᵛ.

question_text: Show that if {xᵘ} are coordinates in some frame R and {xᵘ'} are coordinates in a frame related to R by a boost, then ∂xᵘ/∂xᵘ' = Λᵘᵤ'
solution_text:
In class, we have learned that we can use Λᵘᵤ' to Lorentz transform coordinates from the primed frame to the unprimed frame (both in Cartesian coordinates), namely
xᵘ = Λᵘᵤ' xᵤ'.

Then we can compute
∂xᵘ/∂xᵘ' = ∂(Λᵘᵛ' xᵛ')/∂xᵘ' = Λᵘᵛ' ∂xᵛ'/∂xᵘ' = Λᵘᵛ' δᵛ'ᵘ' = Λᵘᵘ'.

question_text: Acceleration 4- and 3-vectors (This problem uses material from Monday 10/27 Lecture – wait until after lecture to attempt it!). Using the fact that ā = d𝐯⃗/dt, where 𝐯⃗ is the 3-velocity of an object in some frame with time coordinate t, write out the components of the acceleration 4-vector Aᵘ in an arbitrary reference frame. You may assume that ā and 𝐯⃗ are parallel and you may rotate the spatial axes to simplify the problem. Do not use the Lorentz transformations – only use Aₐ = dUₐ/dτ.
solution_text:
Let's choose our coordinates such that ā and 𝐯⃗ are along the x-axis. Then we just need to consider A⁰ and A¹ since A² = A³ = 0. By definition, we have
A⁰ = dU⁰/dτ = dγ/dτ = (dγ/dt)(dt/dτ) = γ(dγ/dt),
where we have used U⁰ = γ and used the chain rule to get the second equality, and used dt/dτ = γ to get the third equality. Now use γ = 1/√(1-v²), where v is the speed of the object in our coordinate system, and plug in:
A⁰ = (1/√(1-v²))(d/dt)(1/√(1-v²)) = v(dv/dt)/(1-v²)² = γ⁴va.

And, similarly
A¹ = dU¹/dτ = d(γv)/dτ = v(dγ/dτ) + γ(dv/dτ) = vA⁰ + γ(dv/dt) = γ⁴v²a + γ²a = γ⁴a.

question_text: Compute the components of Aₐ in the MCRF, call them Aᵘ', again using only Eq. (1) Aₐ = dUₐ/dτ and not the Lorentz transformations.
solution_text:
Let the primed coordinates be those of the MCRF. In the MCRF, the speed of the object u = 0 at the instance, although du/dt ≠ 0 and is along the x-axis by our choice of the spatial axes. By definition,
A⁰' = dU⁰'/dτ = dγ(u)/dτ = (dγ(u)/du)(du/dt) = 0,
where we have used the fact dt = dτ in a MCRF, and the last equality gives zero because taking the derivative of γ(u) with respect to u gives a prefactor of u which is zero.

Similarly,
A¹' = dU¹'/dτ = d[γ(u)u]/dt = γ(u)(du/dt) = du/dt = a'.

question_text: Now using Lorentz transformations in matrix form, relate Aᵘ to Aᵘ' and use the resulting equation to write a in terms of a'.
solution_text:
We have just computed the components of the acceleration 4-vector in an arbitrary frame and the MCRF. Since the acceleration 4-vector is a 4-vector, we know its components in the two different frames are related by the Lorentz transform
⎡A⁰⎤   ⎡γ   γv⎤⎡A⁰'⎤
⎢   ⎥ = ⎢      ⎥⎢   ⎥.
⎣A¹⎦   ⎣γv  γ ⎦⎣A¹'⎦

Using the values of Aᵘ and Aᵘ' we have computed in the previous parts, this gives two equations
γ⁴va = γva',
γ⁴a = γa'.

Both equations suggest that
a = γ⁻³a'.

question_text: Plugging in aᵢ' = const, solve for coordinates of the object (i.e. its position in spacetime) in terms of a'.
solution_text:
Suppose a' is a constant (i.e., the object is undergoing a constant acceleration), then a = γ⁻³a' tells us that
a = dv/dt = a'(1-v²)³/²,
or equivalently
a'dt = dv/(1-v²)³/²,
which we can integrate to solve for v as a function of t:
t = (1/a')∫ dv/(1-v²)³/²,
which can be done using a trig substitution (e.g., v = sin(w)). After setting v(t = 0) = 0 and rearranging the equation, we get
v = dx/dt = a't/√(1+(a')²t²).

Then we can integrate again to get the position x as a function of time. Setting x(t = 0) = 0, we get
x = (1/a')(√(1+(a')²t²) - 1).

Note that we could also set x(t = 0) = 1/a', in that case we get
x = (1/a')√(1+(a')²t²),
and equivalently,
x² - t² = 1/(a')²,
which is to say the trajectory is a hyperbola with a fixed invariant distance of 1/a' from the origin, consistent with what we have seen in the lecture. The coordinates of the object along the trajectory is simply (t,x) = (t, (1/a')√(1+(a')²t²)).

question_text:
Twin parable in a compact universe. Alice and Barbara are identical twin sisters moving in a universe with the usual Cartesian line element ds² = -dt² + dx² + dy² + dz², which has been periodically identified in Alice's rest frame: (t,x,y,z) ∼ (t,x+L,y,z). Alice observes Barbara to be traveling at constant 3-velocity v⃗ = vx̂.

How much time does Alice measure between Barbara leaving and returning? What is Alice's prediction for Barbara's elapsed time based on time dilation? (if you are confused by this, review the standard twin parable.)
solution_text:
In Alice's frame, Barbara needs to travel a total distance of L to make her way back. Since Alice measures Barbara to travel at speed v, the time elapsed during the trip measured by Alice is simply
Δtₐ = L/v.

Due to time dilation, Alice expects Barbara to measure
Δtᵦ = Δtₐ/γ = L/(γv).

question_text:
Twin parable in a compact universe. Alice and Barbara are identical twin sisters moving in a universe with the usual Cartesian line element ds² = -dt² + dx² + dy² + dz², which has been periodically identified in Alice's rest frame: (t,x,y,z) ∼ (t,x+L,y,z). Alice observes Barbara to be traveling at constant 3-velocity v⃗ = vx̂.

How much time elapses in Barbara's rest frame? Compute this directly in Barbara's frame.
solution_text:
In Alice's frame, the periodic identification is
(t,x,y,z) ∼ (t,x+L,y,z).

To find the periodic identification in Barbara's frame, we need to perform a Lorentz transform on (t,x,y,z)
t̃ = γ(t-vx),
x̃ = γ(x-vt),
ỹ = y,
z̃ = z,
where the coordinates with tildes are the ones in Barbara's frame, and perform a Lorentz transform on (t,x+L,y,z)
t̃' = γ[t-v(x+L)] = t̃-γvL,
x̃' = γ[(x+L)-vt] = x̃+γL,
ỹ' = y,
z̃' = z.

We therefore find the periodic identification in Barbara's frame is
(t̃,x̃,ỹ,z̃) ∼ (t̃-γvL,x̃+γL,ỹ,z̃).

In Barbara's frame, Alice started moving from x̃ = 0 with the speed v in the -x direction. When Alice reached x̃ = -γL, her coordinates in Barbara's frame are
(γL/v,-γL,0,0),
where we are assuming y = ỹ = 0 and z = z̃ = 0. Using Barbara's periodic identification, this is the same as
(γL/v-γvL,0,0,0).

In other words, Alice now has returned to Barbara, and Barbara's clock measures the time elapsed to be
Δt̃ᵦ = γL/v - γvL = L/(γv) = Δtₐ.

question_text:
Twin parable in a compact universe. Alice and Barbara are identical twin sisters moving in a universe with the usual Cartesian line element ds² = -dt² + dx² + dy² + dz², which has been periodically identified in Alice's rest frame: (t,x,y,z) ∼ (t,x+L,y,z). Alice observes Barbara to be traveling at constant 3-velocity v⃗ = vx̂.

Argue that Alice's frame is special, and that a given observer can in fact detect whether or not they are in such a special frame. Explain why this is not in violation of the fact that no coordinate system in Minkowski space is special.
solution_text: The length of the compact direction (x-axis) in Alice's frame is L, while it is γL in any other inertial observer's frame. Alice can compare the length of her compact direction with those of others. She then concludes that her frame is special because the length of the compact direction in her frame is minimal. This is in no violation of the fact that no coordinate system in Minkowski space is special because the periodic identification breaks the translation symmetry of the Minkowski space.

question_text: Suppose that you are at the top of a rigid rocket which is half a light year tall. The rocket is accelerating such that your proper acceleration is 1g. Show that 1g is very close to 1 lightyear/year², and use this value for g in this problem.
solution_text:
Let's verify this directly by computing the value for g
1g = G*M_earth/R_earth² = (6.67·10⁻¹¹ m³kg⁻¹s⁻²) × (5.97·10²⁴ kg) / (6.37·10⁶ m)² = 9.81 ms⁻²
= 9.81 m/s² × (1 ly)/(3·10⁸ m/s × 3.15·10⁷ s) × (3.15·10⁷ s / 1 yr)²
= 1.03 ly/yr² ≈ 1 ly/yr².

question_text: Suppose that you are at the top of a rigid rocket which is half a light year tall. The rocket is accelerating such that your proper acceleration is 1g ≈ 1 lightyear/year². What is the proper acceleration at the bottom of the rocket?
solution_text:
We have seen how this is done in the lecture, where we derived the proper acceleration of a friend being a constant distance L₀ away from the top of a rocket. Using the same formula to calculate the proper acceleration at the bottom of the rocket, we have
α_b = α_t / (1 - L₀α_t/c²),
where I have inserted factors of c to make the dimension correct. Plugging in α_t = 1 ly/yr², L₀ = 1/2 ly, and c = 1 ly/yr, we find α_b = 2 ly/yr².

question_text: Suppose that you are at the top of a rigid rocket which is half a light year tall. The rocket is accelerating such that your proper acceleration is 1g ≈ 1 lightyear/year². For every second that passes for a clock at the bottom of the rocket, how much time passes for you?
solution_text: We have seen in class that the top and bottom of the rocket share the same line of simultaneity, so the measurement of proper time happens over the same range of boost parameter, namely we have α_t τ_t = α_b τ_b. Using the trajectories t_t(τ_t) = (1/α_t)sinh(α_t τ_t), x_t(τ_t) = (1/α_t)cosh(α_t τ_t) and t_b(τ_b) = (1/α_b)sinh(α_b τ_b), x_b(τ_b) = (1/α_b)cosh(α_b τ_b), and the fact that simultaneous events lie on lines through the origin, we get tanh(α_t τ_t') = tanh(α_b τ_b'), so α_t τ_t' = α_b τ_b', which means τ_t' = (α_b/α_t)τ_b' = 2τ_b'. In other words, every second that passes for a clock at the bottom of the rocket, two seconds passes for you!

question_text: Suppose that you are at the top of a rigid rocket. The rocket is accelerating such that your proper acceleration is 1g ≈ 1 lightyear/year². What is the proper acceleration of the bottom of the rocket if the rocket is 99/100 of a light year tall?
solution_text: We use the equation α_b' = α_t / (1 - L₀'α_t/c²) again. Using L₀' = (99/100) ly this time and α_t = 1 ly/yr², we get α_b' = 100 ly/yr².

question_text: Consider a set of rods of length R on a hub, spinning relative to some inertial frame. Builders are sent out onto the rods to build a platform on top of the rods while the whole structure is spinning. Assume each builder is stationed at just one place on the "wheel" and does all their work there. They are not all stationed at the ends of the rods. Take the outside of the wheel to be moving at 0.8c relative to the inertial frame. Suppose that the builders at a given distance D from the center measure the circumference of the circle they make on the platform. How does this circumference compare with the distance they measure to the hub?
solution_text: In class, we have seen that a merry-go-round of radius R in an inertial frame still has radius R in the frame that is spinning with the merry-go-round. However, the circumference measured in the spinning frame will be 2πRγ. Applying this to the problem, we conclude that a builder at a distance D from the hub measures the circumference to be C(D) = 2πDγ(v(D)), where v(D) is the tangential velocity, which can be found from the angular velocity by v(D) = ωD = (0.8c/R)D. So, C(D) = 2πD / √(1-(0.8c/R)²D²).

question_text: Consider a set of rods of length R on a hub, spinning relative to some inertial frame. Builders are sent out onto the rods to build a platform on top of the rods while the whole structure is spinning. Assume each builder is stationed at just one place on the "wheel" and does all their work there. Take the outside of the wheel to be moving at 0.8c relative to the inertial frame. Suppose that all of the builders were the same age in the morning before work. When they get together at the end of the day (as determined in the inertial frame), will they all still be the same age? If not, how will their ages differ as a function of their distance from the hub?
solution_text: Consider a builder located at point p on the platform. We can find a MCRF of p, and the builder's proper time dτ is equal to the infinitesimal time element dt_MCRF at that instant. We know how time dilation works between the MCRF and the ground inertial reference frame: dt = γ(v)dt_MCRF, where dt is the infinitesimal time element in the ground inertial reference frame. Then we know at the instant of p, the builder's proper time and the time in the ground frame are related by dt = γ(v)dτ. Due to symmetry around the hub, this relation holds everywhere. Therefore, we can relate the time passed in the ground frame with time measured by a builder located at a distance D from the hub as τ(D) = √(1-(0.8c/R)²D²) × t. In other words, after one day has passed in the ground frame, only √(1-(0.8c/R)²D²) of a day has passed for the builder. The further away the builder from the hub, the younger they will be at the end of the day in the ground frame.

question_text: Consider a set of rods of length R on a hub, spinning relative to some inertial frame.
