Builders are sent out onto the rods to build a platform on top of the rods while the whole structure is spinning. We bring the spinning structure to a stop after the platform is built. Will it still fit together without having to buckle or tear? If not, what will happen to it?
solution_text: When builders are constructing the platform at a distance D away from the hub, the total proper length of all materials needed to pave around the hub is just the circumference, which is 2πDγ as seen in part (a). At the instant when the platform stops, the materials that were originally at a distance D away from the hub are still at a distance D away from the hub, but the circumference now reduces to 2πD. At the same time, the proper length of the materials does not change. Since 2πDγ > 2πD, there is too little room for all the material at a distance D from the hub. Therefore, the platform will have to expand outward for the material to fit.

question_text: In this problem you'll derive the circumference of the relativistic merry-go-round using a different method, by finding coordinate system adapted for the reference frame of the edge of the relativistic merry-go-round. Starting with the inertial ground reference frame, write down the ground rest frame line element in spherical coordinates t,r,θ (you may work in three spacetime dimensions for this problem).
solution_text: We have already found the line element in spherical coordinates in pset 4 problem 2. Here we only need three dimensions, so the line element is ds² = -dt² + dr² + r²dθ².

question_text: In this problem you'll derive the circumference of the relativistic merry-go-round using a different method, by finding coordinate system adapted for the reference frame of the edge of the relativistic merry-go-round. If the merry-go-round is spinning clockwise at angular velocity ω, write down an equation for the worldline of an observer on the edge of the merry-go-round, parametrized by the time coordinate t in the inertial reference frame (i.e. write down an equation for r(t), θ(t), etc).
solution_text: Suppose the merry-go-round has a radius r and the observer started at an angle θ at t = 0, their coordinates in the ground inertial reference frame parametrized by the time t is: t = t, r(t) = r, θ(t) = θ - ωt.

question_text: In this problem you'll derive the circumference of the relativistic merry-go-round using a different method, by finding coordinate system adapted for the reference frame of the edge of the relativistic merry-go-round. Define new coordinates t' = t, r' = r(t), θ' = θ(t). Write down the line element in terms of these primed coordinates. Use the line element to write down the components of the metric. Make sure that the metric that you write down is symmetric under the exchange of component indices.
solution_text: With the new definition of the coordinates, we have t' = t, r' = r, θ' = θ - ωt. Under this new coordinates, the line element ds² = -dt² + dr² + r²dθ² becomes ds² = -(dt')² + (dr')² + (r')²(dθ' + ωdt')² = -[1-(r')²ω²](dt')² + (dr')² + (r')²(dθ')² + 2(r')²ωdθ'dt'. Since ds² = g_μν dx^μ dx^ν, we can compare to find the metric components in this coordinate system. We find the non-zero components are g₀₀ = -[1-(r')²ω²], g₁₁ = 1, g₂₂ = (r')², g₀₂ = g₂₀ = (r')²ω, where index numbers 0, 1, and 2 represent t', r', and θ', respectively. All the remaining components are zero. Note that we have ensured the symmetry of the metric by enforcing g₀₂ = g₂₀ = (r')²ω.

question_text: In this problem you'll derive the circumference of the relativistic merry-go-round using a different method, by finding coordinate system adapted for the reference frame of the edge of the relativistic merry-go-round. To compute the circumference, we must measure the length of the edge of the disk at a single moment of simultaneity. That means that we must integrate θ' from 0 to 2π at a moment of simultaneity. Explain why t' = constant is not a moment of simultaneity for the merry-go-round.
solution_text: Intuitively, since we have defined t' = t, it is actually the same as the time measured by an inertial observer in the ground frame. Since the merry-go-round frame is rotating relative to the ground frame, the intuition of relativity that we have built so far tells us that an observer rotating along with the merry-go-round should experience time differently than an inertial observer in the ground frame, so events that are simultaneous in the ground frame are not necessarily simultaneous in the merry-go-round frame. To see this more precisely, notice that we found non-zero off-diagonal terms in the metric, namely g₀₂ = g₂₀ = (r')²ω. This term mixes space and time, meaning that whenever we advance in θ' along the circle, we also advance in t'. So to measure a single simultaneous length, we have to synchronize all the infinitesimal lengths along the circle.

question_text: In this problem you'll derive the circumference of the relativistic merry-go-round using a different method, by finding coordinate system adapted for the reference frame of the edge of the relativistic merry-go-round. To find a plane of simultaneity for the disk, we need to synchronize all of the points around the disk. Let Alice and Bob be two observers located very close to one another on the edge of the merry-go-round. Alice's θ' coordinate is θ'_A and Bob's θ' coordinate is θ'_A + dθ'. We can synchronize Alice's and Bob's clocks by sending a light ray from Alice to Bob and then having Bob reflect it back to Alice. The observers can then synchronize their clocks using the halfway point of the trip. Let dt'₁ be the coordinate time elapsed for a light ray to travel from Alice to Bob, and let dt'₂ be the coordinate time elapsed for a light ray to travel from Bob to Alice. Alice computes half the trip time by taking the total time she waits for the light ray to arrive back and dividing it by 2. Bob computes the halfway point by measuring the time at which he receives the light ray. What are the coordinates of Alice's halfway point? What are the coordinates of Bob's halfway point? If these halfway points are to be identical, what must be the time lag dt' between Alice and Bob in terms of dt'₁ and dt'₂?
solution_text: Alice computes half the trip time by taking the total time she waits for the light ray to arrive back and dividing it by 2. This suggests the halfway point for Alice is ((dt'₁ + dt'₂)/2, θ'_A), where we have omitted the r' coordinate since all observers we consider are at the edge of the merry-go-round (r' = r). Bob computes the halfway point by measuring the time at which he receives the light ray. This suggests the halfway point for Bob is (dt'₁, θ'_A + dθ'). Alice and Bob's halfway points are simultaneous. This means two infinitesimally close clocks on the disk are synchronized if the coordinate time difference between them is dt' = dt'₁ - (dt'₁ + dt'₂)/2 = (dt'₁ - dt'₂)/2.

question_text: In this problem you'll derive the circumference of the relativistic merry-go-round using a different method, by finding coordinate system adapted for the reference frame of the edge of the relativistic merry-go-round. Using the fact that ds² = 0 for a light ray, relate the expression in the previous part to dθ' in terms of ω and r. To compute the circumference, we now know that for every dθ' that we sum we must also add the dt' above. Use the expression you derived above to compute the circumference. Do you find 2πRγ like we did in lecture?
solution_text: First, consider the null path from (0, θ'_A) to (dt'₁, θ'_A + dθ'), using ds² = 0 and the line element, with r' = r: -[1-r²ω²](dt'₁)² + r²(dθ')² + 2r²ωdθ'dt'₁ = 0. Solving this quadratic equation: dt'₁ = rdθ'(ωr + 1)/(1 - r²ω²). For the light path from Bob to Alice, from (dt'₁, θ'_A + dθ') to (dt'₁ + dt'₂, θ'_A): -[1-r²ω²](dt'₂)² + r²(dθ')² - 2r²ωdθ'dt'₂ = 0. Solving: dt'₂ = rdθ'(1 - ωr)/(1 - r²ω²). Plugging into dt' = (dt'₁ - dt'₂)/2: dt' = ωr²dθ'/(1 - ω²r²). Now integrating along θ' to find the circumference: C = ∫√{-[1-r²ω²](dt')² + r²(dθ')² + 2r²ωdt'dθ'} = ∫√{ω²r⁴/(1-ω²r²) + r²(dθ')²}(dθ')² = ∫r/√(1-ω²r²) dθ' = r/√(1-ω²r²) ∫₀²π dθ' = 2πrγ, where γ = 1/√(1-ω²r²). We have reached the same conclusion as the one we have already seen in class.

question_text: Astronauts in a spaceship traveling on a straight path (as measured by the earth's rest frame, which is here assumed to be an inertial reference frame) past the earth at speed v = 1/2. They would like to tune into a radio station at 700 kHz. To what frequency should they tune the instant that they are closest to earth as measured in the earth frame?
solution_text: When the astronaut is closest to the earth as measured in the earth frame, the radio waves are transmitted to the spaceship along a direction that is perpendicular to the trajectory of the spaceship. This is the case of transverse Doppler shift discussed in class. Using the formula given in the lecture notes, we get f_o = γf_s = (1/√(1-v²))f_s = f_s/√(1-(1/2)²) = 808 kHz.

question_text: Astronauts in a spaceship traveling on a straight path (as measured by the earth's rest frame, which is here assumed to be an inertial reference frame) past the earth at speed v = 1/2. They would like to tune into a radio station at 700 kHz. If at the closest approach above the distance from the earth to the spaceship is L, how should they tune their frequency as a function of proper time measured by the astronauts after the moment in part (a)?
solution_text: We can solve this problem by staying in the earth's frame, knowing that the time on earth t and the proper time of the astronaut τ are simply related by t = γτ. Suppose the astronaut was at her closest approach to the earth at time t = 0. Then at a later time t > 0, the astronaut has traveled a distance vt, measured in the earth frame, so the angle between the radio waves' trajectory and the spaceship's velocity are given by cos(θ_s) = vt/√((vt)² + L²). We can use the formula of relativistic Doppler shift to find f_o(t) = γf_s(1 - v cos θ_s) = γf_s(1 - v²t/√((vt)² + L²)). Now using t = γτ, we get f_o(τ) = γf_s(1 - v²γτ/√((vγτ)² + L²)).

question_text: Based on considerations of 4-momentum alone, determine whether an electron and a photon can collide and result in a single electron and no photon.
solution_text: Suppose it is possible for an electron and a photon to collide and result in a single electron. Then the conservation of 4-momentum requires p^μ + q^μ = (p')^μ, where p^μ, q^μ, and (p')^μ represent the 4-momenta of the electron and photon before collision, and the electron after collision, respectively. Since the 4-vectors on the left and right-hand side are equal, the invariant product with themselves are also equal: (p^μ + q^μ)(p_μ + q_μ) = (p')^μ(p')_μ. Expanding: p^μp_μ + p^μq_μ + p_μq^μ + q^μq_μ = (p')^μ(p')_μ. In the rest frame of the electron, p^μp_μ = -m_e², and for a photon q^μq_μ = 0. Therefore: -m_e² + 2p^μq_μ + 0 = -m_e², which gives p^μq_μ = 0. In the rest frame of the electron before collision, this becomes -m_e E_γ = 0, indicating E_γ = 0. Therefore, there is no photon that can collide with an electron and only result in an electron.

question_text: Consider a particle with rest mass m. Suppose the particle is moving relative to our inertial reference frame at some speed (moving away from us) in the x₁ direction. In our rest frame, the particle's energy is γm. What is the particle's 4-momentum in our reference frame in terms of γ and m (not in terms of v)?
solution_text: By definition, the 4-momentum of a particle is p^μ = mU^μ, and the 4-velocity U^μ has components U^μ = γ(1, V₁, V₂, V₃), so p^μ = mγ(1, V₁, V₂, V₃). Since the particle is moving along the x-axis only, this becomes p^μ = mγ(1, V, 0, 0). Since γ = 1/√(1-V²), we can write V in terms of γ: V = (1-γ⁻²)^(1/2), so the x component of the 4-momentum becomes p^x = mγV = mγ(1-γ⁻²)^(1/2) = (γ²-1)^(1/2)m. Therefore, the 4-momentum is p^μ = m(γ, (γ²-1)^(1/2), 0, 0).

question_text: A particle of rest mass m₁ decays into a particle of rest mass m₂ and a photon. In the rest frame of the particle produced in the decay, what is the energy of the photon in terms of the rest masses m₁ and m₂?
solution_text: By the conservation of 4-momentum, we have (p₁)^μ = (p₂)^μ + q^μ, where (p₁)^μ, (p₂)^μ, q^μ represent the 4-momenta of the particle with rest mass m₁, the particle with rest mass m₂, and the photon, respectively. We can compute the invariant product of both sides: (p₁)^μ(p₁)_μ = [(p₂)^μ + q^μ][(p₂)_μ + q_μ] = (p₂)^μ(p₂)_μ + 2(p₂)^μq_μ + q^μq_μ. We have (p₁)^μ(p₁)_μ = -m₁², (p₂)^μ(p₂)_μ = -m₂², and q^μq_μ = 0. We evaluate (p₂)^μq_μ in the rest frame of the particle with rest mass m₂ and get (p₂)^μq_μ = -m₂E_γ, where E_γ is the photon energy in the rest frame of the produced particle. This gives: -m₁² = -m₂² - 2m₂E_γ, so E_γ = (m₁² - m₂²)c²/(2m₂).

question_text: Cosmic rays consist of high energy protons emitted by astrophysical sources far from Earth. There is a theoretical limit to the maximum energy of cosmic rays from deep space resulting from the cosmic microwave background radiation. If a proton has sufficient energy, it can scatter off of a microwave background photon to form, for example, a neutron and a π meson: p + γ → π⁺ + n. Find the maximum proton energy that can travel through deep space. Take the cosmic microwave background photons to have energies of 6·10⁻⁴ eV (recall that 1 eV = 1.602176565·10⁻¹⁹ J), and using 140 MeV and 938 MeV for the π meson and proton rest energies, respectively. Assume that the neutron and proton rest energies are the same.
solution_text: By the conservation of 4-momentum: (p_p)^μ + q^μ = (p_π)^μ + (p_n)^μ. Computing the invariant product of both sides: [(p_p)^μ + q^μ][(p_p)_μ + q_μ] = [(p_π)^μ + (p_n)^μ][(p_π)_μ + (p_n)_μ]. In the limiting case of the lowest possible proton energy for the reaction to occur, the π meson and neutron are produced at rest in their center of momentum frame, so [(p_π)^μ + (p_n)^μ][(p_π)_μ + (p_n)_μ] = -(m_π + m_n)². The left-hand side gives: [(p_p)^μ + q^μ][(p_p)_μ + q_μ] = -m_p² + 2(p_p)^μq_μ. In the limiting case, the proton must collide head-on with the photon in the lab frame. With the photon traveling in the -x direction and the proton in the +x direction, we get (p_p)^μq_μ = -2E_pE_γ. Therefore: m_p² + 4E_pE_γ = m_π² + m_n² + 2m_πm_n. Using m_n = m_p and solving for the proton energy: E_p = (2m_πm_p + m_π²)c⁴/(4E_γ). Plugging in numerical values gives E_p = 1.2·10²⁰ eV.

question_text: (10 points) (SR) Derive the Lorentz transformations for E⃗ and B⃗ under a boost in the +x direction. Note: the problems marked SR are to be done using only special relativity – i.e. ignoring the gravitational field.
solution_text: We know that all the components of E⃗ and B⃗ are packed into the tensor F_μν, and we know how to transform the components of a tensor to a different reference frame using the Lorentz transform: F_μ'ν' = Λ^μ_μ' Λ^ν_ν' F_μν, where the Lorentz transform matrix along the +x direction (technically the inverse Lorentz transform matrix since we are transforming a (0,2) tensor, similar to how we transform a one-form) is given by the 4x4 matrix with γ, γv terms. The safest way to compute this is simply to sum up μ and ν. For example, to find out E'_1, we can compute F_0'1' = Λ^0_0' Λ^1_1' F_01 + Λ^1_0' Λ^0_1' F_10 = -γ²E_1 + γ²v²E_1 = -γ²(1-v²)E_1 = -E_1. Then E'_1 = -F_0'1' = E_1. Similarly, performing calculations for the rest, we find: E'_1 = E_1, E'_2 = γ(E_2 - vB_3), E'_3 = γ(E_3 + vB_2), and B'_1 = B_1, B'_2 = γ(vE_3 + B_2), B'_3 = γ(B_3 - vE_2).

question_text: (10 points) (SR) Write down the components of *F_μν = (1/2)ϵ_μνλρF^λρ. Note: the problems marked SR are to be done using only special relativity – i.e. ignoring the gravitational field.
solution_text: Recall the Levi-Civita symbol gives 1 if the indices are even permutation of {0, 1, 2, 3} and gives -1 if the indices are odd permutations of {0, 1, 2, 3} and is 0 if there are repeated indices. Then the computation is straightforward, and we get *F_μν = (1/2)ϵ_μνλρF_λρ = [[0, B_1, B_2, B_3], [-B_1, 0, -E_3, E_2], [-B_2, E_3, 0, -E_1], [-B_3, -E_2, E_1, 0]].

question_text: (10 points) Use the mass and radius of the earth to calculate how much faster a clock in Denver runs than a clock in Washington, D.C. does. Denver is 1600m higher than Washington, D.C.
solution_text: From the lecture notes, we have seen that the ratio of proper times between a clock located at A and a clock located at B on the earth (with different altitudes) is given by Δτ_A/Δτ_B = Exp[-(Gm_E/c²)(1/r_B - 1/r_A)]. Assuming D.C. is located at 0 altitude, we take r_DC = r_earth = 6378000m. Denver is 1600m above D.C., so r_Denver = 6379600m. Plugging these into the equation above, we get Δτ_Denver/Δτ_DC = Exp[Gm_E/c²(1/r_DC - 1/r_Denver)] = Exp(2×10^-13) = 1+2×10^-13. This means that a clock in Denver runs faster by 2 × 10^-13 s for every second passed in D.C.

question_text: (20 points) Consider a new parametrization of a geodesic: instead of τ, we will parametrize it with some new parameter σ. Show that the geodesic equation in terms of such an arbitrary parameter can be written: d²x^β/dσ² + Γ^β_μν (dx^μ/dσ)(dx^ν/dσ) = d/dσ[ln(dτ/dσ)] dx^β/dσ
solution_text: The original geodesic equation is d²x^β/dτ² + Γ^β_μν (dx^μ/dτ)(dx^ν/dτ) = 0. Now we want to parametrize the geodesic with a different parameter σ. By the chain rule, we know that dx^β/dτ = (dx^β/dσ)(dσ/dτ). Applying this to the original equation and working through the chain rule derivatives: d/dτ[(dx^β/dσ)(dσ/dτ)] + Γ^β_μν (dx^μ/dσ)(dσ/dτ)(dx^ν/dσ)(dσ/dτ) = 0. This becomes d²x^β/dσ²(dσ/dτ)² + (dx^β/dσ)(d²σ/dτ²) + Γ^β_μν (dx^μ/dσ)(dx^ν/dσ)(dσ/dτ)² = 0. Rearranging: d²x^β/dσ² + Γ^β_μν (dx^μ/dσ)(dx^ν/dσ) = -(dσ/dτ)^-2 (d²σ/dτ²)(dx^β/dσ). Now examining the right-hand side term -(dσ/dτ)^-2 d²σ/dτ². Setting u = dσ/dτ, this becomes -u^-2 du/dτ = -(1/u)(1/u)(du/dτ) = -(1/u)(d/dτ)ln(u) = (d/dτ)ln(1/u) = (d/dσ)ln(dτ/dσ). Therefore: d²x^β/dσ² + Γ^β_μν (dx^μ/dσ)(dx^ν/dσ) = d/dσ[ln(dτ/dσ)] dx^β/dσ.

question_text: (5 points) Consider a new parametrization of a geodesic: instead of τ, we will parametrize it with some new parameter σ. From part (a), the geodesic equation in terms of such an arbitrary parameter can be written: d²x^β/dσ² + Γ^β_μν (dx^μ/dσ)(dx^ν/dσ) = d/dσ[ln(dτ/dσ)] dx^β/dσ. Show that if σ = aτ + b for some constants a and b, we get back the usual geodesic equation we derived in class.
solution_text: Note that the equation from part (a) is already the usual geodesic equation, except the term on the right-hand side. In other words, if we have the right-hand side term to be 0, then we get the usual geodesic equation back. The right-hand side term being 0 implies that ln(dτ/dσ) = constant, which means dτ/dσ = constant. In other words, we have σ = aτ + b. Therefore, if we have σ = aτ + b, we will get the usual geodesic equation back.

question_text: (15 points) Prove that U^a U_a = -1 for timelike geodesics.
solution_text: By definition, U^α U_α = g_αβ U^α U^β. We can choose our coordinates {y^α'} to be the local inertial coordinates that are centered around a point p located on the geodesic. Then in the vicinity of p, the spacetime metric is flat up to second order corrections in the size of the region. Namely, g_α'β' = η_α'β' + O(y^α' - y^α'(p))². In these coordinates, U^α' U_α' = g_α'β' U^α' U^β' = η_α'β' U^α' U^β' + O(y^α' - y^α'(p))². When we evaluate U^α' U_α' at p, then y^α' - y^α'(p) goes to 0, so U^α' U_α'|_p = η_α'β' U^α' U^β'|_p = -1, which we know from special relativity, and this equation is exact. Note that our choice of point p is arbitrary. For any point p' along the geodesic, we can find a local inertial coordinates centered around that point, such that U^α' U_α'|_p' = -1. In addition, U^α' U_α' is an invariant quantity, its value is independent of the coordinate system that we choose. Therefore we conclude that U^α U_α = -1 for a timelike geodesic in general.

question_text: (15 points) Consider the following metric: ds² = -(1 + r²/ℓ²)dt² + dr²/(1 + r²/ℓ²) + r²dΩ², where ℓ is some fixed length. Use the fact that every null curve in two dimensions is a null geodesic to solve for the trajectory of a radial light ray (a light ray that travels along paths of constant θ and φ).
solution_text: Using the given metric, we know that a radial (dΩ = 0) null curve satisfies ds² = 0 = -(1 + r²/ℓ²)dt² + dr²/(1 + r²/ℓ²). The problem tells us that every null curve in two dimensions is a null geodesic, so this equation is already the geodesic equation. We can then solve this equation to find the trajectory of a radial light ray. Rearranging, we get (dr/dt)² = (1 + r²/ℓ²)². For the solution of an outgoing lightray (dr > 0), we get dr/dt = 1 + r²/ℓ². In other words, dt = dr/(1 + r²/ℓ²), which has the solution t(r) = A + ℓtan⁻¹(r/ℓ), where A is an integration constant. Suppose the light ray was emitted from the origin at t = 0, then t(r = 0) = 0, so A = 0. Then our solution is t(r) = ℓtan⁻¹(r/ℓ).

question_text: (10 points) Consider the following metric: ds² = -(1 + r²/ℓ²)dt² + dr²/(1 + r²/ℓ²) + r²dΩ², where ℓ is some fixed length. From part (a), the trajectory of a radial light ray is t(r) = ℓtan⁻¹(r/ℓ). Suppose you are a stationary observer at r = 0, and you turn on a flashlight at t = 0. At what value of t would the radial light rays from your flashlight reach r = ∞? Call this time t_∞. What is the value of r at 2t_∞?
solution_text: t_∞ = lim_{r→∞} ℓtan⁻¹(r/ℓ) = ℓ(π/2). Now we invert t(r) and have r(t) = ℓtan(t/ℓ), so r(2t_∞) = ℓtan(π) = 0.

question_text: (10 points) Consider the following metric: ds² = -(1 + r²/ℓ²)dt² + dr²/(1 + r²/ℓ²) + r²dΩ², where ℓ is some fixed length. From parts (a) and (b), we found that t_∞ = ℓπ/2 and r(2t_∞) = 0. What do you see at your original position r = 0, t = 2t_∞?
solution_text: The last result of part (b) says that at time t = 2t_∞, the light ray is at the origin r = 0. This means you see the light that was emitted at t = 0 has returned to where you are (the origin).

question_text: (15 points) Consider the following metric: ds² = -(1 + r²/ℓ²)dt² + dr²/(1 + r²/ℓ²) + r²dΩ², where ℓ is some fixed length. This metric does not depend on t. It turns out that this means that the following quantity is constant along timelike geodesics: -(1 + r²/ℓ²)(dt/dτ), where τ is a proper time. Call this quantity C. Use this and the fact that U^a U_a = -1 for a timelike geodesic to show that timelike geodesics cannot reach r = ∞ but instead have a maximum at some finite value of r. [hint: dr/dτ = 0 at a maximum]. Find the value of r at the maximum. Note: this means that timelike geodesics move like oscillating boomerangs, constrained to stay away from r = ∞! This spacetime is often said to be like 'gravity in a box': gravity compels observers to stay near r = 0.
solution_text: For a radial geodesic condition, g_μν U^μ U^ν = -1 simplifies to -(1 + r²/ℓ²)(dt/dτ)² + (1 + r²/ℓ²)⁻¹(dr/dτ)² = -1. Rearrange the equation above, we get (dr/dτ)² = (1 + r²/ℓ²)²(dt/dτ)² - (1 + r²/ℓ²). Now using the definition of C, we get (dr/dτ)² = C² - 1 - r²/ℓ². At the maximum dr/dτ = 0, we have r²/ℓ² = C² - 1, which is finite as long as ℓ and C are finite. The maximum distance is r_max = ℓ√(C² - 1).

question_text: (15 points) Timelike geodesics maximize proper time while null geodesics have zero elapsed proper time. Do spacelike geodesics maximize proper distance? Minimize proper distance? Neither?
solution_text: A spacelike geodesic neither maximizes nor minimizes the proper distance. To show this, we just need to find a spacelike geodesic between two points, and show that there exists a path connecting the two points which has a longer proper distance than that of the spacelike geodesic and find another path connecting the two points which has a shorter proper distance than that of the spacelike geodesic. We can simply work in a local inertial coordinate system and focus on the region where we can safely ignore the deviations from flat spacetime. We know that in flat spacetime, geodesics are straight lines, so if we pick two spacelike separated points, then the spacelike geodesic between the two points is just the line of simultaneity between them. Now we can perturb the line of simultaneity to get a new path that connects the two points but restricting the new path to be on the same plane of simultaneity that contains the two points. In other words, along the new path, dt = 0, so its proper length can only be larger than that of the spacelike geodesic. This shows that a spacelike geodesic does not maximize the proper length. Now instead, we can replace some section of the spacelike geodesic with null paths so the perturbation is out of the plane of simultaneity. Since null paths have zero proper distance, the new path that we just created has shorter proper distance than that of the spacelike geodesic, so we conclude that spacelike geodesics do not minimize proper distance.

question_text: Black Holes with a Negative Mass: suppose we wanted to try to make sense of a "fifth region" of Schwarzschild, where r < 0. This is not accessible to any observers since r = 0 is a genuine singularity – you can't travel through it. Nevertheless, we can still imagine this being a different universe altogether, not accessible from the first one in any way. Show that taking r < 0 is identical to taking M < 0 with a radial direction that takes positive values.
solution_text: The Schwarzschild metric is ds² = -(1-2G_N M/r)dt² + (1-2G_N M/r)⁻¹dr² + r²dΩ² from which we see that changing r to -r is equivalent to changing M to -M because in both cases the metric becomes ds² = -(1+2G_N M/r)dt² + (1+2G_N M/r)⁻¹dr² + r²dΩ². Equation (2) is the Schwarzschild metric with a negative mass, but since we have explicitly written out the minus sign of the mass, in Eq. (2) we have M > 0 and r > 0.

question_text: Black Holes with a Negative Mass: suppose we wanted to try to make sense of a "fifth region" of Schwarzschild, where r < 0. This is not accessible to any observers since r = 0 is a genuine singularity – you can't travel through it. Nevertheless, we can still imagine this being a different universe altogether, not accessible from the first one in any way. Is r = 0 a genuine singularity in negative mass Schwarzschild? (Hint: this does not require any calculations beyond what is already done in the lecture notes.)
solution_text: r = 0 is still a genuine singularity. In the lecture notes, we showed that for the Schwarzschild metric, the Kretschmann scalar K = 48G²_N M²/r⁶ blows up at r = 0, so we concluded that r = 0 is a genuine singularity for the Schwarzschild metric. In the metric Eq. (2), the only difference from the Schwarzschild metric is that we have changed M to -M, but the Kretschmann scalar in Eq. (3) is proportional to M², so the Kretschmann scalar is unchanged in the new metric. We therefore conclude that r = 0 is still a genuine singularity.

question_text: Black Holes with a Negative Mass: suppose we wanted to try to make sense of a "fifth region" of Schwarzschild, where r < 0. This is not accessible to any observers since r = 0 is a genuine singularity – you can't travel through it. Nevertheless, we can still imagine this being a different universe altogether, not accessible from the first one in any way. Is there a region where t is the timelike coordinate?
solution_text: Examining the new metric, we find that -(1+2G_N M/r) is always smaller than zero. Therefore, t is everywhere timelike.

question_text: Black Holes with a Negative Mass: suppose we wanted to try to make sense of a "fifth region" of Schwarzschild, where r < 0. This is not accessible to any observers since r = 0 is a genuine singularity – you can't travel through it. Nevertheless, we can still imagine this being a different universe altogether, not accessible from the first one in any way. Is there a region where r is the timelike coordinate?
solution_text: Similarly, (1+2G_N M/r)⁻¹ is always larger than zero, so r is everywhere spacelike and never timelike.

question_text: Black Holes with a Negative Mass: suppose we wanted to try to make sense of a "fifth region" of Schwarzschild, where r < 0. This is not accessible to any observers since r = 0 is a genuine singularity – you can't travel through it. Nevertheless, we can still imagine this being a different universe altogether, not accessible from the first one in any way. Show that an outgoing null trajectory can always reach infinite future time, no matter what value of r it starts at. (Here by outgoing we mean moving towards larger r).
solution_text: For a radial null trajectory, Eq. (2) becomes 0 = -(1+2G_N M/r)dt² + (1+2G_N M/r)⁻¹dr², from which we get (for an outward trajectory where r increases with t) dt/dr = 1/(1+2G_N M/r). After integration, we get t(r) = r-2G_N M ln(2G_N M +r)+C, where C is the integration constant. Suppose the light ray is emitted from r = R at t = 0, then the solution becomes t(r) = (r-R)+2G_N M ln((2G_N M +R)/(2G_N M +r)). Since r approaches infinity faster than ln(r), we see that as r → ∞, t → ∞.

question_text: Black Holes with a Negative Mass: suppose we wanted to try to make sense of a "fifth region" of Schwarzschild, where r < 0. This is not accessible to any observers since r = 0 is a genuine singularity – you can't travel through it. Nevertheless, we can still imagine this being a different universe altogether, not accessible from the first one in any way. Is the spacetime asymptotically flat at r → ∞?
solution_text: Setting r → ∞, Eq. (2) becomes ds² = -dt² +dr² +r²dΩ², which is flat spacetime.

question_text: Clocks in Schwarzschild: Consider a static clock at r > R_s (here static means static in Schwarzschild coordinates: fixed r,θ,φ). How fast does this clock tick compared with a clock at r = ∞?
solution_text: We can use the Schwarzschild metric Eq. (1) to find the proper time of a static clock sitting at some r > R_s: ds² = -(1-2G_N M/r)dt² = -dτ², so we get dτ = √(1-2G_N M/r) dt. We recall that t corresponds to the proper time of a clock sitting infinitely far away, so a clock sitting at r ticks faster by a factor of √(1-2G_N M/r).

question_text: Clocks in Schwarzschild: Consider a static clock at r > R_s (here static means static in Schwarzschild coordinates: fixed r,θ,φ). Suppose that you are standing on the surface of the sun. How fast does your clock tick relative to a far away clock (i.e. at r = ∞)? The mass of the sun is 2·10³⁰kg and the radius at the surface of the sun is 7·10⁸m.
solution_text: Using G = 6.67·10⁻¹¹m³kg⁻¹s⁻², M = 2·10³⁰kg, and r = 7·10⁸m, we get √(1-2G_N M/r) = 0.999998.

question_text: Falling into a Black Hole: Suppose that you are dropped out of a spaceship outside of a black hole and that you fall in. To answer the questions below, use the fact that the spacetime near the horizon of a black hole is just like the region of flat spacetime near an acceleration horizon. The people on the rocket watch the light rays you send out through a telescope. Do they see you age slowly or quickly? When do they see you cross the horizon? If they first sit and watch for a while, is it possible for them to come and rescue you?
solution_text: People on the rocket will receive the light signal from you at increasingly longer intervals measured by a clock on the spaceship. This means they see you age more slowly. Note that the light signal that you emit at the horizon will only reach the spaceship at the infinite future. This means people on the spaceship will never see you cross the horizon. If they first sit and watch for while, it is still possible for them to come and rescue you, as long as they can reach you before you have crossed the horizon.

question_text: Describe one way in which Newtonian gravity fails to predict empirical results.
solution_text: Precession of Mercury, gravitational lensing, clocks at different heights running at different rates, etc.

question_text: Define a null vector.
solution_text: A vector whose tangent satisfies K^a K_a = 0.

question_text: What is the Ricci scalar of Minkowski space in any coordinates?
solution_text: Since R^α_{βγδ} = 0 in Cartesian coordinates of R^{3,1}, R = 0 in Cartesian coordinates of R^{3,1}. But R is coordinate invariant, so R = 0 for R^{3,1} in all coordinates.

question_text: Why are global inertial frames not sufficient to explain gravity?
solution_text: Global inertial frames do not exist in gravity: even free falling frames accelerate.

question_text: Why is ∂_a not a vector in special relativity?
solution_text: ∂_a does not transform like a vector under Lorentz transformations.

question_text: Why is ∂_a not a 1-form in general relativity?
solution_text: Since ∂_μ g_{νρ}|_p = 0 for L.I.C. at p, this would mean ∂_μ g_{νρ} = 0 in any coordinate system at all p, so ∂_μ g_{νρ} can't be a tensor.

question_text: Consider the metric: ds^2 = -(1 + r^2/ℓ^2)dt^2 + (r^2 dr^2)/(1 + r^2/ℓ^2) + r^2 dΩ^2. Note that this is not Schwarzschild. The Riemann tensor of this metric is R_{abcd} = -1/ℓ^2 (g_{ac}g_{bd} - g_{ad}g_{bc}). Compute the Ricci tensor of this spacetime.

Consider the metric: ds^2 = -(1 + r^2/ℓ^2)dt^2 + (r^2 dr^2)/(1 + r^2/ℓ^2) + r^2 dΩ^2. Note that this is not Schwarzschild. The Riemann tensor of this metric is R_{abcd} = -1/ℓ^2 (g_{ac}g_{bd} - g_{ad}g_{bc}). Compute the Ricci scalar of this spacetime.

Consider the metric: ds^2 = -(1 + r^2/ℓ^2)dt^2 + (r^2 dr^2)/(1 + r^2/ℓ^2) + r^2 dΩ^2. Note that this is not Schwarzschild. The Riemann tensor of this metric is R_{abcd} = -1/ℓ^2 (g_{ac}g_{bd} - g_{ad}g_{bc}). Compute the Einstein tensor of this spacetime.

Consider the metric: ds^2 = -(1 + r^2/ℓ^2)dt^2 + (r^2 dr^2)/(1 + r^2/ℓ^2) + r^2 dΩ^2. Note that this is not Schwarzschild. The Riemann tensor of this metric is R_{abcd} = -1/ℓ^2 (g_{ac}g_{bd} - g_{ad}g_{bc}). Compute the Kretschmann scalar of this spacetime.
solution_text: K = R_{abcd}R^{abcd} = 1/ℓ^4 (g_{ac}g_{bd} - g_{ad}g_{bc})(g^{ac}g^{bd} - g^{ad}g^{bc}) = 1/ℓ^4 (16 - g_{ac}g_{bd}g^{ad}g^{bc} - g_{ad}g_{bc}g^{ac}g^{bd} + 16) = 1/ℓ^4 (32 - δ^c_d δ^d_c - δ^d_c δ^c_d) = 24/ℓ^4

question_text: Consider the following curve in the Schwarzschild geometry for r > R_S: r = r_0, θ = θ_0, φ = φ_0. Write an expression for a tangent 4-vector to this curve.
solution_text: This is the curve (t,r,θ,φ) = (t,r_0,θ_0,φ_0). A particular tangent is U^μ → [1, 0, 0, 0]^T.

question_text: Consider the following curve in the Schwarzschild geometry for r > R_S: r = r_0, θ = θ_0, φ = φ_0. Compute the norm of this tangent 4-vector. Is it timelike, spacelike, or null?
solution_text: U^μ U^ν g_{μν} = -(1 - 2GM/r). For r > R_s, this is negative, so U^μ is timelike.

question_text: Consider the following curve in the Schwarzschild geometry for r > R_S: r = r_0, θ = θ_0, φ = φ_0. Recall that the 4-velocity of a particle always satisfies U^a U_a = -1. Is this true of the tangent vector you wrote down above? If not, how do you explain the discrepancy?
solution_text: It is not true. U^μ is not a 4-velocity because we did not parametrize it by τ: we parametrized it by t.

question_text: How much older is your head than your feet?
solution_text: The time delay between clocks at different heights (approximating g = constant between your head and feet): Δτ_l = e^{gl/c^2} Δτ_0. If you are 20 years old according to your feet and have a height of 1.75m (in your rest frame), then Δτ_l - Δτ_0 = Δτ_0(e^{gl/c^2} - 1) = 1.4·10^{-7}s.

question_text: A police car traveling at 0.5c chases bank robbers traveling at 0.75c. Realizing they can't catch up, the police decide to shoot out the robbers' tires. What is the minimum speed required for their bullets to leave the officers guns in order for the robbers to not outrun the bullets as well?
solution_text: In the police car frame (the primed frame below): v'_c = (v_p - v_c)/(1 - v_p v_c) = 2c/5. The bullets need to be traveling at v'_{bullet} > 2c/5.

question_text: A beam of muons, all of which are known to have the same energy, is observed to have the property that on average each muon travels 2000 meters before decaying. A student who hasn't taken 8.033 concludes that the muon has a mean lifetime of 2·10^{-6} seconds and concludes that the muons traveled at 10^9 meters/second. Identify the student's error, and compute the correct muon velocity.
solution_text: The student neglected to account for time dilation: t = γτ_0, where τ_0 = 2 · 10^{-6} s (rest lifetime). vt = vγτ_0 = 2000m. Using the definition of γ, we get v^2/(1-v^2/c^2) = (2000m/2·10^{-6}s)^2, where we have inserted c for dimensional consistency. We find v = 2.87·10^8 m/s.

question_text: Consider a uniformly accelerated observer with proper acceleration α. Compute the 4-velocity components U^μ of the observer in Cartesian Minkowski coordinates (you may leave it in terms of τ).
solution_text: The trajectory is (1/α sinh ατ, 1/α cosh ατ, 0, 0), so the 4-velocity is U^μ = dx^μ/dτ = [cosh ατ, sinh ατ, 0, 0]^T.

question_text: Consider a uniformly accelerated observer with proper acceleration α. Using the fact that in Cartesian coordinates of Minkowski space, all Christoffel symbols vanish, show using the geodesic equation that uniformly accelerated observers do not follow geodesics.
solution_text: U^μ∇_μ U^ν = U^μ(∂_μ U^ν + Γ^ν_{μρ} U^μ U^ρ) = U^μ ∂_μ U^ν = dx^μ/dτ ∂_μ U^ν = dU^ν/dτ ≠ 0. The trajectories do not solve the geodesic equation.

question_text: Consider a uniformly accelerated observer with proper acceleration α. Recall that U^a∇_a U^b is the directional derivative of the 4-velocity in the direction of the 4-velocity. Write down a general expression for the directional derivative of an arbitrary vector V^a in the direction of an arbitrary vector W^a.
solution_text: W^a∇_a V^b

question_text: Kruskal Coordinates (extra difficult, good practice, and above the level of what's expected on the exam). In this problem we'll develop coordinates for the Schwarzschild metric that are good everywhere in the spacetime (except at the actual singularity). Introduce a new coordinate (the so-called Regge-Wheeler "tortoise coordinate"). We'd like this coordinate to replace dr^2 with something more regular. Define: r_* = ∫ r dr/(r-2GM) (1-2GM/r). Write out r_* as a function of r. What happens to r_* as r approaches 2GM? Why is this called a "tortoise" coordinate?
solution_text: r_* = ∫ r dr/(r-2GM) = r + 2GM ln(r/2GM - 1). r_* goes to -∞ as r → 2GM. This is a very slow coordinate!

question_text: Kruskal Coordinates. Define "lightcone coordinates" for the spacetime: u = t - r_*, v = t + r_*. These are known as null coordinates. Which one is constant along light rays moving towards larger r and which one is constant along light rays moving towards smaller r?
solution_text: u = constant along lines moving towards larger r. v = constant along lines moving towards smaller r.

question_text: Kruskal Coordinates. Rewrite the line element in the t-r directions in terms of du, dv, and r(u,v), without evaluating the latter. What are g_{uu} and g_{vv}?
solution_text: ds^2 = -(1 - 2GM/r(u,v)) dudv (+r(u,v)^2 dΩ^2), and g_{uu} = 0 = g_{vv}.

question_text: Kruskal Coordinates. Express 1 - 2GM/r in terms of a function of r that doesn't vanish at r = R_S and the function e^{4GM(v-u)}. Plug this into the line element in the t-r directions to replace 1 - 2GM/r.
solution_text: r(u,v) + 2GM ln(r/2GM - 1) = (v-u)/2, from which we get e^{r/2M}(r/2M - 1) = e^{(v-u)/4M}, so 1 - 2GM/r = 2M/r. ds^2 = -2M/r e^{-r/2M} e^{(v-u)/4M} dudv

question_text: Kruskal Coordinates. Define (yet another!) set of coordinates U and V: U = -e^{-u/4GM}, V = e^{v/4GM}. Rewrite the t-r line element in terms of U and V, keeping the term r^2 dΩ^2 intact.
Kruskal Coordinates. These coordinates are manifestly regular everywhere except at r = 0. The range of U and V is constrained only by r(U,V) > 0. What is the range of U and V so that r(U,V) > 0?
solution_text: r(U,V) > 0. e^{r/2MG}(r/2MG - 1) = e^{(v-u)/2MG} = -VU. When r = 0, UV = 1. r = 0 occurs twice. When r → +∞, UV → -∞. U > 0, V > 0 and U < 0, V < 0 correspond to two singularities. For r > 0, e^{r/2MG}(r/2MG - 1) ranges from -1 to +∞, so the largest possible value of UV is 1. The constraint is U ∈ (-∞,∞) and V ∈ (-∞,∞), but UV < 1.

question_text: Kruskal Coordinates. Show that r(U,V) = 0 corresponds to two different singularities. One of these is the singularity we've already seen in the future of an observer in region II. The other is a singularity in the past of an observer in region IV, which we did not discuss in class.
solution_text: When r = 0, UV = 1. r = 0 occurs twice. When r → +∞, UV → -∞. U > 0, V > 0 and U < 0, V < 0 correspond to two singularities.

question_text: Higgs decay: one of the great successes of the LHC has been to produce the Higgs particle, whose existence is critical for the standard model of particle physics to be able to incorporate certain particles with mass. The rest mass of the Higgs is about m_h c^2 ≈ 125GeV. The most common Higgs decay process is into two photons (which, recall, are denoted by γ). That is, the process: h → γγ. In the rest frame of the Higgs, compute the energy of each photon after the decay process.
solution_text: In the rest frame of the Higgs, the photons have equal and opposite 3-momentum. The total momentum is p^μ → [m_H, 0, 0, 0]^T. This is conserved, so after the collision, p^0 = 2p^0_γ = m_H, so p^0_γ = m_H/2 each.

question_text: Higgs decay: one of the great successes of the LHC has been to produce the Higgs particle, whose existence is critical for the standard model of particle physics to be able to incorporate certain particles with mass. The rest mass of the Higgs is about m_h c^2 ≈ 125GeV. The most common Higgs decay process is into two photons (which, recall, are denoted by γ). That is, the process: h → γγ. Suppose now that in the lab frame the Higgs has energy E_h and that the energy of one of the photons is measured to be E_γ. What is the angle that the photon makes with the original path of the Higgs?
solution_text: We have no information about the second photon. Call its momentum p^a_{γ'}. Call the momentum of the first photon p^a_γ. p^a_{γ'} = p^a_H - p^a_γ. We also know p^a_{γ'} p_{γ'a} = 0. Altogether 0 = (p^a_H - p^a_γ)(p_{Ha} - p_{γa}) = p^a_H p_{Ha} + p^a_γ p_{γa} - 2p^a_H p_{γa} = -m^2_H + 2E_H E_γ - 2p^i_H p_{γi} = -m^2_H + 2E_H E_γ - 2E_γ cos θ √(E^2_H - m^2_H). We solve for θ and get θ = cos^{-1}[(2E_H E_γ - m^2_H)/(2E_γ √(E^2_H - m^2_H))].

question_text: Comparing Metrics in Space: Given the line element (purely spatial, no time direction): ds^2 = du^2 + cosh^2 u dv^2. Note its similarity with the two-dimensional metric of flat space in radial coordinates: ds^2 = dr^2 + r^2 dφ^2. What are the circumferences of a circle in both spaces, where a circle is defined as the set of all points at a given distance the radius, from a point c, the center? You may use u as the radius in the first metric and periodically identify v from 0 to 2π.
solution_text: L = ∮_γ √ds^2, where γ is a path. In Euclidean space, L_E = ∫^{2π}_0 √(dr^2 + r^2 dθ^2) = ∫^{2π}_0 √((dr/dθ)^2 + r^2) dθ = ∫^{2π}_0 r dθ = 2πr. The space is called the Lobachevsky space: L_L = ∮ √ds^2 = ∫^{2π}_{v=0} √(du^2 + cosh^2 u dv^2) = ∫^{2π}_{v=0} cosh u dv = 2π cosh u.

question_text: Comparing Metrics in Space: Given the line element (purely spatial, no time direction): ds^2 = du^2 + cosh^2 u dv^2. Note its similarity with the two-dimensional metric of flat space in radial coordinates: ds^2 = dr^2 + r^2 dφ^2. What are the areas of a circle in both spaces?
solution_text: A_E = ∫^{2π}_0 ∫^R_0 R dr dθ = πR^2. A_L = ∫^{2π}_0 ∫^U_0 cosh U du dv = 2π sinh U

question_text: Comparing Metrics in Space: Given the line element (purely spatial, no time direction): ds^2 = du^2 + cosh^2 u dv^2. Note its similarity with the two-dimensional metric of flat space in radial coordinates: ds^2 = dr^2 + r^2 dφ^2. How do you explain the differences?
solution_text: Euclidean space is flat while Lobachevsky space is not. Lobachevsky space is hyperbolic (kind of like a wormhole). Circumference and area increase much faster with radius!

question_text: Consider two inertial frames: R and R'. R' moves with speed v' = 3c/5 along the x-axis of frame R. The origins and orientations of these frames are chosen so that they match at t = t' = 0; at this moment, the common origin is x=x'=y=y'=z=z'=0. Write down the equations for the coordinate transformations of R→R'.
solution_text:
For F →F', v =3c/5 and γ =5/4. The Lorentz transformations are thus:
t' = (5/4)(t− (3/5c)x)
x' = (5/4)(x− (3c/5)t)
y' =y
z' =z

question_text: Consider two inertial frames: R and R'. R' moves with speed v' = 3c/5 along the x-axis of frame R. The origins and orientations of these frames are chosen so that they match at t = t' = 0. What are the coordinates of event A in the R' frame if its coordinates in the R frame are (1,2,3,4)?
solution_text: These can be read off from the Lorentz transformation: (-1/4,7/4,3,4).

question_text: Consider two inertial frames: R and R'. R' moves with speed v' = 3c/5 along the x-axis of frame R. A rocket whose rest-frame length is L₀ moves along the x-axis of frame R with speed u=4c/5. What is its velocity in frame R'?
solution_text:
We need to relativistically add 4c/5 and −3c/5; the minus sign because the rocket is moving in the same direction as R' as seen in frame R. Let u be the speed of the rocket and v the speed between R and R':
u_x' = (u+v)/(1+uv/c²) = (c/5)/(1−12/25) = 5c/13.

question_text: Consider two inertial frames: R and R'. R' moves with speed v' = 3c/5 along the x-axis of frame R. A rocket whose rest-frame length is L₀ moves along the x-axis of frame R with speed u=4c/5 and has velocity 5c/13 in frame R'. What is the length of the rocket in frame R'?
solution_text:
The rocket's length in this frame is given by a length contraction of its rest frame length using the Lorentz factor corresponding to its speed in R':
γ = 1/√(1−(5/13)²) = 13/12,
so
L = 12L₀/13.

question_text: Suppose the density of a meter stick is measured in one inertial frame. Will observers in other inertial frames agree with this measurement? Explain why or why not.
solution_text: No: the volume of a meter stick changes when its length is measured to be different, but the number of molecules in the meter stick doesn't change!

question_text: Suppose the acceleration of a rocket is measured in one inertial frame. Will observers in other inertial frames agree with this measurement? Explain why or why not.
solution_text: No: acceleration is measured in units of time, which changes from frame to frame.

question_text: Suppose the number of rotations per second made by a spinning merry-go-round is measured in one inertial frame. Will observers in other inertial frames agree with this measurement? Explain why or why not.
solution_text: No: due to time dilation different frames will record a different number of rotations per second.

question_text: Suppose that you first got into the car and only afterwards drove away is measured in one inertial frame. Will observers in other inertial frames agree with this measurement? Explain why or why not.
solution_text: Yes: the two events are both on your worldline and thus have a definitive causal ordering.

question_text: Two trains A and B each have proper length L. In your rest frame, they're moving in the +x direction at speeds 4c/5 and 3c/5 respectively on parallel tracks. A starts out with its front aligned with B's end. How long in your reference frame does it take for the end of A to be aligned with the front of B?
solution_text:
Relative to you, the γ factors associated with A and B are 5/3 and 5/4, respectively. So their lengths in your frame are 3L/5 and 4L/5, respectively. While overtaking B, A has to travel farther than B by an amount equal to the sum of the lengths of the trains: i.e. it must travel 7L/5. The difference in speeds of the two trains as measured by you is 1/5, so:
t_you = (7L/5)/(1/5) = 7L

question_text: King Arthur sets up a race with two rods of length ℓ₀ in the King's frame separating starting lines from the finish line. Knights' horses move at constant velocity v, crossing starting lines simultaneously and reaching finish line simultaneously in the King's frame. Consider coordinates (t_K, x_K), (t₁, x₁), and (t₂, x₂) in King Arthur's, Knight #1's, and Knight #2's frames respectively. The finish line is the origin where all coordinates are zero. What is the distance between Knight #2's starting line and the finish line in Knight #2's frame?
solution_text: Knight #2 sees the starting line move toward him with speed v, so the distance is length contracted to ℓ₀/γ (where γ = 1/√(1−v²/c²)).

question_text: King Arthur sets up a race with two rods of length ℓ₀ in the King's frame. Knights move at constant velocity v, with the finish line as origin where all coordinates are zero. At what time does Knight #2 cross his starting line in his own frame (t₂, x₂)?
solution_text: It takes a time interval Δt₂ = ℓ₀/(γv) for Knight #2 to cover the distance from their starting line to the finish line. Since the finish line is crossed at time zero, the start line is crossed at t₂(E₂) = −ℓ₀/(γv).

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup with knights moving at velocity v, with what velocity does Knight #1's starting line approach Knight #2 according to Knight #2?
solution_text: Knight #1's starting line approaches Knight #2 with velocity v⃗ = vx̂

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup with knights moving at velocity v, with what velocity does Knight #1 approach Knight #2 according to Knight #2?
solution_text: Knight #1 is seen by Knight #2 to move in the x direction with speed v₁ according to 2 ≡ v₂ = 2v/(1+v²/c²).

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup, at what time does Knight #1 cross his own starting line in Knight #2's frame? Give coordinates (t₂, x₂).
solution_text: Knight #2 sees the starting line approach with speed v, and sees Knight #1 moving with speed v₂. The total time Knight #1 runs satisfies: v₂Δt = ℓ₀/γ + vΔt. Solving: Δt = (ℓ₀/γ)/(v₂−v). Using v₂−v = v/(γ(1+v²/c²)), we find Δt = γℓ₀(1+v²/c²)/v. Since Knight #1 crosses the finish line at t₂=0, they must have started at t₂(E₁) = −γℓ₀(1+v²/c²)/v.

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup, does Knight #2 think this is a fair race? Explain why or why not in one or two sentences.
solution_text: Compare the time at which Knight #1 started with the time at which Knight #2 started: According to Knight #2, Knight #1 got a head start, beginning the race early by a time interval δt = t₂(E₂) − t₂(E₁) = 2ℓ₀γv/c². Knight #2 thinks Knight #1 cheated by starting early!

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup, does Knight #1 think this is a fair race? Explain why or why not in one or two sentences.
solution_text: Repeating all this from the other point of view, we'll see that everything is symmetric: Knight #1 will believe that Knight #2 cheating by starting early. Neither of the two knights think this is a fair race.

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup, what are the coordinates (t_K, x_K) of event E_F (knights arrive at finish line) in King Arthur's frame?
In King Arthur's race setup, what are the coordinates (t₂, x₂) of event E_F (knights arrive at finish line) in Knight #2's frame?
solution_text:
t₂ = γ(t_K + vx_K/c²) = 0
x₂ = γ(x_K + vt_K) = 0

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup, what are the coordinates (t_K, x_K) of event E₂ (Knight #2 crosses his starting line) in King Arthur's frame?
solution_text: (t_K, x_K)_E2 = (−ℓ₀/v, ℓ₀)

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup, what are the coordinates (t₂, x₂) of event E₂ (Knight #2 crosses his starting line) in Knight #2's frame?
solution_text:
t₂ = γ(t_K + vx_K/c²) = γ(−ℓ₀/v + v·ℓ₀/c²) = (γℓ₀/v)(−1 + v²/c²) = −ℓ₀/(vγ)
x₂ = γ(x_K + vt_K) = γ(ℓ₀ − v·ℓ₀/v) = 0

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup, what are the coordinates (t_K, x_K) of event E₁ (Knight #1 crosses his starting line) in King Arthur's frame?
solution_text: (t_K, x_K)_E1 = (−ℓ₀/v, −ℓ₀)

question_text: In King Arthur's race setup, what are the coordinates (t₂, x₂) of event E₁ (Knight #1 crosses his starting line) in Knight #2's frame?
solution_text:
t₂ = γ(t_K + vx_K/c²) = γ(−ℓ₀/v − v·ℓ₀/c²) = −γℓ₀(1+v²/c²)/v
x₂ = γ(x_K + vt_K) = γ(−ℓ₀ − v·ℓ₀/v) = −2γℓ₀

question_text: An MIT student is working with delicate microbial samples only to realize that she needs to feed the microbes a very particular compound only available in a manufactory in New York. If she does not add the compound in at most 3 hours, the microbes will die. The New York manufactory is willing to send someone over with the compound, but the GPS estimates it will take the driver 5 hours to reach MIT. The student once took 8.033 and knows about time dilation. She decides to put the microbe samples in a centrifuge of radius R to spin them so that a shorter amount of proper time passes for the microbes. At what angular speed ω must she spin the samples, assuming that the speed is constant throughout? Express your answer in terms of the radius R; you may also include a factor of the speed of light. In your analysis, assume that the vehicle transporting the sample is non-relativistic, so that the only relativistic effects you need to compute come from the centrifuge.

An MIT student is working with delicate microbial samples only to realize that she needs to feed the microbes a very particular compound only available in a manufactory in New York. If she does not add the compound in at most 3 hours, the microbes will die. The New York manufactory is willing to send someone over with the compound, but the GPS estimates it will take the driver 5 hours to reach MIT. The student finds to her disappointment that the centrifuge isn't able to keep up this punishing speed. As the spinning mechanism wears out, the speed slows down. Solve the following recurrence, providing rigorous proof for your answer using either induction or a recursion tree. Give your answer with Big-Theta notation.

T(n) = 2T(n/7) + T(2n/3) + Θ(n)
solution_text:
Via recursion tree: Since the cost at each subproblem is Θ(m) (where m is the size of the subproblem), we can upper bound it with cm for some constant c. We prove by induction that the cost at the kth level of the tree is at most (20/21)^(k-1) cn. Consider the base case first: at the top level of the tree, the cost is at most cn as the original problem is of size n. Suppose, by our inductive hypothesis, that at the kth level of the tree the cost is at most (20/21)^(k-1) cn; then, at the (k+1)th level we have at most a cost of at most 2c·(20/21)^(k-1)·n/7 + c·(20/21)^(k-1)·2n/3 = (2·1/7 + 2/3)·(20/21)^(k-1)cn = (20/21)^k cn. This proves our inductive step. Hence, by induction, the cost at the kth level of the tree is at most (20/21)^(k-1)cn. The total cost is the sum of the costs at all levels, that is Σ_k (20/21)^(k-1)cn, which is a geometric sum converging to Θ(n).

Via induction: Like in the notes for Recitation 1, we guess T(n) ≤ d·n and prove using induction. The intuition for this guess comes from the fact that the subproblem sizes sum to n/7 + n/7 + 2n/3 = 20n/21, which is a constant multiple of n with a coefficient less than 1. Our base case is T(1) ≤ d·1 = d, which is true if we choose d ≥ T(1). For the inductive step, assume that for some d, T(n) ≤ d·n for all n < k. Then we consider T(k). Let Θ(n) be of the form c·n as before. Applying the recursive definition of T(n) and then the inductive hypothesis, we have T(k) ≤ 2T(k/7) + T(2k/3) + c·k ≤ 2·d·k/7 + d·2k/3 + c·k = (20d/21 + c)·k. If we choose d such that 20d/21 + c ≤ d, then T(k) ≤ d·k. This occurs when d ≥ 21c. Thus, both steps of the proof follow if we choose any d ≥ max{T(1), 21c}, and we have a valid proof by induction showing that T(n) = O(n), as desired.

Note that to prove a tight asymptotic bound of Θ(n), we must prove not only T(n) = O(n) but also T(n) = Ω(n). However, the latter is easier, since even without the recursive terms T(n/7) and T(2n/3), we have a work term of Θ(n) in our recurrence for T(n), meaning that T(n) = Ω(n) and thus implying that T(n) = Θ(n), as desired.

question_text:
Asami and Bolin are playing a dice game in which a pair of dice is rolled repeatedly. Asami wins if a sum of 5 is rolled before any sum greater than or equal to 8, and Bolin wins if any sum greater than or equal to 8 is rolled first. We will find the probability that Asami wins the game.

Let E_n denote the event that a 5 occurs on the nth roll and neither 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8 occurs on any of the first (n-1) rolls.

Compute Pr[E_n].
solution_text:
As we found in the previous part, 5 occurs with probability 4/36 and any number greater than or equal to 8 with probability 15/36. Therefore, neither occurs on a given roll with probability 1 - 4/36 - 15/36 = 17/36.

Each of the n rolls required for E_n to occur are independent, so to find the probability Pr[E_n], we can multiply the probabilities that the first (n-1) rolls result in neither 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8 and the probability that the nth roll results in a 5, yielding Pr[E_n] = (17/36)^(n-1) · 1/9.

question_text:
Asami and Bolin are playing a dice game in which a pair of dice is rolled repeatedly. Asami wins if a sum of 5 is rolled before any sum greater than or equal to 8, and Bolin wins if any sum greater than or equal to 8 is rolled first. We will find the probability that Asami wins the game.

Let E_n denote the event that a 5 occurs on the nth roll and neither 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8 occurs on any of the first (n-1) rolls.

Compute Σ_(n=1)^∞ Pr[E_n] and argue that it is the desired probability. In your argument, explain why it is correct to just sum the probabilities, because summing isn't always valid for all problems.

Hint: Σ_(n=0)^∞ r^n = 1/(1-r) for |r| < 1.
solution_text:
Since the events E_n are mutually disjoint and their union is precisely the event whose probability we are trying calculate, we can add their probabilities to obtain the desired result:

5Pr[before ≥ 8] = Σ_(n=1)^∞ Pr[E_n] = Σ_(n=1)^∞ (17/36)^(n-1) · 1/9

This can be evaluated using the hint in the problem to be 1/9 · 1/(1-17/36) = 4/19.

question_text:
Asami and Bolin are playing a dice game in which a pair of dice is rolled repeatedly. Asami wins if a sum of 5 is rolled before any sum greater than or equal to 8, and Bolin wins if any sum greater than or equal to 8 is rolled first.

Now, suppose that in a run of this game, the dice are rolled ten times with neither a 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8 appearing. Given this, let X be the sum of these ten rolls. We would now like to find an upper bound for the following probability Pr[|X - E[X]| ≥ 28].

Given that each of these ten rolls results in neither a 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8, enumerate the possible totals for each roll and their respective probabilities.
solution_text:
Because we know that we roll neither a 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8, the only possible rolls are 2, 3, 4, 6, 7. There are 36-4-15 = 17 rolls that result in these totals, with counts 1, 2, 3, 5, and 6, respectively. Thus, the probabilities are as follows:

Roll | Probability
2    | 1/17
3    | 2/17
4    | 3/17
6    | 5/17
7    | 6/17

question_text:
Asami and Bolin are playing a dice game in which a pair of dice is rolled repeatedly. Asami wins if a sum of 5 is rolled before any sum greater than or equal to 8, and Bolin wins if any sum greater than or equal to 8 is rolled first.

Now, suppose that in a run of this game, the dice are rolled ten times with neither a 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8 appearing. Given this, let X be the sum of these ten rolls.

For these ten rolls, let X_i be the sum of the numbers on the face of the dice for roll i. Compute the expected value of X_i.
solution_text:
The expected value of X_i is given by

E[X_i] = Σ_j j · P[X_i = j] = 1/17 · 2 + 2/17 · 3 + 3/17 · 4 + 5/17 · 6 + 6/17 · 7 = 92/17

question_text:
Asami and Bolin are playing a dice game in which a pair of dice is rolled repeatedly. Asami wins if a sum of 5 is rolled before any sum greater than or equal to 8, and Bolin wins if any sum greater than or equal to 8 is rolled first.

Now, suppose that in a run of this game, the dice are rolled ten times with neither a 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8 appearing. Given this, let X be the sum of these ten rolls.

Show that E[X] = 920/17, where X is the sum of these ten dice rolls.
solution_text:
By linearity of expectation, we have

E[X] = Σ_(i=1)^10 E[X_i].

Thus, E[X] = 10 · 92/17 = 920/17.

question_text:
Asami and Bolin are playing a dice game in which a pair of dice is rolled repeatedly. Asami wins if a sum of 5 is rolled before any sum greater than or equal to 8, and Bolin wins if any sum greater than or equal to 8 is rolled first.

Now, suppose that in a run of this game, the dice are rolled ten times with neither a 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8 appearing. Given this, let X be the sum of these ten rolls.

Compute the variance of X, where X is again the sum of these ten dice rolls.
solution_text:
Because the dice rolls are pairwise independent, we have

Var[X] = Var[Σ_(i=1)^10 X_i] = Σ_(i=1)^10 Var[X_i]

To compute the variance of a single dice roll, we use Var[X_i] = E[X_i^2] - E[X_i]^2.

To find E[X_i^2], we write
E[X_i^2] = Σ_j j^2 · Pr[X_i = j] = 1/17 · 2^2 + 2/17 · 3^2 + 3/17 · 4^2 + 5/17 · 6^2 + 6/17 · 7^2 = 32

The variance of X_i is thus
Var[X_i] = E[X_i^2] - E[X_i]^2 = 32 - (92/17)^2 = 784/289

It follows that the variance of X is given by
Var[X] = 10 · 784/289 = 7840/289

question_text:
Asami and Bolin are playing a dice game in which a pair of dice is rolled repeatedly. Asami wins if a sum of 5 is rolled before any sum greater than or equal to 8, and Bolin wins if any sum greater than or equal to 8 is rolled first.

Now, suppose that in a run of this game, the dice are rolled ten times with neither a 5 nor any number greater than or equal to 8 appearing. Given this, let X be the sum of these ten rolls.

Use Chebyshev's inequality to find an upper bound for Pr[|X - E[X]| ≥ 28].
solution_text:
Using Chebyshev's inequality and the value for the variance of X from the previous part, we find

Pr[|X - E[X]| ≥ 28] ≤ (7840/289)/28^2 = 10/289

question_text:
Entrepreneur Melon Usk recently launched the Giraffe Heavy with record setting payload, including his flagship car, the Edison Roadster. In his ambitious future plans, he wishes to have many launch-sites scattered across the country.

Without much planning, he has chosen a set of launch-sites L, where the location of each launch-site l_i ∈ L is represented by its Cartesian coordinates (x_i, y_i). You may assume that all the x_i and y_i are distinct.

However, in order to guarantee the viability of the coordinated launches, every launch-site must be at least 1 unit of distance away from every other launch-site as measured in Euclidean distance.

Your job as a 6.1220 student is to help Melon determine whether the set L violates this viability property. More formally, we will design an efficient algorithm to check whether there exists a pair l_i, l_j ∈ L such that ||l_i - l_j|| < 1.

Provide a naive algorithm that determines whether there exists a pair of launch-sites less than 1 unit of distance apart. If such a pair exists, your algorithm should return any example pair, and if no pair exists, your algorithm should return FALSE.

Analyze the runtime of your solution.
solution_text: Iterate through all pairwise combinations of l_i, l_j ∈ L and compute for each pair whether ||l_i - l_j|| ≥ 1. Runtime is O(n^2) with respect to the number of launch-sites n = |L| since we compare all possible pairs, and each comparison takes constant time to calculate the distance.
