Not a Nash equilibrium since Alice can improve her utility by decreasing her usage.
solution_text: Solution: B.

question_text: Alice and Bob are renting an apartment together and, as much as they get along very well, one contentious issue is sharing the internet connection. Specifically, we model this sharing as a game where the action of Alice (resp. Bob) corresponds to specifying a fraction (number) 0 ≤ x_A ≤ 1 (resp. 0 ≤ x_B ≤ 1) of the connection they want to claim. Then, the utility u_A of Alice and the utility u_B of Bob become: u_A = {0 if x_A + x_B > 1; x_A(1-x_A-x_B) otherwise} and u_B = {0 if x_A + x_B > 1; x_B(1-x_A-x_B) otherwise}. In the above, the top pair of cases (x_A + x_B > 1) corresponds to the internet link being overloaded, and the bottom pair reflects the need for having enough bandwidth left to maintain latency. [10 points] Find a pair of actions x_A and x_B that give rise to maximal total utility u_A + u_B for Alice and Bob.
solution_text: Solution: We want to choose x_A and x_B so as to maximize u = u_A + u_B = (x_A + x_B)(1-(x_A + x_B)) subject to x_A, x_B ≥ 0 and x_A + x_B ≤ 1. Noticing that the dependence of u (and the constraint) on both x_A and x_B is always through the sum, we can simplify by substituting y = x_A + x_B. After the substitution, our problem is that of maximizing u = y(1 − y) subject to y ∈ [0,1]. This is easily seen (by symmetry, for example) to be maximized when y = 1/2. Therefore, any pair of actions such that x_A + x_B = 1/2 maximizes total utility. Taking, for example, x_A = x_B = 1/4 would work. For these actions, the total utility is 1/4.

question_text: Alice and Bob are renting an apartment together and, as much as they get along very well, one contentious issue is sharing the internet connection. Specifically, we model this sharing as a game where the action of Alice (resp. Bob) corresponds to specifying a fraction (number) 0 ≤ x_A ≤ 1 (resp. 0 ≤ x_B ≤ 1) of the connection they want to claim. Then, the utility u_A of Alice and the utility u_B of Bob become: u_A = {0 if x_A + x_B > 1; x_A(1-x_A-x_B) otherwise} and u_B = {0 if x_A + x_B > 1; x_B(1-x_A-x_B) otherwise}. In the above, the top pair of cases (x_A + x_B > 1) corresponds to the internet link being overloaded, and the bottom pair reflects the need for having enough bandwidth left to maintain latency. [15 points] Show that the pair of actions x_A = 1/3 and x_B = 1/3 constitutes a Nash equilibrium of this game. What is the total utility u_A + u_B it achieves?
solution_text: Solution: To prove that a pair of actions constitutes a Nash equlibrium, we need to show that none of the players have an incentive to deviate. Given the symmetric nature of the set-up, it is enough to consider Alice. Alice's utility at (1/3, 1/3) is 1/9. Keeping Bob's decision fixed, what would Alice's utility be if she changes her action from 1/3 to 1/3 + Δ for some Δ? Let's compute: u_A = (1/3 + Δ) · (1 - (1/3 + Δ) - 1/3) = (1/3 + Δ) · (1/3 - Δ) = 1/9 - Δ² ≤ 1/9 so Alice indeed does not have an incentive to deviate. In fact, any move from 1/3 would result in strictly less utility for Alice. Finally, at (1/3, 1/3), the total utility equals 1/9 + 1/9 = 2/9. As an aside, this is a good example of a "game" where coordinating players (part a) can attain greater total utility than competing players (part b). Indeed, 1/4 > 2/9!

question_text: Alice and Bob are renting an apartment together and, as much as they get along very well, one contentious issue is sharing the internet connection. Specifically, we model this sharing as a game where the action of Alice (resp. Bob) corresponds to specifying a fraction (number) 0 ≤ x_A ≤ 1 (resp. 0 ≤ x_B ≤ 1) of the connection they want to claim. Alice and Bob managed to provide latency bandwidth via alternative means. As a result, their utilities become: u_A = {0 if x_A + x_B > 1; x_A otherwise} and u_B = {0 if x_A + x_B > 1; x_B otherwise}. [15 points] List all the pure Nash equilibria of this game. Hint: Consider a case-by-case analysis depending on x_A + x_B < 1, x_A + x_B = 1, or x_A + x_B > 1.
solution_text: Solution: We can proceed by performing a case-by-case analysis: Case I: x_A + x_B < 1. In this case, any player can increase their utility by increasing their action by a small amount while keeping the constraint x_A + x_B ≤ 1. So there are no Nash Equilibria in this case. Case II: x_A + x_B = 1. In this case, any deviation for any player decreases their utility function. Indeed, consider Alice. If she increases her action, then x_A + x_B > 1 and she gets 0 utility. If she decreases her action, then her utility is similarly decreased by the corresponding amount. So, any pair (x_A, x_B) such that x_A + x_B = 1 constitutes a Nash Equilibrium. Case III: x_A + x_B > 1. In this case the initial utility for both players is 0. If one of x_A or x_B is strictly less than 1, then the other player can increase its utility by decreasing its action so that x_A + x_B = 1. For example, assume that x_A < 1. Then Bob can decrease his action to 1 - x_A and get utility of 1 - x_A > 0. On the other hand, if both x_A and x_B are at least 1, then the pair constitutes a Nash equilibrium. Indeed, in this case, no player can change to get positive utility. Consider Alice. If she changes her action to y_A > 0, then y_A + x_B > x_B ≥ 1 so she would still get utility 0. If she changes to y_A = 0, she still gets utility 0 as u_A = y_A = 0. So, in this case, all pairs (x_A, x_B) such that both x_A ≥ 1 and x_B ≥ 1 constitute Nash equilibria.

question_text: Red Pirate Droberts has discovered three chests of treasure, and needs to split them between himself and the first mate. He can either be greedy, and keep all three chests for himself, or be generous, and keep only one chest for himself. While Droberts is deciding how he wants to split the treasure, the first mate decides whether or not to mutiny. If he mutinies, then the first mate will get whatever Droberts set aside for himself, and if he doesn't, Droberts will run away with whatever Droberts set aside for the crew. As such, the utility matrix is as below. In the matrix, Droberts is the row player and the first mate is the column player. In any particular entry of the matrix, the first number represents the first mate's payoff while the second number represents Droberts' payoff: Droberts\First Mate | Mutiny | No Mutiny; Greedy | (3,0) | (0,3); Generous | (1,2) | (2,1). [Warm-up] Suppose Droberts is known to be greedy and the first mate is going to mutiny. Then, which of the following is true? A. Droberts will want to become generous. B. The first mate will want to choose not to mutiny. C. Both A and B. D. Neither are incentivized to switch strategies; this is a Nash equilibrium.
solution_text: Solution: A.

question_text: Red Pirate Droberts has discovered three chests of treasure, and needs to split them between himself and the first mate. He can either be greedy, and keep all three chests for himself, or be generous, and keep only one chest for himself. While Droberts is deciding how he wants to split the treasure, the first mate decides whether or not to mutiny. If he mutinies, then the first mate will get whatever Droberts set aside for himself, and if he doesn't, Droberts will run away with whatever Droberts set aside for the crew. As such, the utility matrix is as below. In the matrix, Droberts is the row player and the first mate is the column player. In any particular entry of the matrix, the first number represents the first mate's payoff while the second number represents Droberts' payoff: Droberts\First Mate | Mutiny | No Mutiny; Greedy | (3,0) | (0,3); Generous | (1,2) | (2,1). [10 points] Show that there is no pure Nash equilibrium in this game.
solution_text: Solution: Suppose for sake of contradiction that there exists a pure Nash equilibrium. Then, we land in one of four possibilities: Case 1: Droberts is greedy and the first mate mutinies. Then, Droberts is incentivized to switch to being generous. Case 2: Droberts is generous and the first mate mutinies. Then, the first mate is incentivized to not mutiny. Case 3: Droberts is generous and the first mate does not mutiny. Then, Droberts is incentivized to switch to being greedy. Case 4: Droberts is greedy and the first mate does not mutiny. Then, the first mate is incentivized to mutiny. Since in all cases, someone is incentivized to change their strategy, there does not exist a pure Nash equilibrium.

question_text: Red Pirate Droberts has discovered three chests of treasure, and needs to split them between himself and the first mate. He can either be greedy, and keep all three chests for himself, or be generous, and keep only one chest for himself. While Droberts is deciding how he wants to split the treasure, the first mate decides whether or not to mutiny. If he mutinies, then the first mate will get whatever Droberts set aside for himself, and if he doesn't, Droberts will run away with whatever Droberts set aside for the crew. As such, the utility matrix is as below. In the matrix, Droberts is the row player and the first mate is the column player. In any particular entry of the matrix, the first number represents the first mate's payoff while the second number represents Droberts' payoff: Droberts\First Mate | Mutiny | No Mutiny; Greedy | (3,0) | (0,3); Generous | (1,2) | (2,1). [20 points] Say the first mate has probability q of choosing to mutiny, and Droberts has a probability p of being greedy. Find the values of p and q such that these strategies constitute a Nash equilibrium. Hint: If the first mate knows p, how will they decide whether or not to mutiny?
solution_text: Solution: Since we are looking for a Nash equilibrium, we consider what value of p would make the first mate not incentivized to deviate, i.e., the value of p that would make the expected utility of selecting either mutiny or no mutiny be the same, or 3p + 1(1-p) = 2(1-p) or p = 1/4. We can use a similar approach for q. We wish Droberts to be indifferent for the first mate's value of q, so we see that we need 3(1-q) = 2q + 1(1-q) or q = 1/2. Observe that in this case, we found p and q by seeing what they would need to be to make the other party indifferent, not what they would need to be to make themselves indifferent to the other party's choice. Since the utility always sums to 3, these are the same in this case, however, they are not always the same. The interpretation explored in this solution will always lead to a Nash equilibrium, but the other may not.

question_text: For the entirety of this problem, you may assume that P ≠ NP. Your friend claims to have several exciting new polynomial-time algorithms for CNF-SAT! Unfortunately, they claim that each of their algorithms is only guaranteed to be correct when the formula has some special property. In each question below, we give the property of the formula on which your friend's algorithm is supposed to run in polynomial-time. For each such property, you must prove to your friend that their algorithm is either 1. Impossible, as assuming that P ≠ NP, the algorithm cannot run in polynomial-time, or 2. Possible, as the variant is solvable in polynomial-time. You must justify your answers to all parts, except the warm-up, with either a reduction or an algorithm. [Warm-up] Property: every clause in the formula has exactly one literal. For example, the formula x₁ ∧ x₂ ∧ x₃ ∧ x₄ satisfies this property.
solution_text: Solution: Possible. One can check this class of CNF-SAT formulae in polynomial time by simply checking that a variable and its negation do not both appear in the formula, and that FALSE also does not appear in the formula.

question_text: For the entirety of this problem, you may assume that P ≠ NP. Your friend claims to have several exciting new polynomial-time algorithms for CNF-SAT! Unfortunately, they claim that each of their algorithms is only guaranteed to be correct when the formula has some special property. In each question below, we give the property of the formula on which your friend's algorithm is supposed to run in polynomial-time. For each such property, you must prove to your friend that their algorithm is either 1. Impossible, as assuming that P ≠ NP, the algorithm cannot run in polynomial-time, or 2. Possible, as the variant is solvable in polynomial-time. You must justify your answers to all parts, except the warm-up, with either a reduction or an algorithm. [15 points] Property: every clause in the formula has at least one literal that appears positively. For example, the formula (x₁ ∨ x₂ ∨ x₃ ∨ x₄) ∧ (x₅ ∨ x₂) has this property since x₁ and x₂ appear positively in the first clause and x₅ appears positively in the second clause.
solution_text: Solution: Possible. Setting every variable to TRUE is a valid assignment.

question_text: For the entirety of this problem, you may assume that P ≠ NP. Your friend claims to have several exciting new polynomial-time algorithms for CNF-SAT! Unfortunately, they claim that each of their algorithms is only guaranteed to be correct when the formula has some special property. In each question below, we give the property of the formula on which your friend's algorithm is supposed to run in polynomial-time. For each such property, you must prove to your friend that their algorithm is either 1. Impossible, as assuming that P ≠ NP, the algorithm cannot run in polynomial-time, or 2. Possible, as the variant is solvable in polynomial-time. You must justify your answers to all parts, except the warm-up, with either a reduction or an algorithm. [15 points] Property: the formula has an even number of satisfying solutions. That is, the input formula has 0, 2, 4, ... many satisfying solutions, and in particular it does not have an odd number of satisfying assignments.
solution_text: Solution: Impossible. On its face, this problem is a bit trickier because given a formula, we cannot tell whether or not it has an even number of satisfying assignments (thereby, satisfying the preconditions of the algorithm). Nevertheless, we can reduce every CNF-SAT formula to a new formula which is guaranteed to have an even number of satisfying assignments, so it won't matter that the algorithm cannot solve problems that have an odd number of satisfying assignments. Therefore, if your friend's algorithm runs in polynomial time, then there is also a polynomial time algorithm for CNF-SAT, violating P ≠ NP. Reduction: Add one new variable W and one new clause (W ∨ W). Therefore, for every satisfying assignment of the original CNF-SAT formula, there are two distinct satisfying assignments in the new formula—one where W is set to TRUE and one where W is set to FALSE. Therefore, there are an even number of satisfying assignments. Furthermore, the new formula has no other satisfying assignments. Note: The extra clause is actually unnecessary here, but may help clarify the solution, i.e., we only need to add a new variable.

question_text:
Ben Bitdiddle has n friends, and he has bought m gifts that he would like to give to his friends. Each friend i has a demand. In particular, Ben Bitdiddle knows that friend i will be happy if and only if they receive exactly some subset S_i ⊆ {1,...,m} of those gifts. Unfortunately, some of his friends might demand the same gift! Ben Bitdiddle would like to know whether it's possible to make at least h of his friends simultaneously happy. Let HAPPY-FRIEND denote the problem of deciding whether it is possible to make at least h friends simultaneously happy when given sets S_i and a number h.

[Warm-up] Suppose Ben Bitdiddle has 5 friends and he has bought 5 gifts: an apple, a banana, a cherry, a durian, and an egg. Each friend is demanding these sets of gifts:
• Friend 1, Alice, wants S_1 = {apple, banana}.
• Friend 2, Bob, wants S_2 = {banana, cherry, egg}.
• Friend 3, Charlie, wants S_3 = {apple, banana, cherry, durian, egg}.
• Friend 4, Daisy, wants S_4 = {durian, egg}.
• Friend 5, Eve, wants S_5 = ∅.

Which of these pairs of friends can he make simultaneously happy?
A. Alice and Bob
B. Alice and Daisy
C. Charlie and Daisy
D. Charlie and Eve
solution_text: B and D. Ben can make a pair of friends simultaneously happy as long as they don't demand a common gift. Following this, two of the above pairs are work: Alice and Daisy; Charlie and Eve. Their sets S_i do not intersect. The other given pairs of sets intersect and hence don't work.

question_text:
Ben Bitdiddle has n friends, and he has bought m gifts that he would like to give to his friends. Each friend i has a demand. In particular, Ben Bitdiddle knows that friend i will be happy if and only if they receive exactly some subset S_i ⊆ {1,...,m} of those gifts. Unfortunately, some of his friends might demand the same gift! Ben Bitdiddle would like to know whether it's possible to make at least h of his friends simultaneously happy. Let HAPPY-FRIEND denote the problem of deciding whether it is possible to make at least h friends simultaneously happy when given sets S_i and a number h.

[10 points] Show that HAPPY-FRIEND is in NP.
solution_text:
We prove that HAPPY-FRIEND is in NP by showing that there exists a verifier that validates proofs for YES instances of HAPPY-FRIEND in polynomial time. A verifier can interpret the proof as an assignment of gifts to a subset of friends and output YES if and only if the subset is of size at least h, no gift is assigned to more than one friend, and each friend is assigned all the gifts that they demanded. This can be easily checked in polynomial time (in fact, linear time in the total size of the sets S_i and h), and the size of the proof is also polynomial (the number of gifts assigned can be at most m). Hence, HAPPY-FRIEND is in NP.

Note that by definition an instance of HAPPY-FRIEND is a YES instance if and only if it has a proof as described above on which the verifier described above would output YES.

question_text:
Ben Bitdiddle has n friends, and he has bought m gifts that he would like to give to his friends. Each friend i has a demand. In particular, Ben Bitdiddle knows that friend i will be happy if and only if they receive exactly some subset S_i ⊆ {1,...,m} of those gifts. Unfortunately, some of his friends might demand the same gift! Ben Bitdiddle would like to know whether it's possible to make at least h of his friends simultaneously happy. Let HAPPY-FRIEND denote the problem of deciding whether it is possible to make at least h friends simultaneously happy when given sets S_i and a number h.

[35 points] Use a reduction from CLIQUE to show that HAPPY-FRIEND is NP-complete.
solution_text:
Approaching the solution: The key insight to this problem is that two friends i and j can be made simultaneously happy if and only if S_i ∩ S_j = ∅. Extending this intuition to a larger group of friends, h friends can be made simultaneously happy if and only if their corresponding sets are pairwise disjoint. Therefore, we would like to transform CLIQUE into an instance of HAPPY-FRIEND by associating each vertex with a set, in such a way that vertices form a clique if and only if their corresponding sets are pairwise disjoint.

Solution: From part (b) we already know that HAPPY-FRIEND is in NP. To show that HAPPY-FRIEND is NP-complete, it remains to show that it is also NP-hard. We do so by reducing CLIQUE to it.

STEP 1: First we describe how to construct instances of HAPPY-FRIEND from instances of CLIQUE in polynomial time. Given an instance ⟨G,k⟩ of CLIQUE, create the sets S_i as follows. Let G = (V,E). We think of the nodes of the graph as friends and the edges as gifts. Let V = {v_1,...,v_n}, where |V| = n. Set m = (n choose 2). Specifically, there are m = (n choose 2) gifts, one for each of the unordered pairs (i,j), where 1 ≤ i,j ≤ n and i ≠ j. Let us denote the gift corresponding to the unordered pair (i,j) by g_ij. Notice that g_ij = g_ji. For each 1 ≤ i ≤ n, set S_i = {g_ij : (i,j) ∉ E}, and also set h = k. By definition, ⟨S_1,...,S_n,h⟩ is an instance of HAPPY-FRIEND. Creating ⟨S_1,...,S_n,h⟩ takes time linear in the size of ⟨G,k⟩ and the size of ⟨S_1,...,S_n,h⟩ is also linear in the size of ⟨G,k⟩. We use ⟨S_1,...,S_n,h⟩ as the corresponding instance of HAPPY-FRIEND.

STEP 2: We show ⟨G,k⟩ is a YES instance of CLIQUE if and only if ⟨S_1,...,S_n,h⟩ is a YES instance of HAPPY-FRIEND. To this end, we establish two implications:

• If ⟨G,k⟩ is a YES instance of CLIQUE, then ⟨S_1,...,S_n,h⟩ is a YES instance of HAPPY-FRIEND. If ⟨G,k⟩ is a YES instance of CLIQUE, then there is set of vertices C ⊆ V with |C| ≥ k that form a clique in G. We use this fact to come up with an assignment of gifts to a subset of friends. Specifically, for 1 ≤ i ≤ n, we assign friend i all the gifts in S_i if and only if v_i ∈ C. Since |C| ≥ k and h = k, at least h friends have been assigned gifts. The only thing left to prove is that no gift is assigned to more than one friend. Suppose, to the contrary, that some gift g_ij was assigned to more than one friend. By definition, g_ij can only belong to S_i and S_j. Thus, by assumption, g_ij ∈ S_i ∩ S_j. However, since we only assigned gifts to i if v_i ∈ C, this means that i,j ∈ C. But then, (i,j) ∈ E and therefore, g_ij ∉ S_i and g_ij ∉ S_j, a contradiction. Therefore, ⟨S_1,...,S_n,h⟩ is a YES instance of HAPPY-FRIEND.

• If ⟨S_1,...,S_n,h⟩ is a YES instance of HAPPY-FRIEND, then ⟨G,k⟩ is a YES instance of CLIQUE. If ⟨S_1,...,S_n,h⟩ is a YES instance of HAPPY-FRIEND, then there is an assignment of gifts to a subset of friends of size at least h, such that no gift is assigned to more than one friend, and each friend is assigned all the gifts that they demanded. We use this fact to come up with a clique C ⊆ V in G. Specifically, for 1 ≤ i ≤ n, we add i to C if and only if friend i has been assigned all the gifts in S_i. Since at least h friends have been assigned gifts and k = h, |C| ≥ k. The only thing left to prove is that C is a clique in G. Suppose, to the contrary, that some pair of vertices i,j ∈ C does not have an edge between them, i.e., (i,j) ∉ E. By definition, this means that g_ij ∈ S_i and g_ij ∈ S_j. Therefore, g_ij ∈ S_i ∩ S_j. However, this means that friends and i and j cannot be simultaneously happy, a contradiction. Therefore, ⟨G,k⟩ is a YES instance of CLIQUE.

Hence, HAPPY-FRIEND is NP-hard, and hence NP-complete.

question_text:
Barr the Bear has opened up a lemonade stand! All of Barr's friends want to get a tasty drink, but Barr is worried that the line will get too long.

The line in front of Barr's stand starts off empty. Barr's friends always travel in pairs, so each minute either two friends arrive to join the line with probability 0 < p < 1, or no one joins the line with probability 1-p. Each person gets only one lemonade. Barr can make one lemonade each minute, but she can't stockpile lemonade if no one is in line because she wants her lemonade to be fresh. Due to space constraints and people's lack of the patience, two friends arriving at a time when the line has a length of at least n, for some n ≥ 1, will both not join the line. Let's help Barr determine how long the line is likely to get!

Barr wants to represent this process with a graph G, where a random walk in G corresponds to the size of Barr's line in this process. In particular, Barr will have a graph G where the first node (leftmost) corresponds to the state where there are zero people in Barr's line, the second node corresponds to the state where there is one person in Barr's line, the next node corresponding to two people in Barr's line, and so on. Note that each node represents a possible number of people in Barr's line, and the nodes do not correspond to times.

[Warm-up] Which of the following is the probability that the line is empty at the end of two minutes?
A. p²
B. (1-p)²
C. 1-p
D. p(1-p)
solution_text: C. There are two distinct ways in which the line is empty after two minutes, namely (i) no friends arrive in either minute, or (ii) two friends arrive in the first minute, but no friends arrive in the second minute. The probabilities corresponding to each of these is (1-p)² and p(1-p) respectively, which add up to 1-p. Another way to look at this is that the line is empty after two minutes if and only if no friends arrive in the second minute, which happens with probability 1-p.

question_text:
Barr the Bear has opened up a lemonade stand! All of Barr's friends want to get a tasty drink, but Barr is worried that the line will get too long.

The line in front of Barr's stand starts off empty. Barr's friends always travel in pairs, so each minute either two friends arrive to join the line with probability 0 < p < 1, or no one joins the line with probability 1-p. Each person gets only one lemonade. Barr can make one lemonade each minute, but she can't stockpile lemonade if no one is in line because she wants her lemonade to be fresh. Due to space constraints and people's lack of the patience, two friends arriving at a time when the line has a length of at least n, for some n ≥ 1, will both not join the line. Let's help Barr determine how long the line is likely to get!

Barr wants to represent this process with a graph G, where a random walk in G corresponds to the size of Barr's line in this process. In particular, Barr will have a graph G where the first node (leftmost) corresponds to the state where there are zero people in Barr's line, the second node corresponds to the state where there is one person in Barr's line, the next node corresponding to two people in Barr's line, and so on.

[15 points] Barr wants to calculate the stationary distribution π⃗ of G. To start, show that it must be the case that π_i = (p/(1-p))^i π_0 for 0 ≤ i ≤ n-1.
solution_text:
We have the following series of equations characterizing π by the definition of the stationary distribution.

π_0 = (1-p)π_0 + (1-p)π_1
π_1 = pπ_0 + (1-p)π_2
...
π_{n-2} = pπ_{n-3} + (1-p)π_{n-1}
π_{n-1} = pπ_{n-2} + π_n
π_n = pπ_{n-1}

We can solve for all π_i in terms of π_0. Let's start with the first few and try to find a pattern. By the first equation,

π_1 = (p/(1-p))π_0

Substituting into the second equation, we find that

pπ_0 = pπ_0 + (1-p)π_2
(p - (1-p)p)/(1-p) π_0 = (1-p)π_2
(p²/(1-p))π_0 = (1-p)π_2
π_2 = (p/(1-p))²π_0

Following the above argument, we can inductively prove that π_i = (p/(1-p))^i π_0 for 0 ≤ i ≤ n-1.

In particular, we will prove this result via induction on i.

For our base case, we note that if i = 0, then our claimed formula yields π_0 = (p/(1-p))^0 π_0 = π_0, which is clearly true.

For our inductive step, suppose that our claimed formula is true for all i ≤ k < n-1. We then claim that the formula also holds for i = k + 1 ≤ n-1.

The case i = 1 is a bit different from the rest, and we handle this case above.

Now, consider the general case for 1 ≤ k < n-1. In this case, consider row k of the walk matrix (zero-indexed), which is used to calculate π_k. The equation obtained by considering this row of the equation π⃗W = π⃗ is

π_k = pπ_{k-1} + (1-p)π_{k+1}

Since we are assuming that the claimed formula is true for all i ≤ k, we must have π_k = (p/(1-p))^k π_0 = (p/(1-p))π_{k-1}, so we can rewrite this equation as

(p/(1-p))π_{k-1} = pπ_{k-1} + (1-p)π_{k+1}

Solving for π_{k+1} yields

π_{k+1} = (p/(1-p))²π_{k-1} = (p/(1-p))²(p/(1-p))^{k-1}π_0 = (p/(1-p))^{k+1}π_0,

so the formula holds for i = k + 1, as desired.

Thus, by the principle of mathematical induction, we have that the formula π_i = (p/(1-p))^i π_0 holds for all 0 ≤ i ≤ n-1, as desired.

question_text:
Barr the Bear has opened up a lemonade stand! All of Barr's friends want to get a tasty drink, but Barr is worried that the line will get too long.

The line in front of Barr's stand starts off empty. Barr's friends always travel in pairs, so each minute either two friends arrive to join the line with probability 0 < p < 1, or no one joins the line with probability 1-p. Each person gets only one lemonade. Barr can make one lemonade each minute, but she can't stockpile lemonade if no one is in line because she wants her lemonade to be fresh. Due to space constraints and people's lack of the patience, two friends arriving at a time when the line has a length of at least n, for some n ≥ 1, will both not join the line. Let's help Barr determine how long the line is likely to get!

Barr wants to represent this process with a graph G, where a random walk in G corresponds to the size of Barr's line in this process. In particular, Barr will have a graph G where the first node (leftmost) corresponds to the state where there are zero people in Barr's line, the second node corresponds to the state where there is one person in Barr's line, the next node corresponding to two people in Barr's line, and so on.

[25 points] Barr now wants to prove that π⃗ exists. Determine a condition on p for the stationary distribution π⃗ to exist, and give an explicit expression for π_i in such a case (your answer must not be in terms of π_0).
solution_text:
In order for π to be a probability distribution, we must have that Σ_{i=0}^n π_i = 1. Therefore,

π_n = 1 - Σ_{i=0}^{n-1} π_i

From part (c),
π_i = (p/(1-p))^i π_0

for 0 ≤ i ≤ n-1. We consider two cases. First, suppose p = 1/2. Then,
π_0 = π_1 = ... = π_{n-1}

Therefore,
π_n = 1 - Σ_{i=0}^{n-1} π_i = 1 - nπ_{n-1}

But we also have π_n = pπ_{n-1} = π_{n-1}/2. Therefore,
π_{n-1}/2 = 1 - nπ_{n-1}

which solves to
π_0 = π_1 = ... = π_{n-1} = 2/(2n+1); π_n = 1/(2n+1)

Next, suppose p ≠ 1/2. Then,
π_n = 1 - Σ_{i=0}^{n-1} (p/(1-p))^i π_0 = 1 - [1 - (p/(1-p))^n]/[1 - p/(1-p)] π_0

But we also have π_n = pπ_{n-1} = p(p/(1-p))^{n-1} π_0. Therefore,
p(p/(1-p))^{n-1} π_0 = 1 - [1 - (p/(1-p))^n]/[1 - p/(1-p)] π_0

which solves to
π_0 = [1 - p/(1-p)]/[p/(1-p) * (p/(1-p))^{n-1} + (1 - (p/(1-p))^n)/(1 - p/(1-p))] = 1/[2p/(1-p) * (p/(1-p))^n - 1]

giving
π_i = (p/(1-p))^i * [1 - p/(1-p)]/[2p/(1-p) * (p/(1-p))^n - 1]

for 0 ≤ i ≤ n-1, and
π_n = (2p-1)/[2p/(1-p) * (p/(1-p))^n - 1]

Note that the finite geometric sum used above works as long as p/(1-p) ≠ 1, i.e., p ≠ 1/2. Thus, there exists a stationary distribution for all values of p where 0 < p < 1 and it is given by the expressions above.

question_text:
Barr the Bear has opened up a lemonade stand! All of Barr's friends want to get a tasty drink, but Barr is worried that the line will get too long.

Barr's friends always travel in pairs, so each minute either two friends arrive to join the line with probability 0 < p < 1, or no one joins the line with probability 1-p. Each person gets only one lemonade. Barr can make one lemonade each minute, but she can't stockpile lemonade if no one is in line because she wants her lemonade to be fresh. Due to space constraints and people's lack of the patience, two friends arriving at a time when the line has a length of at least n, for some n ≥ 1, will both not join the line. Let's help Barr determine how long the line is likely to get!

Barr wants to represent this process with a graph G, where a random walk in G corresponds to the size of Barr's line in this process. In particular, Barr will have a graph G where the first node (leftmost) corresponds to the state where there are zero people in Barr's line, the second node corresponds to the state where there is one person in Barr's line, the next node corresponding to two people in Barr's line, and so on.

[10 points] Does G always converge to the stationary distribution you found in (d), assuming that it exists? Prove that it does or show that it does not.
solution_text: If a stationary distribution exists, every random walk on G will converge to it. Because there is a self-loop on node 0, the graph is aperiodic. G is also strongly-connected: for any two nodes i < j, we can get from i to j with j - i consecutive minutes of adding two new people, while we can get from j to i with j - i consecutive minutes of adding no new people.

question_text:
In an attempt to get full wifi coverage, BIT (Bitsville Institute of Technology) scattered routers across its campus. Unfortunately, BIT has just discovered that if there are multiple wireless signals in one place, it can degrade performance. BIT administrators decide to turn off some routers so that there are no overlapping networks but wish to maximize the number that stay on. (The remaining routers no longer need to cover the entire campus, but no two of the remaining routers can have overlapping coverage areas.)

There are n routers at positions (x₁,y₁),...,(xₙ,yₙ) in the plane, for real numbers xᵢ and yᵢ. Each router covers a circle of radius 1 centered at its position.

Assume we have routers at the following positions: (0,0), (1.2,0), (0,1.2), and (0.4,0.4). What is the maximum number of routers that the administrators can keep on while satisfying the problem's constraints?

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4
solution_text: Solution: A. For two routers to be kept on, their coverage areas must not overlap. Since a router's coverage area is a circle of radius 1 centered at its position, two routers can be kept on only if they are more than a distance of 2 apart. Each one of the router positions given are within a distance of 2 of one another. Therefore, only one of them can be kept on while satisfying the problem's constraints.

question_text:
In an attempt to get full wifi coverage, BIT (Bitsville Institute of Technology) scattered routers across its campus. Unfortunately, BIT has just discovered that if there are multiple wireless signals in one place, it can degrade performance. BIT administrators decide to turn off some routers so that there are no overlapping networks but wish to maximize the number that stay on. (The remaining routers no longer need to cover the entire campus, but no two of the remaining routers can have overlapping coverage areas.)

There are n routers at positions (x₁,y₁),...,(xₙ,yₙ) in the plane, for real numbers xᵢ and yᵢ. Each router covers a circle of radius 1 centered at its position.

The BIT library is a 4 × 4 square building. Show that there is a polynomial time algorithm to determine the maximum number of routers within the library that can remain on.
solution_text:
Solution: The areas of coverage for all routers in the library are contained within in a 6 × 6 square of area 36. Each circle covers an area of π · 1² = π. Therefore, there can exist at most 36/π < 12 routers with non-overlapping coverage areas.

Given this observation, there is a simple polynomial time brute-force algorithm: explicitly enumerate all sets of ≤ 11 routers, checking all pairs of routers within each set for overlapping coverage areas, and output the largest set with no overlaps. There are

(n choose 1) + ⋯ + (n choose 11) = O(n¹¹)

sets of routers of size at most 11, and there are at most (11 choose 2) = O(1) pairs of routers within in each set to check of overlapping coverage areas. Thus, our algorithm runs in O(n¹¹), i.e., polynomial time.

question_text:
In an attempt to get full wifi coverage, BIT (Bitsville Institute of Technology) scattered routers across its campus. BIT administrators decide to turn off some routers so that there are no overlapping networks but wish to maximize the number that stay on. Each router covers a circle of radius 1 centered at its position.

Seeing your solution for the library (a 4 × 4 square building with polynomial time algorithm), an administrator proposes: "Great! Now we're done. Just divide up the campus into a grid of 4 × 4 squares centered at the origin (0,0), run the algorithm on routers within each square, and simply combine the results from all the squares." Explain why this proposal may not produce a correct solution.
solution_text: Solution: The problem is that the coverage areas of routers in one 4 × 4 square may overlap with those of routers in another. The algorithm from part (b) that solves each 4 × 4 square individually is not aware of routers outside each square, and so may end up picking a pair of routers from different boxes whose coverage areas overlap.

question_text:
In an attempt to get full wifi coverage, BIT (Bitsville Institute of Technology) scattered routers across its campus. BIT administrators decide to turn off some routers so that there are no overlapping networks but wish to maximize the number that stay on. Each router covers a circle of radius 1 centered at its position.

Your algorithm from part (b) is too slow to run on the entire campus, but you are willing to accept an approximation. Show that the problem with the administrator's proposal from part (c) can be tackled using randomization, i.e., give a randomized polynomial time algorithm that identifies at least 1/4 as many routers as the optimal solution in expectation.

Hint: Use a random offset for your grid. That is, instead of centering the grid at (0,0) as in part (c), think about picking a random point (x,y) as the grid center. To do this, you may assume that you can sample a uniformly random real in a polynomially-bounded range in constant time.
solution_text:
Solution: The algorithm is as follows. First, impose a randomly offset grid of 4×4 squares of area 16, and partition the routers into their respective squares. Discard any routers whose coverage areas intersect the grid lines, then run the algorithm from part (b) on each square on the remaining routers. Finally, combine the results from all the squares.

We start by analyzing correctness. Let OPT be the number of routers in the optimal solution. Let X = (x,y) be a random point denoting the grid center, and let A(X) be the number of routers in the output of our algorithm when the grid center is X. We want to show that this algorithm identifies at least 1/4 as many routers as the optimal solution in expectation; in other words, we want to show that E[A(X)] ≥ OPT/4, where the expectation is taken over the random choice of the grid center X.

Let OPT(X) be the number of routers in the optimal solution whose coverage areas do not intersect any grid lines when the grid center is X. First, we want to show E[OPT(X)] = OPT/4. Consider the probability that the coverage area of any single router intersects a grid line. Within a 4×4 box, the coverage area of any router placed in the 2×2 center box of area 4 will not intersect a grid line. When we pick a random grid center, any given router lands up in a random position in some square. Therefore, for any given router, the probability of its coverage area not intersecting a grid line is 4/16 = 1/4. Then, by linearity of expectation, we have E[OPT(X)] = OPT/4.

Now, fix the grid center X. In each square, our algorithm picks the maximal number of routers r in the 2 × 2 center box of the square. Thus, the number of routers in the optimal solution in the 2 × 2 center box of the same square is at most r. Since our algorithm combines the maximal routers in the center box of each square, we have that for all X, A(X) ≥ OPT(X). Then E[A(X)] ≥ E[OPT(X)].

Putting this all together, we have E[A(X)] ≥ E[OPT(X)] = OPT/4, and our algorithm identifies at least OPT/4 routers in expectation. The polynomial runtime follows from the fact that our algorithm from part (b) runs in polynomial time.

question_text:
In an attempt to get full wifi coverage, BIT (Bitsville Institute of Technology) scattered routers across its campus. BIT administrators decide to turn off some routers so that there are no overlapping networks but wish to maximize the number that stay on. Each router covers a circle of radius 1 centered at its position.

Generalize your algorithm from part (d) so that for any 0 < ε < 1, there exists a randomized polynomial time algorithm that identifies at least (1−ε) times as many routers as the optimal solution in expectation.
solution_text:
Solution: The idea is to increase the spacing s of the grid squares. Let the grid be comprised of s × s squares of area s². Using the same analysis from the previous part, for any given router, the probability of its coverage area not intersecting a grid line is ((s-2)/s)². Note that for s sufficiently large,

((s-2)/s)² > 1-ε

Specifically,

s > 2/√(1-√(1-ε)) ≥ 2/√ε

suffices. Reworking our algorithm from part (b) for s × s squares, we will have to consider sets of routers of size at most (s+2)²/π = O(s²) = O(1/ε²), and hence the runtime of the algorithm from part (b) would be n^O(1/ε²), which is still polynomial for any fixed ε.

Using the algorithm from part (d) with this grid of larger s × s squares then identifies at least (1−ε)OPT routers in expectation following the exact calculations form part (d).

question_text:
We say that an array A[1,...,n] of distinct real numbers is unimodal if there exists an integer k such that 1 ≤ k ≤ n, A[1,...,k] is monotonically increasing, and A[k,...,n] is monotonically decreasing.

We say that A is ε-far from being unimodal, for 0 < ε < 1, if you have to remove more than εn elements from A in order for the remaining sequence to be unimodal.

Which among the following are values of ε such that
A = [1,9,7,5,8,10,14,13,20,19,21,3]
is ε-far from being unimodal?

A. 1/6
B. 1/4
C. 1/3
D. 1/2
solution_text: Solution: A and B. This problem is essentially asking for the minimum number of elements that one has to remove from A in order for the remaining sequence to be unimodal. First notice that we can remove 4 elements from A, namely 9,5,13,19, in order for the remaining sequence to be unimodal. Therefore, A can only be ε-far from being unimodal for ε < 4/12 = 1/3. Furthermore, by inspection, one can see that if we remove at most 3 of the 4 elements 9,5,13,19, the remaining sequence is not unimodal. Therefore, we need to remove at least 4 elements from A in order for the remaining sequence to be unimodal, i.e., A is ε-far from being unimodal for all ε < 4/12 = 1/3.

question_text:
We say that an array A[1,...,n] of distinct real numbers is unimodal if there exists an integer k such that 1 ≤ k ≤ n, A[1,...,k] is monotonically increasing, and A[k,...,n] is monotonically decreasing.

We say that A is ε-far from being unimodal, for 0 < ε < 1, if you have to remove more than εn elements from A in order for the remaining sequence to be unimodal.

Design a sublinear-time property tester that, given 0 < ε < 1 and an array A[1,...,n] of distinct real numbers outputs:
• YES with probability 1 if A is unimodal, and
• NO with probability at least 3/4 if A is ε-far from being unimodal.

Prove the correctness of your property tester and analyze its runtime. For full credit, your property tester should run in O((1 + 1/ε) log n) time.
solution_text:
Solution: Recall that by definition, A is unimodal if there exists an integer k such that 1 ≤ k ≤ n, A[1,...,k] is monotonically increasing, and A[k,...,n] is monotonically decreasing. We first use binary search to find a candidate k: each query will be of two consecutive indices, to see if we are to the left of k or to the right. Since A consists of distinct values, we will never get a tie. Then, once we have such a k, we check that A[k] > A[k + 1], and then run our sortedness algorithm from lecture with parameter ε on A[1,...,k] and A[k +1,...,n]. If both return YES, we return YES, otherwise we return NO.

To see that this is correct, suppose that A is unimodal. Then our search for k must return the correct k, A[k] > A[k + 1], and A[1,...,k] and A[k + 1,...,n] must both be sorted (increasing and decreasing respectively), so our subroutines both return YES and we return YES as well, as required.

On the other hand, if A is ε-far from being unimodal, meaning we need to remove more than εn elements from A to make it unimodal, then no matter what k we pick, either A[k] < A[k+1], or at least one of A[1,...,k] or A[k,...,n] can't be ε-close to sorted (increasing and decreasing respectively).

Suppose, for the sake of contradiction, that A[k] > A[k+1] and both A[1,...,k] and A[k + 1,...,n] are ε-close to sorted (increasing and decreasing respectively). This means we can remove at most εk elements from A[1,...,k] to obtain a fully (increasingly) sorted list, and ε(n−k) elements from A[k,...,n] to obtain a fully (decreasingly) sorted list. But then we could remove at most εk + ε(n − k) = εn elements from A[1,...,n] to make it unimodal, contradicting the fact that A is ε-far from being unimodal. It follows that either A[k] < A[k+1], or at least one of our subroutines must return NO with probability at least 3/4, and so we also return NO with probability at least 3/4.

Finally, we analyze the runtime of our property tester. The initial binary search to find a candidate k takes O(log n) time. Subsequently, we perform two runs of our sortedness algorithm from lecture. The runtimes of there are O(log k/ε) and O(log(n−k)/ε) respectively. Each of these are at most O(log n/ε). Therefore, the total runtime of our property tester is O((1+ 1/ε) log n).

question_text:
Bob is holding a party with his favorite computer science researchers! This one is themed around solving systems of linear equations. Unfortunately, all of the partygoers have been conducting deep learning research, and everyone forgot all of their algorithms except gradient descent. Knowing gradient descent is truly the mother of all algorithms, Bob is unafraid.

To get the party started, Bob would like to solve the system A⃗x = b⃗, where
A = [[1, 2], [-1, 2]], b⃗ = [1, 3], x⃗ = [x₁, x₂]

Bob comes up with the objective function
f_{A,b}(x⃗) = (1/2)x⃗ᵀ(AᵀA)x⃗ - b⃗ᵀAx⃗

Now, all Bob needs to do is to run gradient descent.

[Warm-up] Which of the following is true?
A. ∂f_{A,b}/∂x₁ = 8x₁ + 8 and ∂f_{A,b}/∂x₂ = 2x₂ - 2.
B. ∂f_{A,b}/∂x₁ = 8x₁ - 8 and ∂f_{A,b}/∂x₂ = 2x₂ + 2.
C. ∂f_{A,b}/∂x₁ = 2x₁ + 2 and ∂f_{A,b}/∂x₂ = 8x₂ - 8.
D. ∂f_{A,b}/∂x₁ = 2x₁ - 2 and ∂f_{A,b}/∂x₂ = 8x₂ + 8.
solution_text:
C. We can write out f_{A,b} = x₁² + 4x₂² + 2x₁ - 8x₂ and take the partials. We obtain
∂f_{A,b}/∂x₁ = 2x₁ + 2
∂f_{A,b}/∂x₂ = 8x₂ - 8

question_text:
Bob is holding a party with his favorite computer science researchers! This one is themed around solving systems of linear equations. Unfortunately, all of the partygoers have been conducting deep learning research, and everyone forgot all of their algorithms except gradient descent. Knowing gradient descent is truly the mother of all algorithms, Bob is unafraid.

To get the party started, Bob would like to solve the system A⃗x = b⃗, where
A = [[1, 2], [-1, 2]], b⃗ = [1, 3], x⃗ = [x₁, x₂]

Bob comes up with the objective function
f_{A,b}(x⃗) = (1/2)x⃗ᵀ(AᵀA)x⃗ - b⃗ᵀAx⃗

[10 points] Show that for any critical point x⃗ (a point is a critical point if the gradient at it is zero or undefined) of f_{A,b}, we have x₁ = -1, x₂ = 1 (which are the solutions to the system A⃗x = b⃗).
solution_text: Any critical point has gradient zero (since the gradient is always defined). Thus, the partials must be zero, and we have the result when solving for x₁, x₂ as 2x₁ + 2 = 0 ⟹ x₁ = -1 and 8x₂ - 8 = 0 ⟹ x₂ = 1.

question_text:
Bob is holding a party with his favorite computer science researchers! This one is themed around solving systems of linear equations. Unfortunately, all of the partygoers have been conducting deep learning research, and everyone forgot all of their algorithms except gradient descent. Knowing gradient descent is truly the mother of all algorithms, Bob is unafraid.

To get the party started, Bob would like to solve the system A⃗x = b⃗, where
A = [[1, 2], [-1, 2]], b⃗ = [1, 3], x⃗ = [x₁, x₂]

Bob comes up with the objective function
f_{A,b}(x⃗) = (1/2)x⃗ᵀ(AᵀA)x⃗ - b⃗ᵀAx⃗

[20 points] Argue that f_{A,b} is α-strongly convex, and find the largest such value of α. Also show that the function is β-smooth, and find the smallest such value of β.

Recall that a function f with Hessian H is α-strongly convex if x⃗ᵀHx⃗ ≥ α for all unit vectors x⃗, and that f is β-smooth if x⃗ᵀHx⃗ ≤ β, for all unit vectors x⃗. Also, x⃗ is a unit vector if √(x₁² + x₂²) = 1.
solution_text:
We have
∂²f_{A,b}/∂x₁² = 2, ∂²f_{A,b}/∂x₂² = 8, ∂²f_{A,b}/∂x₁∂x₂ = ∂²f_{A,b}/∂x₂∂x₁ = 0

Therefore, the Hessian is
H = [[2, 0], [0, 8]] = AᵀA

In general, the eigenvalues of a diagonal matrix are the entries along the diagonals, and by definitions of α-strongly convex and β-smooth, α = 2 is the smallest such eigenvalue, and β = 8 is the largest such eigenvalue.

Alternatively, we are trying to find the largest value of α such that x⃗ᵀHx⃗ = 2x₁² + 8x₂² ≥ α, and the smallest value of β such that x⃗ᵀHx⃗ = 2x₁² + 8x₂² ≤ β, for x₁, x₂ such that √(x₁² + x₂²) = 1. One can rewrite this as
2x₁² + 8x₂² = (2x₁² + 2x₂²) + 6x₂²
or
2x₁² + 8x₂² = (8x₁² + 8x₂²) - 6x₁²

Since x⃗ is a unit vector,
2x₁² + 8x₂² = 2 + 6x₂²
and
2x₁² + 8x₂² = 8 - 6x₁²

Since x₁² and x₂² are non-negative, we have that
2x₁² + 8x₂² ≥ 2
and
2x₁² + 8x₂² ≤ 8

One can choose x₁ = 1, x₂ = 0 in the first inequality and x₁ = 0, x₂ = 1 in the second inequality to make these inequalities tight, so these are the largest and smallest values of α and β respectively.

Bob is holding a party with his favorite computer science researchers! This one is themed around solving systems of linear equations. Unfortunately, all of the partygoers have been conducting deep learning research, and everyone forgot all of their algorithms except gradient descent. Knowing gradient descent is truly the mother of all algorithms, Bob is unafraid.

To get the party started, Bob would like to solve the system A⃗x = b⃗, where
A = [[1, 2], [-1, 2]], b⃗ = [1, 3], x⃗ = [x₁, x₂]

Bob comes up with the objective function
f_{A,b}(x⃗) = (1/2)x⃗ᵀ(AᵀA)x⃗ - b⃗ᵀAx⃗

[10 points] Bound how many iterations it will take for gradient descent on f_{A,b} to converge ε-close to the optimal solution x⃗*. You can express your answer in big O(·) notation in terms of x⃗⁽⁰⁾ and ε, where x⃗⁽⁰⁾ is your initial point for gradient descent.
solution_text:
Recall from lecture that if f is α-strongly convex and β-smooth, then gradient descent will converge ε-close to the optimal solution in
O((β/α) log((f(x⃗⁽⁰⁾) - f(x⃗*))/ε)) = O(log(f(x⃗⁽⁰⁾)/ε))
iterations. Here, we treat α = 2, β = 8, and x⃗* (and hence f(x⃗*)) as constants.

question_text:
Balbo Biggins has found three rings covered with mysterious inscriptions. Each ring has a continuous line of n characters each from an alphabet Σ, where |Σ| > 2. Notice that because these are rings, we can circular wrap around for the mysterious inscriptions.

[15 points] The first ring only uses two of the symbols of Σ. Balbo knows it contains a length-m word s₁ (where s₁ also uses the same two symbols of Σ), but wants to find where s₁ appears in the ring. Develop an algorithm using FFT to find all occurrences of s₁ in the ring in O((n+m)log(n+m)) time.
solution_text:
This is a string-matching problem, similar to the one discussed in lecture. The difference here is that one of the strings is a ring and therefore we have to deal with the issue of circular overlap. For instance, say m = 3, n = 8, and that 0 and 1 were the two symbols of Σ being used. The word s₁ = 101 appears in 11111110 when seen on a ring. We can handle this issue of circular overlap as follows. All we need to do is cut the ring in an arbitrary position and replicate the first m - 1 characters at the end to get a string of length n + m - 1. In our example, if we cut the ring after the 0 and replicate the first m - 1 = 2 characters, we get the string 1111111011 of length n + m - 1 = 10. We now have a straightforward string-matching problem.

For completeness, we present the complete solution formally. Without loss of generality, let us denote a, b ∈ Σ as the two symbols in the alphabet that appear on the ring and in s₁. We represent the word s₁ as a polynomial S₁(x) of degree m - 1. Let s₁,ᵢ represent the ith character of s₁. Define
S₁(x) = Σ(i=0 to m-1) S₁,ᵢ xⁱ
where
S₁,ᵢ = -1 if s₁,ᵢ = a
S₁,ᵢ = 1 if s₁,ᵢ = b

Similarly, the string obtained by cutting the first ring in an arbitrary position and replicating the first m-1 characters at the end can be encoded as a polynomial R₁(x) of degree n + m - 2 with coefficients of either -1 or 1. However, in order to facilitate convolution via polynomial multiplication in order to perform the string-matching, we must reverse the coefficients of R₁. Let R₁,rev(x) denote the polynomial of degree n + m - 2 with the same coefficients as R₁(x), but in reverse.

We now use FFT to multiply the two polynomials S₁(x) and R₁,rev(x). The coefficient of xⁱ in the product will correspond to a shift of i and represent the number of matching characters between the word s₁ and the substring of the ring of length m beginning at a shift of i. At any offset, if s₁ is present in the ring, every character will match and the coefficient at the matching offset will have a value of m. Thus, we can determine all occurrences of s₁ in the ring by looking at each coefficient of the product polynomial and checking which of them are m.

The polynomials can be constructed in linear time and the product polynomial can also be read in linear time. The most expensive step is computing the product polynomial which takes O((n+m)log(n+m)) time using FFT.

question_text:
Balbo Biggins has found three rings covered with mysterious inscriptions. Each ring has a continuous line of n characters each from an alphabet Σ, where |Σ| > 2. Notice that because these are rings, we can circular wrap around for the mysterious inscriptions.

[15 points] Balbo inspects the second ring. This ring uses all characters from Σ. Balbo wishes to find a second length-m word s₂ in the inscription of the second ring, but the ring has several unreadable characters. For simplicity, he assumes that the unreadable characters don't matter, i.e., an unreadable character on the ring matches with every character from Σ. Develop an O(|Σ|(n+m)log(n+m)) time algorithm to perform the matching.
solution_text:
We now need new ideas for two reasons: (1) we are now dealing with an alphabet Σ with more than two symbols, and (2) we need to deal with the unreadable characters that are essentially treated as wildcards, i.e., they match with every character from Σ. The fact that |Σ| appears in the target for the runtime gives us a hint.

For each letter σ ∈ Σ, we will find all occurrences where the ring and word s₂ match on all positions where σ appears in s₂. For this we use a technique similar to part (a), but with a slightly different encoding. For each σ ∈ Σ, we represent the word s₂ as a polynomial S₂,σ(x) of degree m - 1. Let s₂,ᵢ represent the ith character of s₂. Define
S₂,σ(x) = Σ(i=0 to m-1) S₂,σ,ᵢ xⁱ
where
S₂,σ,ᵢ = 1 if s₂,ᵢ = σ
S₂,σ,ᵢ = 0 if s₂,ᵢ ≠ σ

Similarly, for each σ ∈ Σ, the string obtained by cutting the second ring in an arbitrary position and replicating the first m - 1 characters at the end can be encoded as a polynomial R₂,σ(x) of degree n + m - 2 with coefficients of either 1 or 0, the key difference being that the character σ as well as an unreadable character are both mapped to 1, while other characters from Σ are mapped to 0. As in part (a), in order to facilitate convolution via polynomial multiplication in order to perform the string-matching, we must reverse the coefficients of R₂,σ. Let R₂,σ,rev(x) denote the polynomial of degree n + m - 2 with the same coefficients as R₂,σ(x), but in reverse.

We now use FFT to multiply the two polynomials S₂,σ(x) and R₂,σ,rev(x), for each σ ∈ Σ. We say that a shift i is good for the character σ ∈ Σ if the coefficient of xⁱ in the product of the two polynomials S₂,σ(x) and R₂,σ,rev(x) equals the number of times the character σ appears in s₂. Note that if a shift i is simultaneously good for every σ ∈ Σ, then s₂ occurs in the ring at that shift i, considering unreadable characters as matches. Thus, we can determine all occurrences of s₂ in the ring by looking at each coefficient of each of the |Σ| product polynomials.

Here are some other ways to check whether a shift i is good:
1. For each shift i, sum the ith coefficients of each of the |Σ| product polynomials and check that the sum equals m. Concretely, if cᵢ,σ is the coefficient of xⁱ in the product of S₂,σ(x) and R₂,σ,rev(x), then Σ(σ∈Σ) cᵢ,σ = m if and only if shift i is good for all σ ∈ Σ.
2. We can also map unreadable characters to 0 instead of 1. In this case, we check if the sum of the ith coefficients of each of the |Σ| product polynomials is equal to m - tᵢ, where tᵢ is the number of unreadable characters in the length-m window of the ring at shift i. If so, the shift i is good for all σ ∈ Σ. This will require computing the number of unreadable characters in every length-m window of the ring, which can be done via dynamic programming in O(n+m) time.

Again, the most expensive step is computing each of the product polynomials which takes O((n+m)log(n+m)) time for each σ ∈ Σ using FFT for a total of O(|Σ|(n+m)log(n+m)) time.

question_text:
Balbo Biggins has found three rings covered with mysterious inscriptions. Each ring has a continuous line of n characters each from an alphabet Σ, where |Σ| > 2. Notice that because these are rings, we can circular wrap around for the mysterious inscriptions.

[10 points] Balbo looks at the third ring and is relieved that all of the characters are readable! Unfortunately, the length-m word s₃ he wants to match to the ring has some unreadable characters. As before, an unreadable character in s₃ matches with every character from Σ. Modify your algorithm from part (b) to solve this matching problem under the same runtime constraints.
solution_text:
This problem looks suspiciously similar to part (b), the only difference being that the unreadable characters appear in the word rather than the ring. However, this difference does introduce a tiny hiccup. Recall that in our solution to part (b), in order to check if a shift was good for σ ∈ Σ, we compared a coefficient of a product polynomial to the number of times the character σ appeared in the word. This was only possible because the word had no unreadable characters and so the number of times the character σ appeared in the word was fixed. Now, however, the word s₃ has unreadable characters.

Notice that the ring on the other hand has only readable characters! So, we can use the number of times the character σ appears in the ring as a reference as opposed to the number of times it appears in the word s₃. However, we will have to obtain the number of times the character σ appears in every length-m window of the ring, for each σ ∈ Σ. This can be done via dynamic programming in O(n+m) time for each σ ∈ Σ. Thus, we can precompute all the counts we will need in O(|Σ|(n+m)) time.

Once we have computed these counts, the same algorithm from part (b) works, where we determine the good shifts by comparing the corresponding coefficient of the corresponding product polynomial to the number of times the corresponding character appeared in the corresponding window of the ring.

Alternatively, we follow the other ways to check whether a shift is good that were outlined in part (b):
1. If we map unreadable characters to 1, checking Σ(σ∈Σ) cᵢ,σ = m for every i works exactly as in part (b). This allows us to avoid the work of computing counts in every length-m window of the ring.
2. If we map unreadable characters to 0, since the unreadable characters appear in the word rather than the ring, we can avoid the previous work of computing the number of unreadable characters in every length-m window, and simply check that the sum of the ith coefficients of each of the |Σ| product polynomials is equal to m - t, where t is the number of unreadable characters in s₃.

Again, the most expensive step is computing each of the product polynomials which takes O((n+m)log(n+m)) time for each σ ∈ Σ using FFT for a total of O(|Σ|(n+m)log(n+m)) time.

question_text:
You were approached by Prof. Cooper, a brilliant MIT faculty member and particle physicist, to help him confirm what he views as his newest and greatest discovery: Cooper's Law. (Apparently, 6.1220 is now a useful class for particle physics too!) To this end you need to analyze a stream of particles represented as a collection S of n > 0 particles, in which each particle p ∈ S has a color c(p) ∈ {R,G,B}, and also a charge h(p) which can be any integer in the range of -100·n to 100·n (both limits being inclusive).

In this problem, your goal will be to check whether or not this stream contains a Cooper triple, i.e., three particles r, g, and b such that c(r) = R, c(g) = G, and c(b) = B, and their respective charges satisfy the relationship
h(r) + 2·h(g) + 4·h(b) = 8

[Warm-up] Suppose n = 6 and that the stream of n particles represented as the collection S = {p₁,...,p₆} has
• c(p₁) = R, h(p₁) = 100
• c(p₂) = G, h(p₂) = 200
• c(p₃) = B, h(p₃) = 300
• c(p₄) = R, h(p₄) = 400
• c(p₅) = G, h(p₅) = 500
• c(p₆) = B

How many distinct values could we assign to h(p₆) such that the stream contains a Cooper triple?
A. No such value exists
B. 2
C. 4
D. 8
solution_text: C. A Cooper triple consists of three particles r, g, and b such that c(r) = R, c(g) = G, and c(b) = B. Since our stream has two particles of each color, there are 2³ = 8 candidate Cooper triples. Four of them, namely (p₁,p₂,p₃), (p₁,p₅,p₃), (p₄,p₂,p₃), and (p₄,p₅,p₃), are not Cooper triples since they do not satisfy h(r) + 2·h(g) + 4·h(b) = 8.

and the univariate polynomials
P(x) = 1 + 2x + 3x² + ... + 11x¹⁰
Q(x) = 1 + 2x + 3x² + ... + 7x⁶

Which of the following statements are true?
A. The constant term in the product F(x)·G(x) is the same as the constant term in the product P(x)·Q(x).
B. The coefficient of x in the product F(x)·G(x) is the same as the coefficient of x in the product P(x)·Q(x).
C. The coefficient of x² in the product F(x)·G(x) is the same as the coefficient of x¹⁰ in the product P(x)·Q(x).
D. The coefficient of 1/x² in the product F(x)·G(x) is the same as the coefficient of x⁶ in the product P(x)·Q(x).
